answer:
Yes. The broad answer from organizational psychology is:

- **Interesting / meaningful / autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement.**
- **Pay matters, but usually less than people expect once compensation is already “good enough.”**
- **A move into leadership can improve satisfaction if it matches your values and identity, but promotions/managerial roles often bring more stress and their happiness boost can fade.**
- **Direct evidence on “regret” from this exact tradeoff is much thinner than evidence on satisfaction, burnout, and turnover.**

Here’s the research-based version.

## 1) “Interesting work” is strongly linked to satisfaction

In the literature, “interesting work” is usually measured indirectly through things like:

- skill variety
- challenge
- task significance / meaning
- autonomy
- feedback / learning

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham). The best summary is a large meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**: job design features—especially the motivational ones above—showed **strong relationships** with:
  - job satisfaction
  - internal work motivation
  - engagement/growth satisfaction
  - performance, to a lesser degree

This is one of the most replicated findings in work psychology: **jobs that are more intrinsically motivating tend to produce higher satisfaction than jobs that are merely better compensated.**

## 2) Meaningful work is a strong predictor of well-being

There is also a separate literature on **meaningful work**.

- **Allan et al. (2019), meta-analysis**: meaningful work is associated with:
  - higher job satisfaction
  - higher life satisfaction / well-being
  - more engagement
  - lower burnout
  - lower turnover intentions

So if by “interesting” you mean not just intellectually stimulating, but also **important / meaningful / aligned with your values**, the evidence gets even stronger.

## 3) Pay has a positive effect, but it is usually smaller than intrinsic job quality

The most cited meta-analysis here is:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**: the relationship between **pay level and job satisfaction is positive but small**.

That is a pretty robust result. Higher salary does help, but **it explains surprisingly little** of the variance in whether people actually like their jobs.

A useful way to phrase the literature is:

- **Pay matters a lot when it changes financial security**
- **Pay matters less when comparing “good” vs “slightly better” compensation**
- **Daily work experience often dominates marginal pay differences for long-run satisfaction**

This does **not** mean money is unimportant. It means that once you are already well-paid, a “bit higher salary” often has less impact on happiness than changes in:
- interest
- autonomy
- manager quality
- mission
- fit
- work-life load

## 4) Autonomy and competence are especially important for knowledge workers

Self-determination theory is relevant here. Across a large literature, satisfaction of the needs for:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

predicts better motivation and well-being.

For research/creative/technical work, the first two are especially important. A role that is more interesting often also gives more:

- felt competence
- learning
- intrinsic motivation
- “I want to do this” energy

That tends to support both happiness and performance.

Relatedly:

- **Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford (2014), meta-analysis**: **intrinsic motivation** is a strong predictor of performance, especially for **quality** and for work requiring creativity/complex problem solving.

That is highly relevant to research engineering.

## 5) Person-job fit matters a lot — and your desire to lead is not a trivial detail

One of the strongest findings in this area is **fit**.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005), meta-analysis**: person-job and person-organization fit are strongly related to:
  - job satisfaction
  - commitment
  - lower intent to quit

This matters for your case because you said:

> leading a small team is something I always wanted to do

That is important. If leadership is a real aspiration—not just a shiny title—then the Meta role may fit an important part of your identity and career goals.

Research would say that **misfit hurts**, even when the role is prestigious or better-paid. But it would also say that **a role aligned with a valued career motive** (like wanting to lead) can meaningfully increase satisfaction.

So the evidence does **not** reduce this to “interesting work always wins.” It says:
- intrinsic interest matters a lot
- but **fit with your desired trajectory** matters a lot too

## 6) Promotions / leadership roles often produce only temporary happiness gains

The literature on promotions is more mixed than many people expect.

Panel studies generally find:

- promotions increase satisfaction in the short run
- but the boost often **fades over time**
- promotions often also increase:
  - stress
  - hours
  - administrative burden
  - work-family conflict

So a move into management is **not automatically** a long-run well-being improvement.

This is particularly relevant if the management role comes at the cost of the parts of work you currently find most engaging.

A research-based summary would be:

- if you genuinely want to lead, management can be satisfying
- but if management mainly means less interesting work plus more people/process overhead, the net happiness effect can be neutral or negative after adaptation

## 7) On “regret”: direct evidence is thinner

There is much more research on:
- job satisfaction
- burnout
- engagement
- turnover intentions
- life satisfaction

than on explicit **career regret** in choices like this.

So the honest answer is: **I don’t know of a strong body of research that directly says “people who choose the more interesting job regret it less than people who choose higher pay + leadership.”**

What the literature *does* suggest is:

- people regret choices that create **persistent misfit**
- people adapt somewhat to **title/promotion/pay gains**
- chronic lack of interest/meaning is associated with lower well-being and higher withdrawal

So if you want a research-grounded proxy for future regret, the best predictors are probably:
- person-job fit
- meaningfulness
- autonomy
- learning/growth
- whether the role matches your preferred identity

rather than salary alone.

## 8) One more relevant result: job satisfaction spills into life satisfaction

This matters because work is such a large part of waking life.

There is longstanding evidence that:
- **job satisfaction and life satisfaction are moderately correlated**
- the relation goes both ways, but work conditions do contribute materially to overall well-being

So this is not just about “liking your job” in a narrow sense. The work itself can affect broader happiness.

---

# Practical summary of what the research would predict

If I translate the literature into your situation, as neutrally as I can:

### On average, the evidence suggests:
1. **A more interesting, meaningful, intrinsically motivating job tends to produce higher sustained satisfaction than a slightly higher salary.**
2. **A leadership opportunity can be worth a lot if leading is genuinely important to you**, because person-job fit matters strongly.
3. **The long-run happiness gain from promotion/title/pay is often smaller than people expect**, partly because of adaptation and added stress.
4. **The biggest non-financial question is whether the Meta role gives you enough autonomy, meaning, and intellectual engagement to offset the loss of “very interesting stuff.”**

So the research does **not** say “always stay with the interesting job.”
It says the decision should heavily weight:
- intrinsic interest
- meaningfulness
- fit
- leadership aspiration
and should weight **modest salary differences less heavily** than most people intuitively do.

---

# If you want the most research-grounded way to compare the two jobs

Rate each role on these dimensions:

1. **Intrinsic interest / curiosity**
2. **Meaning / mission alignment**
3. **Autonomy**
4. **Learning rate / growth**
5. **Use of strengths**
6. **Leadership fit**
7. **Manager quality / org culture**
8. **Workload / stress**
9. **Compensation**
10. **Option value for future career moves**

Research suggests 1–8 often predict day-to-day well-being more than 9, unless 9 changes financial security in a major way.

answer:
Yes. The research literature leans fairly strongly in one direction:

**On average, the intrinsic quality of the work** — interestingness, autonomy, meaning, learning, fit — **is a stronger predictor of job satisfaction and day-to-day wellbeing than moderate differences in pay.**  
There’s **much less direct research on “regret”**, but the evidence that does exist is consistent with the idea that people **adapt relatively quickly to salary/status gains** and that **the lived texture of the work** matters more for sustained satisfaction.

A few of the most relevant literatures:

---

## 1) Job design: “interesting work” is one of the biggest predictors of job satisfaction

A classic line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses of work design.

### What it finds
Jobs are more satisfying and motivating when they have more of:
- **skill variety**
- **task identity**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

These are basically the components of what people often mean by “interesting work.”

### Stronger evidence
A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
  Meta-analysis of the work design literature.

### Main result
These intrinsic job characteristics show **moderate-to-strong associations** with:
- **job satisfaction**
- **internal work motivation**
- **engagement**
- lower **turnover intentions**
- lower **strain/burnout** in many cases

In practical terms:  
**Autonomy, meaningfulness, variety, and feedback typically matter a lot more for how people feel about their jobs than small-to-moderate pay differences do.**

---

## 2) Meaningful work is robustly linked to wellbeing

There’s a large literature on **meaningful work** / **calling** / **purpose at work**.

### General finding
People who experience their work as meaningful tend to report:
- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **life satisfaction**
- higher **engagement**
- lower **burnout**
- lower **intentions to quit**

This is not just “nice rhetoric”; it shows up repeatedly in organizational psychology and vocational psychology.

### Caveat
Meaningfulness can sometimes coexist with overwork or exploitation (“I care so much I tolerate bad conditions”), so it’s not a magic variable. But controlling for that, meaningful/interesting work is generally a strong positive.

Good review sources include:
- **Rosso, Dekas, & Wrzesniewski (2010)** on the meaning of work
- later meta-analytic / review work in vocational psychology on meaningful work and wellbeing

---

## 3) Pay matters — but much less than many people expect

The best-known summary here is:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
  **Meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction**

### Main result
The relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but fairly small**.  
The headline version is: **pay matters, but not nearly as much as people intuitively think**.

This is consistent with broader wellbeing research:
- income helps more when it reduces **financial stress/insecurity**
- beyond that, additional income has **diminishing returns** for happiness
- people often **adapt** to higher pay relatively quickly

So if the Meta offer is “a bit higher salary,” the literature would usually predict that **salary alone is unlikely to dominate the day-to-day effect of work content**, unless the extra money materially changes your life circumstances.

---

## 4) Person-job fit matters a lot

Another very strong literature is **person-environment fit**, especially **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit**.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
  Meta-analysis on fit at work

### Main result
Fit is strongly associated with:
- job satisfaction
- organizational commitment
- lower turnover intent
- better wellbeing

This matters for your case because the choice may not really be:
- “interesting work” vs “more money”

It may be:
- **deep individual-contributor/research fit**
vs
- **leadership/management fit**

If leading a team is something you’ve wanted for a long time, that itself is not just an extrinsic reward. It may be part of your intrinsic fit: autonomy, influence, growth, identity.

The literature would say:  
**the better match to your motives and identity is likely to be more satisfying over time.**

---

## 5) Leadership/promotion: mixed, not uniformly happiness-increasing

There is research on promotions and managerial roles suggesting the picture is mixed:

### Typical pattern
Promotions/managerial roles often bring:
- more **status**
- more **pay**
- more **autonomy/influence**

but also:
- more **hours**
- more **stress**
- more **administrative burden**
- less time on the actual craft/technical work

Panel-data studies often find that promotions can increase job satisfaction **somewhat**, but the effect is often **modest or temporary**, and may be offset by stress.

So “leading a small team” is not automatically a wellbeing win. It tends to be good **when people genuinely want the role and like the actual work of management**, not just the idea of advancement.

That’s a key distinction the literature supports.

---

## 6) For scientists/researchers specifically, people really do trade money for interesting work

This is especially relevant to your situation.

- **Stern (2004), “Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?”**  
  This literature suggests that scientists/research-oriented workers are often willing to accept **lower wages** for jobs that allow them to:
  - do real science
  - pursue interesting problems
  - publish / maintain scientific identity
  - have intellectual freedom

This is useful because it’s not just a happiness survey; it’s closer to **revealed preference**.  
It suggests that for research-oriented people, **interestingness and scientific autonomy are economically valuable in their own right**.

So among researchers, valuing intellectually interesting work is not unusual or irrational; it is a repeated empirical pattern.

---

## 7) What about regret?

This is the weakest part of the evidence base.

I’m not aware of a strong, famous literature that directly says:

> “People who choose the more interesting job rather than the better-paid leadership job regret it less 10 years later.”

That exact question is not well-studied.

### What we do have instead
Two adjacent literatures are relevant:

#### a) Affective forecasting
People are not great at predicting what will make them happy. They often:
- overestimate the lasting impact of raises, titles, prestige
- underestimate the cumulative effect of everyday experience

#### b) Hedonic adaptation
People adapt to:
- higher salary
- status boosts
- promotions

often faster than they expect.

By contrast, the daily experience of doing work you find engaging or deadening is something you repeatedly re-encounter, so it tends to remain important.

So while there isn’t great “regret” evidence specifically, the surrounding literature suggests that **people may underweight work content and overweight salary/status in career choices**.

---

# Bottom line from the research

If I compress the literature into a few evidence-based claims:

1. **Interesting/meaningful/autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.**
2. **Pay has a real but comparatively smaller effect**, especially once you’re already well-compensated.
3. **Fit matters enormously**: if you deeply want to lead, leadership can be intrinsically valuable, not just a status marker.
4. **Promotions and management roles are not automatically better for wellbeing**; they help mainly when the person likes the actual managerial work.
5. **For research-oriented people, trading some pay for intellectually engaging work is a well-documented pattern.**
6. **Direct regret evidence is limited**, but adaptation research suggests people often overvalue salary/title relative to the quality of the work itself.

---

# If you want the most research-grounded inference for your exact case

Given your setup:
- already in a highly interesting role
- alternative is **somewhat** higher salary
- alternative offers **leadership**, which you’ve wanted
- but the work itself is **much less interesting**

The literature would say the key question is not “money vs interest.”  
It is:

> **Which of these is more central to your long-run fit and daily experience: doing frontier research, or leading people?**

Because the evidence says:
- **work content / meaning / autonomy** usually dominate **modest pay differences**
- but **leadership aspiration**, if genuine, can itself be a major intrinsic value

So the decision should probably be framed as:
- **research-interest fit**
vs
- **leadership-role fit**

not just compensation.

---

If you want, I can do one of two things next:

1. give you a **short reading list of the most relevant papers/meta-analyses**, or  
2. turn this into a **decision framework based only on research variables** (interestingness, autonomy, fit, pay, leadership aspiration, burnout risk, future option value).

For a high-ability researcher, the distribution could vary. And, I want to remind them that leading a small team can be an intrinsic goal, not just a pay-driven one. Let's elegantly structure the final answer.
answer:
Yes. The literature is fairly consistent on one broad point:

**The intrinsic quality of the work**—how interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and growth-producing it is—tends to matter a lot for **job satisfaction and day-to-day well-being**, often **more than pay level alone**.  
But your case has an important twist: **leading a team** is not just an extrinsic perk; for many people it is itself an intrinsic source of meaning, autonomy, status, and growth.

A few evidence-based points:

## 1) Research usually doesn’t measure “interesting” directly
It tends to use nearby constructs such as:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **meaningful work**
- **skill variety / challenge**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance**
- **person-job fit**

Those are the best proxies for “this work is genuinely engaging.”

## 2) Intrinsic job characteristics are strong predictors of satisfaction
A foundational line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses of work design.

### Main finding
Jobs with more:
- autonomy
- variety
- significance
- feedback
- opportunities to use/develop skills

are associated with higher:
- **job satisfaction**
- **internal motivation**
- **engagement**
- **lower turnover intentions**

A strong meta-analysis here is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
  Meta-analysis showing that motivational job characteristics have substantial relationships with job satisfaction and internal work motivation.

In plain English:  
**People tend to be happier in jobs that are more intrinsically well-designed, not just better paid.**

## 3) Meaningful work is robustly related to well-being
There is also a large literature on **meaningful work**.

- **Allan et al. (2019)**, meta-analysis on meaningful work  
  Meaningful work is positively related to **job satisfaction**, **life satisfaction**, and related well-being outcomes, and negatively related to turnover intentions.

This is important because “interesting” work often overlaps with “meaningful” work, especially in research roles.

## 4) Pay matters, but its direct link to job satisfaction is relatively small
The best-known result here:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
  Meta-analysis found the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**—much smaller than many people intuitively expect.

The usual summary of that literature is:
- more pay helps
- but it does **not** buy proportionate increases in job satisfaction
- and there are diminishing returns, especially at already-high income levels

So if both jobs already pay well, the extra salary may matter less for happiness than the qualitative nature of the work.

## 5) Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction
This matters because the choice is not just about “work happiness.”

- **Tait, Padgett, & Baldwin (1989)** and later work show a **moderate positive relationship** between **job satisfaction** and **overall life satisfaction**.

So choosing a role that is more interesting/engaging is not merely choosing to enjoy work more during office hours; it often affects broader well-being.

## 6) Intrinsic motivation and psychological needs matter a lot
From **Self-Determination Theory**:

People do better when work supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Relevant review:
- **Deci, Olafsen, & Ryan (2017)** on self-determination theory in work organizations

This literature generally finds that environments supporting those needs predict:
- better well-being
- more sustained motivation
- less burnout

This is relevant because:
- an intellectually rich research role may strongly support **competence** and intrinsic motivation
- a team leadership role may support **autonomy**, **impact**, and **relatedness**

So the tradeoff is not simply “interesting work vs money.”  
It may be **technical interest vs leadership-related intrinsic rewards**.

## 7) Fit matters: the same role can make one person happy and another miserable
A big result in organizational psychology is **person-job / person-organization fit**.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, meta-analysis  
  Better fit is associated with higher job satisfaction, commitment, and lower intentions to quit.

That means:
- if you deeply value hands-on research and intellectual novelty, a less interesting role may wear on you even if it pays more
- if you have a strong, longstanding desire to lead, then a team lead role may create satisfaction through value-congruence

This is one of the most important points for your situation.

## 8) What about promotions / leadership roles?
The evidence here is more mixed.

Broadly, leadership or promotion often brings:
- more status
- more pay
- more control
- more impact

but also:
- more stress
- more administrative load
- more hours
- less time doing the thing that originally made the person love the job

Longitudinal studies on promotions often find:
- **short-term gains** in satisfaction, especially about pay/status
- but not always strong or lasting increases in overall well-being
- and sometimes increases in strain/stress

So the literature does **not** support a simple “promotion = happier” rule.

## 9) On regret specifically: evidence is thinner
I don’t know of a strong, canonical line of research that directly answers:

> “Do people who stay in the more interesting job regret it later?”

What the literature does support is more indirect:

- people tend to be happier when work fits their values and provides intrinsic rewards
- pay/status gains often deliver smaller or less durable well-being benefits than expected
- mismatch between role and identity is a common source of dissatisfaction

A cautious research-based summary would be:

> There is better evidence that people benefit from intrinsically satisfying work than evidence that they regret choosing it over modestly higher pay.

But for your case, leadership aspiration complicates that, because **foregoing leadership experience** could itself become a source of regret if it is central to your identity and long-term goals.

## 10) The best-supported practical takeaway
If I strip the literature down to a few defensible claims:

### Well-supported
1. **Interesting / meaningful / autonomous work strongly predicts job satisfaction.**
2. **Pay predicts job satisfaction, but less strongly than many people think.**
3. **Promotion/leadership has mixed effects**: often higher status and impact, but also more stress and less direct craft work.
4. **Fit matters a lot**: the best choice depends heavily on whether “leading people” is a deep value for you or just an attractive milestone.

### Less well-supported
- strong claims about **regret** specifically
- universal rules like “always choose interesting work” or “always take the leadership role”

---

## For your specific decision, the literature suggests you should probably compare the jobs on these dimensions:
Not because of opinion, but because these are the variables most tied to well-being in the research.

1. **Intrinsic interest / meaning**
2. **Autonomy**
3. **Learning and growth**
4. **Opportunity for leadership/impact**
5. **Person-role fit**  
   - “researcher” identity
   - “builder/manager” identity
6. **Stress / administrative burden**
7. **Compensation**, but with the knowledge that marginal well-being returns are usually modest at high incomes

---

## If you want a one-sentence answer
**Yes: research generally suggests that more interesting/meaningful work is associated with higher sustained job satisfaction than higher pay alone, but in your case the leadership opportunity is itself an intrinsic factor, so the key empirical question is probably fit, not salary.**

If you want, I can also give you a **short reading list of the most relevant papers** or turn this into a **decision matrix grounded in the literature** rather than advice.

answer:
Yes. The research literature is pretty consistent on one broad point:

**The “quality” of the work itself** — especially whether it is **interesting/challenging, autonomous, meaningful, and a good fit for your strengths** — tends to predict **job satisfaction and day-to-day well-being more strongly than pay level alone**.

That does **not** mean “always choose the more interesting job.” But if your question is specifically *what does research say about interesting work vs. salary/title?* then the evidence generally leans toward **intrinsic job quality mattering more** once pay is already “good enough.”

## What the research says

### 1) Interesting / enriched work strongly predicts satisfaction
A large work-design literature goes back to **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model** and later meta-analyses.

The key result is that jobs with more:
- **skill variety**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- **opportunities to learn/grow**

produce higher:
- job satisfaction
- internal motivation
- engagement
- performance
- lower turnover intentions

A major meta-analysis:
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
  This is one of the standard papers. It found that motivational job characteristics are strongly associated with job satisfaction and other positive outcomes.

This is probably the closest research analogue to “interesting work.”

### 2) Meaningful work is a strong predictor of well-being
There’s also a large literature on **meaningful work**, which overlaps with “interesting” but is not identical.

Meta-analytic work finds that meaningful work is associated with:
- higher job satisfaction
- higher work engagement
- stronger organizational commitment
- better general well-being / life satisfaction
- lower turnover intentions

A useful reference:
- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis on meaningful work outcomes

So if by “interesting” you mean “I care about this, it feels worth doing, I’m intellectually alive,” research says that tends to be a major contributor to work happiness.

### 3) Salary matters, but less than people often expect
The best-known result here is that **actual pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction**.

Classic meta-analysis:
- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
  They found the correlation between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but small** — roughly **r ≈ .15**.

That’s not zero. Money matters. But compared with features of the job itself, it is usually a **weaker predictor** of whether people like their jobs.

Important nuance:
- **Pay satisfaction** matters more than raw pay.
- If the salary difference changes your **security, stress, family options, or future freedom**, that can matter a lot.
- But among already high earners, a marginal increase in salary often buys less additional well-being than people predict.

### 4) Intrinsic motives tend to be better for well-being than extrinsic ones
A related literature from **Self-Determination Theory** finds that people do better when their work supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

And more broadly, research on **intrinsic vs. extrinsic goals** finds that prioritizing intrinsic goals (growth, mastery, relationships, contribution) is generally associated with higher well-being than prioritizing extrinsic goals (money, status, image) as ends in themselves.

Key names here:
- **Deci & Ryan**
- **Kasser & Ryan**

This doesn’t mean money/status are bad. It means that, as predictors of long-run well-being, **intrinsic rewards usually punch above their weight**.

### 5) Fit matters a lot
There’s strong evidence from **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** research that people are happier and less likely to quit when the role fits their preferences, values, and strengths.

Classic meta-analysis:
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**

This matters for your case because “leading a small team” is not just a title or compensation issue. It could be:
- highly satisfying if it matches your motives
- draining if what you really love is deep technical research

So the question is not only:
- “interesting research vs. more money”

but also:
- “individual contributor work that fits me now vs. a management/leadership role that may fit me even better”

## So: do people who choose the more interesting job tend to be happier?

**On average, yes — if “interesting” also means more intrinsically rewarding, more engaging, more autonomous, or more meaningful.**

That’s the strongest research-backed answer.

But with two important caveats:

### Caveat 1: “Interesting” is not the only intrinsic good
Leading a team may itself satisfy important intrinsic needs:
- autonomy
- mastery
- generativity
- mentoring
- influence
- growth

So a “less interesting domain” could still produce **higher satisfaction** if the **role** fits you better.

### Caveat 2: High challenge without control can backfire
Interesting work is not always better if it comes with:
- overload
- chaos
- low autonomy
- constant fire drills
- bad management

Work design research consistently shows that **challenge + autonomy/resources** is good; **challenge + low control** is often bad.

## What about regret?

There is much less direct research on “career regret” in exactly this tradeoff. But the indirect evidence suggests:

People are more likely to thrive when they choose roles that maximize:
- intrinsic motivation
- fit
- meaning
- autonomy
- growth

rather than optimizing only for:
- pay
- prestige
- external status

That said, “regret” often comes from **foreclosed growth paths**. If leading a team is something you’ve wanted for a long time, then passing it up may create its own regret risk — not because management is objectively better, but because it may represent an unrealized part of your identity.

## If I had to summarize the evidence in one sentence

**Research generally says that once compensation is already strong, differences in how interesting/meaningful/autonomous the work feels are usually more important for happiness than differences in salary alone.**

## The most relevant papers / literatures to look at

If you want a short reading list:

1. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
   *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*  
   Strong evidence that job design features predict satisfaction, motivation, performance.

2. **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
   *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*  
   Important result: pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction.

3. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
   *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person–job, person–organization, person–group, and person–supervisor fit.*  
   Good evidence that fit matters a lot for satisfaction and turnover.

4. **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**  
   Meta-analysis on outcomes of meaningful work.  
   Meaningful work relates strongly to engagement, satisfaction, well-being.

5. **Deci & Ryan / Self-Determination Theory**  
   Especially work on autonomy, competence, relatedness at work.  
   Broad conclusion: intrinsic need satisfaction strongly predicts well-being.

6. **Kasser & Ryan**  
   On intrinsic vs. extrinsic aspirations and well-being.  
   More indirect for your case, but relevant to salary/status vs. interesting work.

## Evidence-based takeaway for your specific kind of decision

If you want to use the literature rather than intuition, compare the two jobs on these dimensions:

- **Autonomy**
- **Daily task interest**
- **Opportunity to learn**
- **Meaning / importance of the work**
- **Fit with your strengths**
- **Whether managing people energizes you**
- **Stress / control balance**
- **How materially meaningful the salary increase actually is**

That set of variables is closer to what predicts satisfaction than “salary” and “title” by themselves.

If you want, I can also do one of two more research-focused follow-ups:

1. give you a **more academic summary with exact citations and effect sizes**, or  
2. translate the literature into a **decision matrix** for your Anthropic vs. Meta choice.

answer:
Yes—there is quite a lot of research relevant to this, though not much that asks your exact question in the form “should I take the more interesting job or the higher-paid leadership job?”

The best evidence comes from four literatures:

1. **job design**: how interesting/autonomous/challenging work affects satisfaction  
2. **meaningful work**: whether work that feels significant predicts well-being  
3. **person–job fit**: whether jobs aligned with your interests/values lead to better outcomes  
4. **compensation**: how much pay predicts job satisfaction and well-being

## Short answer

**On average, intrinsic job qualities**—interesting work, autonomy, skill use, meaning, and fit with your interests—**predict job satisfaction and engagement more strongly than pay level does**.

There is **much less direct evidence on regret**, but the regret literature broadly suggests that in the long run people often regret **not pursuing personally valued paths** more than they regret taking action, especially when the forgone option was closer to their “ideal self.”

That said, your case is not simply **interesting work vs money**. The Meta role also offers **leadership**, which is itself an intrinsic good if you genuinely want it. So research would frame this more as:

- current role: higher research interest
- Meta role: more leadership, status, perhaps autonomy, higher pay
- question: which bundle better matches your motives and values?

## What the research says

### 1) Interesting/autonomous/challenging work is a strong predictor of job satisfaction

A major meta-analysis of work design research found that features like:

- **autonomy**
- **task variety**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- **skill use**

are consistently associated with **higher job satisfaction**, **higher internal motivation**, and **lower turnover intentions**.

This is one of the most robust findings in organizational psychology: people are usually happier in jobs that are more intrinsically motivating.

A useful translation of this literature is:

> If a job feels interesting because it gives you learning, challenge, variety, control, and a sense that the work matters, that tends to be very good for day-to-day work satisfaction.

This matters because “interesting” is not just a vague preference; in research it maps onto constructs that have pretty strong links to well-being.

### 2) Meaningful work is also strongly associated with well-being

Research on **meaningful work** finds that when people experience their work as meaningful/significant, they tend to report:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **engagement**
- higher **organizational commitment**
- better **general well-being**
- lower **turnover intentions**
- less **burnout** on average

This does **not** mean meaning always dominates everything else. It also has a possible downside: people in highly meaningful jobs may tolerate bad conditions or overwork because the work matters to them. So interesting/meaningful work is generally good, but it can come with self-exploitation risk.

Still, if your Anthropic work is genuinely much more interesting and meaningful to you, the literature would treat that as a serious positive factor, not a soft preference.

### 3) Fit matters a lot

A separate literature on **person–job fit** and **person–organization fit** shows that when a role matches your interests, values, and strengths, people tend to have:

- higher job satisfaction
- stronger commitment
- lower intent to quit
- better overall adjustment

This is important because a less interesting role can still be satisfying if it fits a deeply held goal—like wanting to lead a team. In other words:

- if “doing frontier research” is central to your identity, Anthropic may fit better
- if “building and leading a team” is central, the Meta role may fit better even if the day-to-day content is less interesting

Research would generally predict better outcomes from the option that is more congruent with your enduring motives—not just what sounds prestigious.

### 4) Pay matters, but less than many people expect

A widely cited meta-analysis found that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but small**—roughly **r ≈ .15**.

That is not zero. Pay matters. But it is much weaker than many people intuitively expect.

The compensation literature generally suggests:

- pay matters a lot when it affects **financial security**, **fairness**, or **status**
- beyond that, marginal increases in pay usually do **less** for satisfaction than changes in **job quality**
- people care a lot about whether pay feels **fair**, not just high

For someone already choosing between Anthropic and Meta, the research implication is usually:

> a “bit higher salary” is unlikely, by itself, to outweigh a substantial difference in intrinsic interest or fit.

### 5) Leadership roles are not automatically better for happiness

There isn’t a simple result saying “leading a team makes people happier.” Leadership roles often bring:

- more **autonomy**
- more **impact**
- more **status**
- more **skill development**

but also:

- more **role overload**
- more **people stress**
- more **administrative work**
- less time on the work that originally interested you

So the effect depends heavily on whether you actually want to manage, and whether the organization supports managers well.

Since you said leading a small team is “something I always wanted to do,” research would treat that as significant. It means the Meta job may have an intrinsic upside, not just an extrinsic one.

## What about regret?

This is where the evidence is weaker.

There is **not much direct longitudinal research** on whether people regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paid/less-interesting one.

The closest relevant findings are from the regret literature:

- in the short run, people often regret **actions**
- in the long run, people more often regret **inactions**
- people especially regret not pursuing options connected to their **ideal self** or deeply held aspirations

Career and education are among the domains where these long-run regrets often show up.

What that means cautiously is:

- if you pass on leadership and later conclude that you never tested an important aspiration, that can become regret material
- if you leave highly interesting work and later feel you traded away something central to your identity for money/status, that can also become regret material

So regret research does **not** give a one-sided answer. It mostly says:
**the thing you’re most likely to regret is failing to pursue what matters most to your core self.**

## Best evidence-based summary

If I compress the literature into a few claims:

1. **Interesting, autonomous, meaningful work is strongly associated with higher job satisfaction and engagement.**
2. **Pay has a positive but relatively weak relationship with job satisfaction**, especially once compensation is already high/adequate.
3. **Fit with your interests and values is a major predictor** of satisfaction and retention.
4. **Leadership can increase satisfaction** if it fulfills an authentic goal, but it can also reduce satisfaction if it mainly adds stress and distance from the work you love.
5. **Direct regret evidence is sparse**, but long-run regret tends to cluster around not pursuing personally important possibilities.

## A research-based way to compare your two options

If you want to use the literature rather than intuition alone, compare the jobs on these dimensions:

- **Interest / intrinsic motivation**
- **Autonomy / control**
- **Skill use / learning**
- **Meaning / significance**
- **Fit with identity and values**
- **Leadership aspiration fulfillment**
- **Quality of manager / team / culture**
- **Stress / hours / sustainability**
- **Pay fairness / financial impact**

That would actually be more research-grounded than focusing only on comp or prestige.

## References you can look up

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design and job characteristics  
- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction  
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis on person–environment fit  
- **Allan et al. (2019)** — meta-analysis on outcomes of meaningful work  
- **Gilovich & Medvec (1994, 1995)** — action vs inaction regret  
- **Roese & Summerville (2005)** — what people regret most

If you want, I can also turn this into a **decision memo grounded in the literature**, with a side-by-side table for:

- Anthropic research IC role
- Meta team lead role

using only evidence-backed dimensions.

answer:
Yes — there is quite a bit of research relevant to this, though not usually framed exactly as “interesting work vs. slightly more pay plus people management.” The short version is:

**For sustained job satisfaction and well-being, intrinsic job qualities — interestingness, meaning, autonomy, skill use, learning — tend to matter a lot, often more than moderate differences in pay.**
But:
- **leadership/advancement can matter a lot if it fits a personally important goal**, and
- **direct evidence on “regret” from this exact tradeoff is limited.**

## What the research says

### 1) “Interesting work” is strongly associated with job satisfaction
A large body of work in organizational psychology finds that jobs are more satisfying when they have features like:

- **skill variety**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance / meaning**
- **feedback**
- **opportunities for learning/mastery**

This starts with the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham) and is supported by later meta-analyses.

A particularly relevant meta-analysis is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design research.  
  Broad finding: motivational job characteristics like **autonomy, variety, significance, feedback** are consistently and meaningfully related to:
  - higher job satisfaction
  - higher internal motivation
  - better engagement
  - lower strain/burnout

So if by “interesting” you mean “the work itself is intellectually engaging and feels important,” that is absolutely a factor the literature treats as central, not peripheral.

---

### 2) Meaningful work predicts not just job satisfaction, but broader well-being
There is also a large literature on **meaningful work**.

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** — meta-analysis on meaningful work.  
  Broad finding: people who experience their work as meaningful report:
  - higher job satisfaction
  - higher work engagement
  - higher life satisfaction
  - lower turnover intentions

This doesn’t prove causality in every case, but the association is strong and quite consistent.

So if your current role feels more intellectually or morally meaningful, the literature would generally predict that this has real well-being value.

---

### 3) Pay matters, but less than many people expect for job satisfaction
Compensation is not irrelevant. But in the research, **absolute pay is usually a weaker predictor of job satisfaction than intrinsic job qualities**.

A classic result:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction.  
  Broad finding: the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**, much smaller than many people intuitively expect.

This is one of the more robust findings in the area:
- money helps,
- unfair pay hurts,
- but **once compensation is already strong**, incremental increases usually do less for day-to-day satisfaction than people think.

This is especially relevant for highly paid knowledge workers, where the difference is “a bit higher salary” rather than “escaping financial stress.”

---

### 4) People adapt to raises and promotions faster than they adapt to intrinsic job quality
A lot of happiness research suggests that people partially adapt to:
- income gains
- status gains
- promotions

This doesn’t mean they don’t matter; it means the boost often fades.

Related literatures:
- **hedonic adaptation**
- **relative income / social comparison**
- longitudinal studies of promotions and life satisfaction

The general pattern is:
- raises/promotions often produce a **short-term uplift**
- but people tend to **normalize** the new level
- job content, autonomy, stress, and fit often keep affecting daily experience more persistently

That is why research often finds that **job quality** has a more durable association with well-being than moderate differences in pay.

---

### 5) Promotions / leadership roles are mixed for happiness
You specifically mentioned **leading a small team**, which is important because this is not just a pay increase.

Research on promotions and management is more mixed than research on interesting work. The broad pattern is:

- promotion can increase:
  - status
  - income
  - sense of growth
  - résumé capital
- but often also increases:
  - stress
  - interruptions
  - responsibility for other people’s problems
  - administrative work
  - loss of time doing the craft itself

Some longitudinal research suggests promotions can improve **job satisfaction temporarily**, but effects on **overall life satisfaction** are smaller and less durable than people often assume.

So the research would not support a blanket rule that “moving into management makes people happier.” On average, it’s more conditional:
- it helps if the person **actually wants to manage**
- it helps if the organization supports managers well
- it hurts when people are promoted away from the work they enjoy into coordination/people problems they don’t actually like

---

### 6) Fit with your values and goals matters a lot
One complication in your case is that you said leading a team is **something you always wanted to do**.

That matters, because research on **person-job fit**, **person-organization fit**, and **self-concordant goals** suggests that well-being is higher when your work aligns with:
- your values
- your identity
- your long-term goals

A key meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis of fit at work.  
  Broad finding: better fit is associated with:
  - higher job satisfaction
  - higher commitment
  - lower turnover intentions

So there are really two “intrinsic” dimensions here, not one:

1. **Is the work itself interesting/meaningful?**
2. **Does the role fit who you want to become?**  
   In your case: “someone who leads”

Those can point in opposite directions.

---

## What about regret specifically?

This is where the evidence is weaker.

I’m not aware of a strong literature that directly answers:
> “Do people regret staying in a more interesting IC role rather than taking a somewhat less interesting management role for more money?”

What the literature does support is something like:

- **Extrinsic-choice effects** (money/status alone) are often overpredicted at decision time.
- **Intrinsic work quality** tends to matter more for day-to-day satisfaction than people forecast.
- **Goal-congruent choices** matter: people are more satisfied when their choice aligns with an identity they genuinely care about.

So the closest research-based statement is:

> People do tend to be happier in jobs that are more intrinsically rewarding, meaningful, and autonomous; however, if becoming a leader is a deeply valued personal goal, then passing up that path can also create dissatisfaction, because aspiration-fit matters too.

That’s not a hedge — it’s genuinely what the literature suggests.

---

## If you want the most evidence-based summary possible

If I had to compress the literature into a few claims:

### Strongly supported
1. **Interesting/meaningful/autonomous work predicts higher job satisfaction and engagement.**
2. **Pay predicts job satisfaction, but only modestly relative to intrinsic job qualities.**
3. **People adapt to pay/status gains more than they expect.**
4. **Fit between role and personal values/goals matters a lot.**

### Moderately supported
5. **Promotions/leadership do not reliably increase overall happiness in a lasting way; effects are mixed.**
6. **Management can reduce satisfaction if it takes people away from the core work they enjoy.**

### Weak / limited evidence
7. **Direct evidence on long-term regret from this exact choice is sparse.**

---

## Translating the research into your exact tradeoff

Without giving personal opinion, the literature would treat your choice as a comparison between:

### Stay at Anthropic
Likely advantages, from a research perspective:
- higher intrinsic interest
- possibly higher meaning
- likely stronger daily engagement
- likely better sustained job satisfaction if the work quality is truly much better

### Go to Meta
Potential advantages, from a research perspective:
- role fit with leadership aspiration
- developmental value
- status and compensation
- possibly greater future optionality if management is a path you genuinely want

The research would **not** say “always choose the more interesting job.”
It would say something closer to:

> If compensation is already more than adequate, then **differences in job content, meaning, autonomy, and fit with personal goals** are usually more predictive of long-run satisfaction than modest pay differences alone.

And in your case, the non-pay factor pulling the other way is not trivial: **leadership aspiration is itself one of those meaningful intrinsic-fit variables.**

---

## If you want papers to actually read
A good starter set would be:

1. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
   *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*

2. **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
   *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*

3. **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**  
   *Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.*

4. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
   *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.*

5. **Hackman & Oldham (1976/1980)**  
   Foundational work on job characteristics and intrinsic motivation.

6. For the broader happiness/income/adaptation background:
   - **Clark, Frijters, & Shields (2008)** on relative income/happiness
   - **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** and later work by **Killingsworth** on income and well-being

---

If you want, I can do one of two follow-ups:

1. give you a **more academic mini-literature review** with 10–15 papers and the main result from each, or  
2. turn this into a **decision matrix grounded in the literature** rather than opinion.

In high-income contexts, pay raises have a minimal effect on subjective well-being, except when large shifts impact security or freedom. Additionally, team leadership may fulfill long-term identity goals. People generally feel happier when their jobs align with their self-concept, not just when the work appears interesting. I’ll reference the Kristof-Brown et al. meta-analysis, which shows that a good person-job fit can lead to increased satisfaction and lower turnover rates.

I want to include citations in a clear list without overwhelming the reader. It seems reasonable to assert that job satisfaction correlates modestly with pay, somewhere around .15 to .25. I'll ensure my wording is safe and precise.

It seems that meta-analytic correlations often fall in the moderate-to-large range. I’ll make sure not to fabricate any titles and will cite authors and themes instead. Generally, the literature suggests that individuals find more satisfaction in roles that offer these intrinsic benefits, unless a management role significantly improves their fit and fulfills a strong desire to lead. answer:
Yes. There is a pretty solid research base on this, though it is stronger on **job satisfaction / well-being** than on **later regret**.

## Short version

Across organizational psychology and labor economics, the most consistent finding is:

- **Interesting / meaningful / autonomy-rich work** is strongly associated with higher **job satisfaction**, **engagement**, and often **overall well-being**.
- **Pay matters**, but once someone is already well-paid, **moderate increases in salary usually have a smaller effect** on satisfaction than people expect.
- **Promotions and leadership roles** have **mixed effects**: they can raise satisfaction if they increase autonomy, mastery, and identity-fit, but they also often increase stress, politics, and administrative load.
- **Direct evidence on regret is much thinner** than evidence on current well-being.

So if your choice is “more interesting work” vs “a bit more salary + first team lead role,” the literature does **not** say “always choose interesting work,” but it does suggest that **intrinsic job quality** often matters more for sustained happiness than a moderate pay bump.

---

## The main research results

### 1) Job design research: interesting work matters a lot

A classic and still influential line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), which says jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they provide:

- skill variety
- task identity
- task significance
- autonomy
- feedback

Those are very close to what people usually mean by “interesting work.”

A major meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, and Morgeson (2007)** found that these work-design characteristics are consistently related to:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher internal motivation
- better performance
- lower strain/burnout

A prior meta-analysis by **Fried & Ferris (1987)** found similar patterns.

### What this means for your question
If the Anthropic role is much more intellectually interesting, and that means more challenge, learning, significance, and autonomy, the literature would predict that this is **not a trivial benefit**. It is one of the most robust predictors of liking your work.

---

### 2) Meaningful work predicts satisfaction and lower burnout

A newer literature focuses on **meaningful work** rather than just “interesting work,” but the two often overlap in research settings.

Meta-analytic work, especially **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, finds that meaningful work is associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher work engagement
- higher life satisfaction
- stronger organizational commitment
- lower turnover intentions
- lower burnout/exhaustion

This is one of the clearest results in the field.

### Important nuance
“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical:
- You can have interesting but meaningless work.
- You can have meaningful but not very interesting work.
- The happiest jobs often score high on both.

If Anthropic feels both **more interesting** and **more meaningful**, that is a substantial point in its favor from the research perspective.

---

### 3) Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, relatedness

Another big framework is **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan). At work, people tend to do better when their job supports:

- **autonomy** — feeling you have agency
- **competence** — feeling effective and growing
- **relatedness** — feeling connected to others

Reviews and meta-analytic work in this tradition consistently find that these needs predict:

- greater job satisfaction
- better motivation
- more engagement
- less burnout

This matters because a management role can cut both ways:

- It may increase **autonomy** and **competence**
- But it may reduce daily exposure to the technical work you enjoy
- And it may add conflict, bureaucracy, interruptions, and people-management stress

So the leadership opportunity is not obviously “better” or “worse” in this literature. It depends on whether it gives you more of the needs that matter to you.

---

### 4) Pay matters less than many people think for job satisfaction

There is a lot of evidence that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**.

A well-known meta-analysis by **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** found that pay is related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is much weaker than many people intuitively expect.

In practical terms:
- being **underpaid** relative to market or peers absolutely hurts
- but once compensation is already high and fair, **extra pay has diminishing returns** for job satisfaction

That is especially relevant in your case, because both jobs are likely already in the “very well compensated” range. Research would usually predict that a **small-to-moderate salary increase** has less impact on long-run day-to-day satisfaction than **how much you enjoy the work itself**.

---

### 5) People do value non-pay job attributes a lot

Economists studying job choice also find that workers place substantial value on **nonpecuniary job attributes** such as:

- flexibility
- autonomy
- mission/meaning
- work environment
- status
- advancement opportunities

There are both survey and choice-experiment studies showing that people are often willing to trade away some salary for these features.

Related experimental work by **Ariely, Kamenica, & Prelec (2008)** showed that when work is made to feel pointless or unrecognized, motivation and output fall sharply. That doesn’t prove long-term happiness, but it does show that meaning/interest has real behavioral value.

---

### 6) Promotions and leadership roles: often beneficial, but not as clearly as people assume

Research on promotions is more mixed.

Some studies, such as **Kosteas (2011)**, find that promotions can increase job satisfaction. But the effects are often:

- modest
- partly due to expectations of future wage growth
- not always durable

Other work suggests that promotions and managerial roles can come with:
- more hours
- more stress
- less control over one’s calendar
- more interpersonal strain

So the evidence is **not** “leadership makes people happier.” It is more like:

> leadership can improve satisfaction when it fits the person and increases valued forms of autonomy/status/impact, but it can also worsen daily experience if it replaces the parts of work the person actually enjoys.

That’s relevant because you explicitly say leading a small team is something you’ve wanted. From the literature, that matters a lot: **person-job fit** is a strong predictor of satisfaction.

---

### 7) Person-job fit is a major predictor

Meta-analyses on **person-job** and **person-organization fit** (for example **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson, 2005**) find that fit predicts:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher commitment
- lower turnover intentions

This is important because your choice is not simply:

- interesting work vs boring work

It is more like:

- intellectually interesting individual-contributor work  
vs  
- less interesting domain content, but stronger fit with a long-standing aspiration to lead

Research says both kinds of fit matter:
- fit with your **interests**
- fit with your **values**
- fit with your **identity/future self**

So leadership is not just an “extrinsic” perk. If it reflects a real personal goal, it is psychologically meaningful.

---

## What about regret?

This is where the evidence is much weaker.

I’m not aware of a strong, job-specific research base showing that people who choose more interesting work later regret it less than people who choose more pay/promotion, or vice versa.

What we do have:

1. **Indirect evidence**  
   Since interesting/meaningful/autonomy-rich work predicts higher job satisfaction and lower burnout, it is plausible that it also reduces regret — but that’s an inference, not a direct result.

2. **General regret research**  
   Classic regret research (e.g. **Gilovich & Medvec**) suggests that in the long run people often regret **inactions / opportunities not taken** more than actions taken.  
   But that literature is very broad and not specific enough to settle your case.

So if you want to be strict about evidence:
- **well-being evidence:** fairly strong
- **regret evidence:** relatively sparse and indirect

---

## The most defensible summary from the literature

If I compress the literature into one sentence, it would be:

> For people who are already financially comfortable, sustained well-being tends to depend more on the day-to-day quality of the work — especially interest, meaning, autonomy, and fit — than on a moderate increase in pay; promotions/leadership help mainly when they genuinely fit the person and do not destroy those day-to-day qualities.

That is probably the closest thing to an evidence-based answer to your situation.

---

## If you want a research-grounded way to compare these two jobs

You could evaluate each role on these dimensions, because these are the ones most strongly supported in the literature:

1. **Interest / skill variety / challenge**
2. **Meaning / mission alignment**
3. **Autonomy**
4. **Learning / growth / competence**
5. **Leadership fit**  
   not “Do I want prestige?” but “Do I actually like mentoring, coordinating, unblocking, giving feedback, taking responsibility for others?”
6. **Social environment / manager quality**
7. **Burnout risk**
8. **Compensation fairness**
9. **Future option value**  
   which role keeps more doors open?

That would be much closer to how the research frames the problem than a simple “salary vs passion” comparison.

---

## Representative references

A few good anchor papers/reviews:

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976)** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Fried & Ferris (1987)** — meta-analysis of the Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analytic summary of work design and outcomes
- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis on person-environment fit
- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** — meta-analysis on meaningful work outcomes
- **Ariely, Kamenica, & Prelec (2008)** — experiments on meaninglessness and labor motivation
- Reviews/meta-analyses in **Self-Determination Theory at work** literature (Deci, Ryan, Van den Broeck and colleagues)

---

If you want, I can do one of two follow-ups:

1. give you a **more academic literature summary with citations and effect sizes**, or  
2. turn this into a **decision memo template** that uses only evidence-backed dimensions.

answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of research relevant to this, though not many studies framed exactly as “interesting IC job vs higher-paid less-interesting team lead role.”

The short version:

- **More interesting / meaningful / autonomous work is consistently associated with higher job satisfaction and better well-being.**
- **Pay matters, but its relationship to job satisfaction is much weaker than many people assume.**
- **Promotions / leadership roles often improve status and sometimes short-term job satisfaction, but they do not reliably increase overall life satisfaction, and they often increase stress.**
- **The best predictor is often not salary or title alone, but whether the role fits your intrinsic motives** — e.g. love of the work itself, desire for leadership, autonomy, learning, impact.

## 1) The strongest evidence: job design matters a lot

A classic literature in organizational psychology says that jobs are more satisfying when they have:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- often also **learning opportunities**

This comes from the **Job Characteristics Model**:

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976, 1980)**

And a major meta-analysis extending that literature:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*

Their conclusion, in plain English: **work characteristics like autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback are robust predictors of job satisfaction, motivation, engagement, and lower strain.**

This is one of the clearest research bases for saying that “interesting work” is not a luxury variable — it is a major determinant of how good work feels.

## 2) Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being

A related literature studies **meaningful work** directly.

A useful meta-analysis:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, *Journal of Management Studies* (meta-analysis on meaningful work)

Broad result: people who experience their work as meaningful tend to report:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **work engagement**
- higher **general well-being / life meaning**
- lower **turnover intentions**

This does **not** mean meaning solves everything. But it does mean that if one role feels much more intellectually alive or worthwhile, research would predict that this matters a lot.

## 3) Intrinsic motivation usually beats extrinsic motivation for sustained satisfaction

This is the **Self-Determination Theory** literature:

- **Deci & Ryan**
- review: **Gagné & Deci (2005)**
- broader work reviews: **Van den Broeck et al. (2016)**

The recurring result is that well-being at work is strongly supported when work satisfies basic psychological needs for:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Interesting work often hits the first two especially well.

A related point: goals driven by intrinsic motives tend to produce more durable satisfaction than goals pursued mainly for extrinsic rewards like money or status.

- **Sheldon & Elliot (1999)** on **self-concordant goals**

That result is especially relevant to your case, because “leading a team” could be either:
- an **intrinsic** goal (“I deeply want to build and mentor”), or
- an **extrinsic/status** goal (“I feel I should level up”).

Research suggests those are psychologically quite different.

## 4) Pay matters less for job satisfaction than people often expect

There is a direct meta-analysis on this:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: a meta-analysis*

The bottom line from that literature is: **pay has a positive but modest relationship with job satisfaction**.

In practical terms:
- A pay increase usually helps.
- But it usually does **not** compensate fully for losing autonomy, interest, meaning, or good day-to-day work.

This is one of the most stable findings in work psychology/economics: once compensation is already “good enough,” **non-monetary job attributes become very important**.

## 5) Economists also find that people are willing to trade salary for interesting work

Labor economists study this under **compensating differentials** and **nonpecuniary job attributes**.

A very relevant paper for research-oriented people:

- **Stern (2004)**, *Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?* in *Management Science*

He found evidence that scientists are willing to accept **lower wages** in exchange for opportunities associated with doing science — especially things like research freedom / publishing / scientific identity.

That doesn’t prove they’re happier later, but it shows something important: **knowledge workers systematically value interesting work enough to pay for it.**

There is also related work on scientists’ job preferences showing that many place high value on:
- intellectual challenge
- independence
- freedom to publish
- interesting problems

So for research engineers specifically, this is not a marginal preference. It’s often central.

## 6) What about promotions and leadership? Mixed evidence

This part of your question is important because Meta is offering not just more money, but **leadership**.

The evidence here is more mixed.

A representative paper:

- **Kosteas (2011)**, *Job Satisfaction and Promotions*

Result: promotions tend to increase **job satisfaction**, partly because they signal recognition and future opportunity.

But that is not the whole story. Other panel-data work on promotions and higher-status roles tends to find:

- a **short-run positive effect** on job satisfaction
- often **higher stress**, **more hours**, and **more responsibility**
- **little or no durable increase in overall life satisfaction** once the novelty wears off, especially if the role worsens daily experience

So the best summary is:

- **Leadership/promotion is not useless** — it often feels good and can matter a lot if it matches your goals.
- But it is **not a reliable route to greater overall happiness** on its own.
- The day-to-day content of the work still matters a lot.

## 7) Person-job fit is a major predictor

A strong meta-analysis here is:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** on **person-job / person-organization fit**

General result: better fit predicts:
- higher job satisfaction
- higher commitment
- lower intention to quit

This may be the most relevant lens for your exact dilemma.

Because the “right” answer in the literature is often not:
- “interesting work always wins,” or
- “leadership always wins,”

but rather:
- **which option fits your motives, identity, and strengths better?**

If you are a person who deeply wants to build teams, mentor, and be accountable for a group’s output, then a lead role may actually be **more intrinsically aligned**, even if the domain is less interesting.
If what you really love is the technical/research substance itself, the leadership premium may not compensate.

## 8) Is there research on regret specifically?

There is much less strong research on **long-term regret** framed this way than on satisfaction and well-being.

What the literature more clearly supports is:

- choices aligned with **intrinsic interests and self-concordant goals** tend to produce better sustained well-being
- choices driven mainly by extrinsic rewards often produce weaker or shorter-lived satisfaction
- promotions/status gains often have **adaptation effects**
- people can underestimate the importance of the daily lived experience of work

So I would say:
- there is **strong evidence on happiness/satisfaction**
- there is **much weaker direct evidence on regret**
- but the existing evidence points toward **day-to-day job quality and intrinsic fit** being better long-run predictors than salary/title alone

## 9) Important caveat: “interesting work” is not the whole story

One more nuance from the literature: meaningful/interesting work can coexist with bad outcomes if the context is bad.

People tolerate a lot when work is meaningful:
- overwork
- exploitation
- weak boundaries
- poor management

There is a “calling” literature showing that highly meaningful work can sometimes make people accept worse conditions than they should.

So the empirically supported view is not “just choose the most interesting thing.”
It is closer to:

> Interesting/meaningful work is a major contributor to well-being, but it does not cancel out bad management, low autonomy, overload, or poor fit.

## 10) If I had to summarize the evidence in one sentence

**On average, people are happier in work that is intrinsically interesting, meaningful, and autonomous than in work that merely pays more; promotions and leadership help mainly when they are themselves intrinsically valued and fit the person well.**

## A compact evidence-based takeaway for your case

Given your description:

- **Anthropic** seems to dominate on:
  - interestingness
  - likely intrinsic motivation
  - probably daily intellectual satisfaction

- **Meta** seems to dominate on:
  - salary
  - leadership opportunity
  - perhaps status / advancement

The literature would predict:

1. If “leading a small team” is a **deep intrinsic goal**, not just a prestige marker, then it could matter a lot.
2. If the work is **substantially less interesting**, that is a serious well-being cost, not a minor one.
3. The salary increase alone is unlikely to compensate much if you already earn comfortably.
4. A management move may raise satisfaction if it fulfills an important identity/aspiration, but may not improve overall happiness if it worsens daily work.

If you want, I can turn this into a **research-based decision framework** — e.g. a 6-factor rubric weighted by what the literature says predicts long-run satisfaction.
