answer:
Yes — there is a fairly robust research literature on this, though it usually doesn’t use the phrase “interesting work” directly. It breaks it into things like:

- intrinsic motivation
- task variety / intellectual challenge
- autonomy
- meaningfulness
- learning/growth
- person–job fit

And the broad result is:

> These intrinsic features of work are usually much stronger predictors of job satisfaction and day-to-day well-being than pay level, once pay is already “good enough.”

A few research findings that are especially relevant:

## 1) Interesting/autonomous/meaningful work is a strong predictor of job satisfaction

A classic line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses such as **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**.

They find that jobs with more:

- skill variety
- task significance
- autonomy
- feedback

are associated with higher:

- internal work motivation
- job satisfaction
- engagement
- lower burnout / withdrawal

In plain language: people tend to be happier in jobs that are mentally engaging, consequential, and self-directed.

This effect is not tiny. In organizational psychology, these work-design variables are among the more reliable predictors of job satisfaction.

---

## 2) Pay matters, but its relationship to job satisfaction is modest

A well-known meta-analysis is **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** on pay and job satisfaction.

The headline result is roughly:

> **Pay level has a positive but fairly small association with job satisfaction.**

The correlation is usually described as **modest** — much smaller than many people intuitively expect.

Two important nuances from that literature:

- **Pay fairness** often matters more than absolute pay.
- **Relative pay / social comparison** matters a lot. A raise feels good, but people adapt, and comparisons shift.

So the evidence does **not** say salary is irrelevant. It says that after a reasonable threshold, more pay tends to buy less happiness than better work itself.

---

## 3) Meaningful work has strong links to satisfaction and well-being

There is also a large literature on **meaningful work**. Reviews and meta-analyses (for example **Allan et al.** and other reviews in vocational and organizational psychology) generally find that meaningful work is associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher life satisfaction
- greater engagement
- lower distress / burnout
- lower turnover intention

This is one of the more consistent findings in modern work psychology.

For knowledge workers especially, “interesting” often overlaps heavily with “meaningful,” “challenging,” and “aligned with identity.” Those tend to matter a lot.

---

## 4) Self-determination research: autonomy, competence, and relatedness matter a lot

**Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan; applied to work in many studies and meta-analyses) finds that well-being at work depends strongly on whether the job supports:

- **autonomy** — feeling you have choice/control
- **competence** — feeling effective and able to grow
- **relatedness** — connection to others

A job that is “less interesting” often threatens the first two:
- less autonomy over what you care about
- less felt competence-growth or intellectual vitality

A role leading a team may increase autonomy/impact, though — which is why your case isn’t simply “interesting vs money.”

---

## 5) Person–job fit matters: people are happier when the work matches their interests

There’s a separate literature on **interest congruence** / **person–job fit** (Holland-type vocational fit research; later meta-analytic work such as Nye et al.).

The result is usually:

> People whose jobs fit their interests and dispositions tend to report higher job satisfaction and somewhat better performance.

The effect is not enormous, but it is reliable.

This matters in your case because “leading a small team” is not just a title. If that matches something you deeply want, that is itself an intrinsic factor, not just an extrinsic one.

---

## 6) Do people regret choosing interesting work over higher pay?

This specific “regret” question is studied much less directly than job satisfaction.

What the literature measures more often is:

- job satisfaction
- burnout
- engagement
- turnover intention
- mental well-being

So I’d be careful claiming there is a strong direct evidence base on **regret** specifically.

But the indirect evidence points like this:

- People in more intrinsically rewarding jobs tend to report higher satisfaction.
- People adapt relatively quickly to pay increases.
- Misfit in daily work content tends to keep hurting, because you experience it every day.

So if you want the most research-grounded answer possible, it would be:

> The evidence is stronger for “people are happier in intrinsically satisfying work” than for “people later regret not maximizing salary.”

---

## 7) What about leadership roles specifically?

This part is more mixed.

Leadership/management roles can increase:
- status
- pay
- scope
- perceived impact
- autonomy

But they also often increase:
- stress
- hours
- coordination overhead
- administrative load
- people problems
- distance from hands-on work

So the research here usually suggests that **leadership improves satisfaction mainly when it fits what the person actually wants**.

That is, if you truly want to manage and build a team, that can be a real well-being gain.
If you mainly want the title, but dislike the actual work of management, it often disappoints.

This is important because many technical people say they want to “lead,” but the thing they actually want is one of:
- more influence
- more strategic input
- mentoring
- ownership
- recognition

Those are not identical to formal people management.

---

## 8) A research-based summary for your exact tradeoff

If I translate the literature into your case as carefully as possible:

### The evidence suggests:
- **Interesting, autonomous, meaningful work** is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.
- **Higher salary alone** usually has a smaller effect on satisfaction than people expect.
- **Leadership opportunity** can matter a lot, but mainly if leading a team is genuinely important to your sense of growth and fit.
- If the Meta role is **much less interesting**, that is a real negative in the variables most strongly tied to long-run satisfaction.
- If “leading a small team” is a deep aspiration, that is also a real positive in the same intrinsic domain — not merely a résumé boost.

So from a research perspective, the tradeoff is **not**:

- interesting work vs money

It is more like:

- interesting work / intrinsic motivation  
  **vs**
- leadership aspiration / autonomy / impact / growth  
  plus some extra salary

That’s why the answer isn’t automatically “stay” or “go,” even on the science.

---

## 9) What seems most robust from the literature

If I had to compress the research into a few high-confidence claims:

1. **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work predicts job satisfaction strongly.**
2. **Pay predicts job satisfaction only modestly, especially once compensation is already high.**
3. **Fit matters:** if management is something you authentically want, that can be a major positive.
4. **Daily work experience matters more than people often expect.** People live inside the job content every day.
5. **There is less strong evidence on “regret” than on satisfaction and well-being.**

---

## 10) If you want, I can also give you:
- a short reading list of the most relevant papers/meta-analyses
- a table comparing effect sizes roughly across pay, autonomy, meaning, and fit
- or a decision framework built only from the research literature, not personal advice

If you want, I can do the reading-list version next.

answer:
Yes — there is a fair amount of research on this, though it usually uses terms like **intrinsic motivation**, **job characteristics**, **meaningful work**, **autonomy**, and **job satisfaction** rather than “interesting work.”

## Short version

The broad result is:

- **More interesting / autonomous / meaningful work is consistently associated with higher job satisfaction and better well-being.**
- **Pay matters, but its relationship with job satisfaction is usually much weaker than people expect**, especially once income is already high.
- **Autonomy and day-to-day task quality** tend to matter a lot.
- On **regret specifically**, the literature is much thinner. But the evidence we do have suggests people often **overweight salary/status** and **underweight the lived experience of the work itself**.

So if the tradeoff is **“slightly more money + management opportunity” vs “substantially more interesting work”**, the literature generally points to the interesting work side as being more predictive of sustained satisfaction — **unless** the management opportunity gives you something you deeply value, like autonomy, growth, or identity fit.

---

## What the research says

### 1) Job design research: interesting work strongly predicts satisfaction

This is probably the most relevant literature.

The classic framework is **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**, which says jobs are more motivating when they provide:

- **skill variety**
- **task identity**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

Those are close cousins of what most people mean by “interesting work.”

Two major summaries:

- **Fried & Ferris (1987)** — meta-analysis  
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — larger, more modern meta-analysis

Their overall conclusion: jobs higher in these characteristics are associated with higher:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **job satisfaction**
- **engagement**
- often **performance**

And among these factors, **autonomy** is usually one of the strongest predictors.

### Takeaway
If the Anthropic role is much more intellectually engaging, varied, and autonomous, research would predict that this matters a lot for job satisfaction.

---

### 2) Meaningful work also predicts well-being

A related literature studies **meaningful work** — work perceived as significant, worthwhile, or aligned with one’s values.

Meta-analytic work by **Allan and colleagues** and others finds that meaningful work is associated with:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **work engagement**
- higher **life satisfaction**
- lower **turnover intentions**
- better **mental health**

This isn’t exactly the same as “interesting,” but in knowledge work they often overlap: people often find work interesting because it feels important, intellectually alive, or aligned with identity.

### Takeaway
If one role feels notably more meaningful or intrinsically compelling, the literature treats that as a major positive.

---

### 3) Pay matters — but usually less than people think for job satisfaction

There’s a robust finding here:

- **Pay is positively related to job satisfaction**
- but the relationship is **surprisingly modest**

A well-known meta-analysis by **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** found that the correlation between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **quite small** — around **r ≈ .15**.

That does **not** mean money is irrelevant. It means:

- it matters more when pay is low or financially stressful
- it matters when pay is perceived as **unfair**
- but once you’re already well-compensated, more pay tends to buy **less additional satisfaction** than changes in day-to-day job quality

In higher-income populations, extra money often improves life convenience and security, but it usually does **not** transform how enjoyable work feels on Tuesday afternoon.

### Takeaway
If the Meta offer is only “a bit higher salary,” the literature would generally predict a **small** direct well-being benefit from the money itself, especially at already-high tech compensation levels.

---

### 4) Autonomy and control matter a lot for mental health

Another strong research stream studies **job control** or **decision latitude**.

Findings from the **job demand–control** literature and reviews like **Stansfeld & Candy (2006)** show that low-control jobs are associated with worse mental health outcomes. Large cohort studies like **Whitehall II** also found that low job control predicts worse health.

This matters because a move into management can affect autonomy in either direction:

- Sometimes leadership roles increase autonomy and influence.
- Sometimes they replace deep work with coordination, people issues, and political load, which some people experience as *less* satisfying.

### Takeaway
If the team-lead role would give you much more real control over what gets built and how, that is a real positive in the research.  
If it mostly replaces interesting technical work with meetings and people-management overhead, the autonomy benefit may be smaller than the title suggests.

---

### 5) Poor-quality jobs can offset the benefits of employment itself

A more sobering line of research looks at **job quality**, not just being employed.

For example, **Butterworth et al.** found that poor psychosocial job quality — low control, high demands, insecurity, unfairness — can be so harmful that some bad jobs provide little or no mental-health advantage over unemployment.

That’s not your situation, obviously. But it reinforces a broader point:

> The quality of the work experience matters enormously; “a job” is not a single category.

In elite knowledge work, this often shows up as:
- interesting vs stale work
- creative autonomy vs bureaucracy
- mission alignment vs misalignment
- deep craft vs organizational overhead

---

## What about regret?

This is the weakest part of the evidence base.

I don’t know of a strong literature saying, cleanly, “people who choose the more interesting job regret it less 10 years later.” That exact question is not very well studied.

What we do have are adjacent findings:

### A) Intrinsic vs extrinsic goals
Research in **Self-Determination Theory** and related work (e.g. **Kasser & Ryan**) finds that prioritizing **intrinsic goals** — growth, learning, connection, contribution — is generally associated with better well-being than prioritizing **extrinsic goals** like money, status, and image.

This doesn’t prove every interesting-job choice is correct, but it does suggest that choices aligned with intrinsic motives tend to fit human well-being better.

### B) Affective forecasting errors
There is also a large literature showing people are imperfect at predicting what will make them happy. They often **overestimate** the lasting emotional impact of salary/status changes and **underestimate** the importance of recurring daily experience.

Work is one of the most repeated daily experiences in adult life. That makes the *texture* of the job matter a lot.

### C) Hedonic adaptation
People often adapt to raises, titles, and prestige faster than they expect. They may adapt less fully to the day-to-day quality of the work itself, because it keeps showing up every day.

### Takeaway
Direct regret evidence is limited, but the surrounding literature points in the same direction: **people often mispredict how much more money or status will improve lived experience relative to work content and autonomy.**

---

## Important caveats

### 1) “Interesting work” is not the only intrinsic factor
Sometimes a less interesting role still wins on:
- autonomy
- learning
- leadership growth
- identity fit
- future option value

If leading a team is something you’ve wanted for a long time, that matters. Research on well-being strongly emphasizes **person-job fit**.

### 2) Interesting work can have downsides
Some studies on passion/calling suggest that highly interesting work can also lead to:
- overwork
- identity fusion with work
- accepting bad boundaries
- tolerating lower pay or instability

So “more interesting” is not automatically better if it comes with major costs in stress, workload, or lack of control.

### 3) Most studies are not on senior AI researchers choosing between Anthropic and Meta
A lot of this literature is broad and observational. The core findings are robust, but the exact tradeoff in your case is unusually specific and high-end.

---

## If I had to compress the literature into a few claims

1. **Day-to-day job quality matters a lot.**
2. **Autonomy is one of the most important job features.**
3. **Interesting/meaningful work predicts higher satisfaction more strongly than pay does.**
4. **At high income levels, a modest salary bump usually has a relatively small effect on happiness.**
5. **Direct evidence on “regret” is sparse, but adjacent evidence suggests people often underweight intrinsic fit.**

---

## The papers / literatures most worth looking at

If you want a reading list, I’d start with these:

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976, 1980)** — Job Characteristics Model  
- **Fried & Ferris (1987)** — meta-analysis on the job characteristics model  
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design and outcomes  
- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** — pay level and job satisfaction meta-analysis  
- **Stansfeld & Candy (2006)** — psychosocial work environment and mental health  
- **Butterworth et al. (2011)** — psychosocial quality of work and mental health  
- **Deci & Ryan / Self-Determination Theory** — autonomy, competence, relatedness at work  
- **Kasser & Ryan** — intrinsic vs extrinsic goals and well-being  
- **Allan et al.** on **meaningful work** and well-being

---

## Bottom line from the research

If your current job is **substantially more interesting** and the Meta role is only **slightly better on pay**, then the literature would generally predict that **staying in the more interesting role is more likely to preserve happiness/job satisfaction**.

The strongest research-based reasons *against* that conclusion would be if the Meta role offers much more of one of these:

- **autonomy**
- **real leadership growth**
- **better fit with your long-term identity**
- **substantially better future career option value**

If you want, I can do a second pass and give you:
1. a **more formal literature review** with paper-by-paper summaries, or  
2. a **research-based decision framework** for this exact choice.

answer:
Yes. There’s a fairly solid research base here, and the broad pattern is:

**Interesting/intrinsically rewarding work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement, usually stronger than pay alone.**  
For **overall life happiness**, the effect is still positive, but smaller and more indirect.  
For **regret**, the evidence is much thinner; researchers study satisfaction, fit, burnout, and turnover more than explicit “career regret.”

A useful translation: in the literature, “interesting work” is usually broken into things like:

- **autonomy**
- **skill variety / learning**
- **task significance / meaning**
- **feedback**
- **fit with your values and motives**

Those are the dimensions most consistently linked to well-being.

## What the research says

### 1) Job design matters a lot
A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features*

This synthesized a very large literature on work design. The key result was that features such as:

- autonomy
- task variety
- task significance
- feedback
- social support

were **strongly associated** with:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **internal motivation**
- higher **engagement**
- lower **burnout**
- lower **turnover intentions**

This is probably the single most relevant body of evidence for “interesting work vs. other job factors.”

### 2) Meaningful work has robust benefits
Another strong result:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, *Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis*

They found that **meaningful work** is reliably associated with:

- greater **job satisfaction**
- greater **life satisfaction**
- greater **well-being**
- greater **commitment**
- lower **withdrawal intentions**

So if “the work is much more interesting” also means “it feels more meaningful / intellectually alive / worth doing,” research generally says that matters a lot.

### 3) Pay matters, but less than many people expect
On salary specifically:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis*

Their conclusion was basically that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**.

That is an important result for your situation. It does **not** say money doesn’t matter. It does say that, in the data, differences in **job content** and **fit** often matter more for day-to-day job satisfaction than salary differences do.

Especially once someone is already well-compensated, a “bit higher salary” usually predicts a **smaller change in satisfaction** than a major difference in:

- autonomy
- challenge
- learning
- interest
- meaning

### 4) Fit matters a lot
A classic meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, *Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work*

They found that **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** are strongly related to:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **organizational commitment**
- lower **intention to quit**

This matters because the average effect is not the whole story. Some people care enormously about interesting work; others care more about scope, team leadership, compensation, or stability.

So the literature says not just “interesting work is good,” but more precisely:  
**jobs that fit your motives and values tend to produce better long-run outcomes.**

### 5) Intrinsic vs extrinsic goals: intrinsic usually wins for well-being
There’s also a large literature from self-determination theory.

Relevant names here are **Deci**, **Ryan**, **Vansteenkiste**, **Van den Broeck**.

The recurring result is that when work satisfies psychological needs for:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

people tend to show better:

- well-being
- persistence
- engagement
- lower burnout

And when people orient strongly toward **intrinsic work values** (growth, interest, contribution, learning), they tend to do better psychologically than when they orient mainly toward **extrinsic values** (money, status, image), especially if the extrinsic values dominate.

A useful paper in this area is:

- **Vansteenkiste et al. (2007)** on **intrinsic vs extrinsic work value orientation**

The takeaway is not “money is bad.” It’s that **money alone is usually a weaker source of durable satisfaction than intrinsic work quality.**

## For researchers/scientists specifically: yes, there is direct evidence
This is especially relevant to your case.

- **Stern (2004)**, *Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?*

This is one of the clearest papers showing that scientists/researchers are often willing to accept **lower pay** in exchange for the non-monetary rewards of scientific work itself—things like:

- research freedom
- ability to publish
- doing work that feels genuinely scientific/intellectually interesting

There are follow-ups too, including work by **Sauermann & Roach**, showing that this preference is **real but heterogeneous**: some researchers value those non-pecuniary aspects a lot, some less.

That’s important. The evidence does **not** say everyone should choose the more interesting job. It says many researchers demonstrably place a large value on intellectually meaningful work, enough to trade salary for it.

## Do people regret choosing the more interesting job?
This is the weakest part of the evidence base.

There is **much less direct research** on explicit regret like:

- “I chose the interesting job over the higher-paying one; do I regret it 5 years later?”

What the literature more often measures is:

- satisfaction
- burnout
- commitment
- turnover intentions
- value congruence / fit

From that literature, the safest summary is:

- people tend to do worse when there is a **mismatch** between what they value and what the job provides
- dissatisfaction is often driven by **loss of autonomy, loss of meaning, poor fit, or chronic stress**
- there is **not** strong evidence for a general pattern that people later regret choosing the more intrinsically interesting job over a somewhat higher-paying one

So if you want the most evidence-based phrasing:

> Direct regret evidence is limited, but the stronger empirical pattern is that intrinsically rewarding, well-fitting work predicts higher satisfaction and lower desire to leave, while pay has a more modest effect.

## One nuance that matters in your case
Your choice is not just:

- **interesting work**
vs
- **more money**

It also includes:

- **leading a small team**

Research would treat leadership opportunity as potentially **intrinsic**, not just extrinsic, if it gives you more:

- autonomy
- impact
- skill variety
- growth
- identity

So the relevant comparison is really:

### Anthropic:
- more intellectually interesting
- maybe stronger intrinsic motivation from the work itself

### Meta:
- a bit more salary
- leadership opportunity
- maybe more autonomy/impact, but less interesting day-to-day work

The literature would say both sides contain non-monetary value. The highest-pay option is not automatically the highest-well-being option; the **best-fitting bundle of intrinsic and extrinsic rewards** usually predicts better outcomes.

## If I had to compress the literature into 3 lines

1. **Interesting / meaningful / autonomous work is a major driver of job satisfaction and engagement.**
2. **Pay matters, but its effect on satisfaction is usually smaller than people assume, especially once pay is already high.**
3. **The best predictor of long-run outcomes is often fit: whether the job gives you the things you personally care about most.**

## Papers worth reading
If you want a short reading list, I’d start with:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — work design meta-analysis  
- **Judge et al. (2010)** — pay and job satisfaction meta-analysis  
- **Allan et al. (2019)** — meaningful work meta-analysis  
- **Kristof-Brown et al. (2005)** — person-job / person-organization fit meta-analysis  
- **Stern (2004)** — scientists trading pay for scientific work  
- **Sauermann & Roach** — heterogeneity in scientists’ preferences  
- **Van den Broeck et al. (2016)** / **Vansteenkiste et al.** — self-determination and intrinsic vs extrinsic work values

If you want, I can turn this into a **more concrete evidence summary for your exact tradeoff**—e.g. “interesting research vs pay vs first-line management responsibility”—and keep it strictly tied to published findings.

**Considering job satisfaction and adaptation**

While people may feel happier in the present moment, adaptation can mean that the benefits of salary or status aren't lasting. answer:
Yes. There isn’t a single famous study on the exact choice “more interesting IC role vs less interesting team-lead role for somewhat more money,” but several research literatures converge on a fairly clear pattern:

## Short version

Across organizational psychology and well-being research, **intrinsic job quality** — things like **interesting/challenging work, autonomy, learning, task significance, and fit with your motives/values** — tends to be a **stronger predictor of job satisfaction and day-to-day well-being** than **pay level**, especially once pay is already “good enough.”

So if by “interesting” you mean intellectually engaging, meaningful, and growth-promoting work, the research generally says that **people in those jobs are happier on average** than people in less interesting jobs that merely pay somewhat more.

What the literature is **much weaker on** is your exact “future regret” question. There is not, to my knowledge, a strong body of direct evidence saying “people regret choosing interesting work over higher pay” or vice versa. The evidence is stronger for **current and medium-term satisfaction, engagement, burnout, and turnover** than for retrospective regret.

---

# The main findings I’d lean on

## 1) Job design research: interesting/challenging/autonomous work matters a lot

A very large literature on **work design** finds that jobs with more:

- **autonomy**
- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- **opportunities to learn/use skills**

are associated with higher:

- **job satisfaction**
- **internal motivation**
- **engagement**
- **performance**
- lower **turnover intention**

A major reference here is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, a **meta-analysis** of work design research across **259 studies**.

Their overall conclusion was not subtle: motivational job characteristics are strongly related to important outcomes, especially satisfaction and motivation.

This is probably the closest literature to your “interesting work” question.

### Why this matters for your situation
If Anthropic is substantially more interesting because it gives you more intellectual stimulation, learning, and intrinsic motivation, that is not a soft or trivial factor. In the literature, that kind of thing is usually a **central determinant** of how people feel about their work.

---

## 2) Pay matters, but its relationship with job satisfaction is surprisingly modest

There is also a meta-analysis specifically on **pay and job satisfaction**:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**

The key result people remember is that the correlation between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but fairly small**. In plain English: higher-paid people are somewhat more satisfied, but **not by that much**, and certainly not enough to dominate everything else.

That doesn’t mean money is irrelevant. It means:

- money matters a lot when it changes real life constraints, security, or status in a way you care about;
- but **modest pay differences** usually do **less** for happiness than people expect.

This is also consistent with broader subjective well-being research: people often **adapt** to income changes, while the day-to-day experience of work keeps happening every day.

### Relevance to your case
If Meta is offering “a bit higher salary,” the literature would generally predict that this **by itself** is not likely to compensate for a meaningfully worse day-to-day work experience.

---

## 3) Fit matters a lot: the right role for *you* beats generic prestige

Another strong literature is **person-job / person-organization fit**.

A key reference:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, meta-analysis on person-environment fit.

General finding: when there is better fit between a person and their job/organization, you see higher:

- job satisfaction
- organizational commitment
- performance

and lower turnover intentions.

This matters because “leading a small team” is not just an extrinsic perk. For some people, management is intrinsically rewarding; for others, it pulls them away from the work they actually love.

So research would say the key question is not simply:

- “management vs IC”

but rather:

- “which role is more aligned with my motives, values, and strengths?”

If leading people is something you’ve “always wanted,” that is potentially a **real intrinsic benefit**, not just a status signal.

---

## 4) Meaningful work is consistently associated with better well-being

There is also a substantial literature on **meaningful work**. A useful summary source is:

- **Allan et al. (2019)**, meta-analysis on outcomes of meaningful work

The broad finding: people who experience their work as meaningful tend to report better:

- job satisfaction
- life satisfaction
- well-being
- engagement

and lower turnover intention.

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but they often travel together in knowledge work. If one job feels much more worthwhile, stimulating, or aligned with what you care about, the literature treats that as consequential.

---

## 5) Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, relatedness are core

Another very robust line of work comes from **Self-Determination Theory (SDT)**. Across many studies, including workplace research, satisfaction of the needs for:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

is associated with better well-being, motivation, and performance.

So if the more interesting role also gives you:

- more freedom,
- more mastery,
- more contact with people you like and respect,

that is a strong positive package.

If the team-lead role gives you more autonomy and impact but less competence/interest because the work is dull, then you’re looking at a real tradeoff among these basic needs.

---

## 6) Promotions/leadership can help, but they are not automatically happiness-improving

The literature on promotions and management is mixed enough that I would be cautious.

Leadership roles often bring:

- more status
- more autonomy
- more pay
- more influence

but also:

- more coordination overhead
- more responsibility for others
- more interruptions
- less time on the work you may actually enjoy
- more stress / role conflict

So “team lead” is not automatically better for well-being. It depends heavily on whether:

- you genuinely want the people/coordination side,
- the scope is manageable,
- the team is good,
- the organization lets managers actually lead rather than just absorb process.

This is where “I always wanted to lead” matters a lot. If that desire is strong and authentic, the role may satisfy **growth** and **impact** motives even if the domain itself is less interesting.

---

# What about regret specifically?

This is the weakest part of the evidence base.

I **don’t know of strong research directly showing** that people who choose more interesting work over higher-paying/managerial roles later regret it less, or vice versa.

What I do think the broader evidence supports is this:

1. **People are not great at forecasting** how much salary/status bumps will affect long-term well-being.
2. **Daily experience** matters a lot because work is a repeated experience, not a one-time event.
3. Regret often comes from violating one’s deeper values or goals — which means the “right” answer depends a lot on whether your stronger value is:
   - doing frontier research,
   - or developing as a leader.

So I would be comfortable saying:
- the evidence for **current happiness** favors intrinsic job quality;
- the evidence for **future regret** is much thinner and more person-specific.

---

# If I translate the literature into your exact tradeoff

Very roughly, the research suggests something like:

## Staying in the more interesting job is more likely to maximize happiness if:
- the salary difference is not life-changing,
- the current role gives you strong intellectual engagement,
- you highly value interesting problems and talented peers,
- the management opportunity is nice-to-have rather than core-to-identity.

## Taking the Meta offer is more likely to maximize happiness if:
- leading people is itself deeply motivating to you,
- you would gain real autonomy/scope/impact,
- the work is “less interesting” but still sufficiently engaging,
- the role is a strong fit for a longer-term goal you care about.

The important research-based nuance is: **leadership opportunity can itself be an intrinsic good**. So this is not necessarily “interesting work vs money.” It may be:

- **interesting technical work**
vs
- **growth/leadership/impact**

And research does not say technical interest always beats growth or leadership. It says **intrinsic factors and fit usually matter more than modest pay differences**.

---

# The most defensible bottom line

If you want the answer most supported by the literature, I’d put it this way:

> **Yes: on average, people are happier in jobs that are intrinsically rewarding — interesting, autonomous, meaningful, growth-promoting, and well-matched to them — than in jobs that are merely somewhat better paid.**
>
> **No: I would not say there is equally strong evidence about long-run regret from this exact kind of career choice.**
>
> **And the biggest moderator in your case is whether “leading a small team” is itself one of the intrinsically rewarding things you most want.**

---

# Good papers/references to look up

If you want to read the actual research, these are good starting points:

1. **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).**  
   *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*  
   Journal of Applied Psychology.

2. **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).**  
   *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*  
   Journal of Vocational Behavior.

3. **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005).**  
   *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.*  
   Personnel Psychology.

4. **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L. (2019).**  
   *Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.*  
   Journal of Management Studies.

5. **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976/1980).**  
   Classic work on the **Job Characteristics Model**.

6. For SDT at work, a good review starting point is:  
   **Deci, Olafsen, & Ryan (2017)** on self-determination theory in work organizations.

---

If you want, I can do a second pass that is even more research-heavy: e.g. a **mini literature review with effect sizes and stronger distinctions between job satisfaction, life satisfaction, burnout, and regret**.

answer:
Yes. The short version from organizational psychology and labor economics is:

- **Interesting/intrinsically rewarding work is a major predictor of job satisfaction and engagement.**
- **Its association with well-being is usually larger than the association of pay level with well-being.**
- **Promotions/leadership roles help some people, but not uniformly; they often bring more stress and administration, so the net effect depends a lot on fit.**
- **There is much less direct research on “later regret” than on job satisfaction, burnout, and turnover.**

A useful caveat: research usually does **not** study “interesting” as one simple variable. It breaks it into things like:

- intrinsic motivation / intellectual interest
- skill variety and learning
- autonomy
- task significance / meaning
- person-job fit
- opportunities for growth

Those are the constructs most relevant to your question.

## 1) Job design research: interesting work matters a lot

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham). It predicts that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they offer:

- **skill variety**
- **task identity**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

Meta-analytic work has generally supported this. In particular:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** reviewed a very large work-design literature and found that motivational job characteristics like **autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback** show **moderate-to-strong positive relationships** with:
  - job satisfaction
  - internal motivation
  - engagement
  - commitment  
  and negative relationships with turnover intentions.

A reasonable summary of that literature is: **the day-to-day nature of the work itself strongly affects how people feel about their jobs**.

## 2) Meaningful / intrinsically rewarding work is linked to higher well-being

A separate but overlapping literature looks at **meaningful work** and **intrinsic motivation**.

Broadly, it finds:

- People who experience their work as meaningful report:
  - higher job satisfaction
  - lower burnout
  - lower turnover intentions
  - often better general well-being as well

This is also consistent with **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan): work is better for well-being when it supports three psychological needs:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Interesting work often supports **competence** and sometimes **autonomy**. Leading a team can support **autonomy** and **relatedness**. So your choice is not just “interesting vs money”; it may be “competence/interest vs autonomy/leadership.”

## 3) Pay matters, but usually less than people expect once pay is already high enough

There is a well-known meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** found that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but fairly small**. The headline result often cited is a correlation around **.15**.

That does **not** mean money is unimportant. It means:

- salary helps
- but salary alone explains relatively little of who is satisfied at work

This is especially relevant when comparing **two already high-paying jobs**. In that range, the incremental happiness effect of “a bit more money” is usually smaller than the effect of:

- enjoying the work
- having autonomy
- feeling effective
- liking the people
- having a role that fits your values and goals

On broader life well-being, income still matters, but the effect shows **diminishing marginal returns**, especially at high income levels. So if both options are already financially excellent, research would usually predict that **non-monetary job qualities become comparatively more important**.

## 4) Promotions and management are not universally happiness-improving

There is research on promotions and supervisory roles showing something like this pattern:

- promotions often increase:
  - status
  - pay
  - perceived success
- but they also often increase:
  - stress
  - time pressure
  - administrative load
  - work-family conflict

Panel-data studies on promotions often find an **initial boost in job satisfaction**, but the effect may be **modest or temporary**, and it does not always translate into better overall well-being.

So the evidence does **not** support a simple view that “moving into leadership makes people happier.” It seems to depend heavily on whether the person actually wants the work of managing:

- coaching
- hiring
- conflict resolution
- prioritization
- more meetings
- less direct technical work

This matters for your case because “leading a small team” is a genuine positive only if you want the **actual substance of management**, not just the title or the abstract idea.

## 5) Intrinsic goals usually predict better well-being than extrinsic goals

A long line of research from **Self-Determination Theory** and related work finds that prioritizing **intrinsic goals**—growth, mastery, relationships, contribution—tends to be associated with better well-being than prioritizing **extrinsic goals** like money, image, and status.

That does **not** mean higher salary is bad. It means that when people orient their choices mainly around external rewards, the well-being payoff is often weaker than they expect.

For your situation, this suggests a key distinction:

- If Meta is attractive **mainly because of more money/status**, the literature is not very encouraging about that being the best route to sustained satisfaction.
- If Meta is attractive because **leading people is itself intrinsically important to you**, then the move may fit the same research very well.

## 6) What about regret?

This is the part where the evidence is thinner.

I do **not** know of strong, famous studies that cleanly track people over years and ask:  
“Did you regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paying management job?”

Most of the literature uses proxies like:

- job satisfaction
- burnout
- turnover intentions
- actual quitting
- engagement
- mental health

Those proxies generally suggest:

- less meaningful / less intrinsically rewarding work is associated with more disengagement and more desire to leave
- more meaningful / interesting work is associated with lower turnover and higher satisfaction

So while the “regret” literature is not as direct, the broader evidence points toward **people doing better in jobs they find intrinsically rewarding and fitting**, especially when basic financial needs are already more than met.

## 7) The research-based takeaway for a case like yours

If I translate the literature into your exact tradeoff, the findings would suggest:

1. **Interesting work is not a minor perk.**  
   It is one of the strongest known predictors of job satisfaction.

2. **A somewhat higher salary is unlikely, by itself, to compensate for a large drop in day-to-day interest**, especially when both jobs already pay very well.

3. **But leadership opportunity is a real counterweight**, because autonomy, influence, and growth are also strong predictors of work satisfaction.

So the evidence does **not** say:
- “Always stay with the more interesting job.”

It says something closer to:
- **People tend to do best when their work satisfies intrinsic motives** such as interest, mastery, autonomy, meaning, and fit.
- If your strongest intrinsic motive right now is **building and leading a team**, then the Meta role may fit the literature better than it first appears.
- If the leadership piece is secondary and the main change is “more money, less interesting work,” the literature would usually favor staying where the work is more intrinsically engaging.

## 8) Best references to start from

If you want the core research, these are good anchor points:

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1980). _Work Redesign_.**
- **Fried, Y., & Ferris, G. R. (1987).** The validity of the Job Characteristics Model: A review and meta-analysis.
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).** Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).** The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis.
- **Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M. (2000).** The “what” and “why” of goal pursuits: Human needs and the self-determination of behavior.
- **Rosso, B. D., Dekas, K. H., & Wrzesniewski, A. (2010).** On the meaning of work: A theoretical integration and review.
- For income and well-being more broadly:
  - **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**
  - **Killingsworth (2021)**
  - **Killingsworth, Kahneman, & Mellers (2023)**

If you want, I can also do a **more concrete literature-based decision matrix** for your two options using the dimensions that actually predict job satisfaction: interest, autonomy, growth, meaning, leadership fit, social environment, and compensation.

answer:
Yes. The short version is:

- **“Interesting work”** — usually studied as **intrinsic motivation, meaningful work, autonomy, skill variety, and person-job fit** — is **consistently one of the strongest predictors** of job satisfaction and work well-being.
- **Pay matters**, but in the job-satisfaction literature its effect is usually **smaller** than those intrinsic job qualities.
- **Leadership/promotion** is more mixed: it can increase satisfaction through growth, autonomy, and status, but also increase stress and reduce time spent on the work itself.
- **Direct evidence on “regret” is surprisingly thin**. The literature is much stronger on satisfaction, engagement, burnout, turnover intentions, and long-run well-being than on retrospective regret.

A research-based summary follows.

---

## 1) Job design research: interesting/autonomous work predicts satisfaction

A large classic literature in organizational psychology says that jobs are better when they have:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **task identity**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

This is the **Job Characteristics Model**.

### Key findings
- **Hackman & Oldham (1976, 1980)**: these characteristics predict **internal work motivation** and **job satisfaction**.
- **Fried & Ferris (1987)**, meta-analysis: broadly supported the model; job characteristics were meaningfully related to **motivation** and **satisfaction**.
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, meta-analysis: motivational job characteristics — especially **autonomy**, **task variety**, and **task significance** — had strong relationships with **job satisfaction**, **engagement**, **commitment**, and lower **turnover intentions**.

### What this means for your question
If by “interesting” you mean something like:
- intellectually engaging,
- challenging,
- meaningful,
- with room for creativity,
- and not overly constrained,

then the literature says those features are **not trivial perks**. They are central to whether people like their jobs.

---

## 2) Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being

A more recent literature studies **meaningful work** directly.

### Key findings
- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis: meaningful work is positively associated with:
  - **job satisfaction**
  - **work engagement**
  - **life satisfaction**
  - **organizational commitment**

  and negatively associated with:
  - **turnover intentions**
  - **strain/burnout-type outcomes**

The effects are typically **moderate to fairly large** by psychology standards.

### Interpretation
People who feel their work is meaningful or interesting are, on average, **more satisfied** and **less likely to want to leave**. That does not prove every individual should always choose the more meaningful role, but it does strongly suggest that “interestingness” has real predictive power for happiness at work.

---

## 3) Person-job fit matters a lot

Another relevant literature asks whether the role fits a person’s **interests, values, and abilities**.

### Key findings
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, meta-analysis on person-environment fit:
  - **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** are strongly associated with **job satisfaction**
  - also linked to **organizational commitment**
  - and lower **intent to quit**

- Related vocational-interest work also finds that **interest congruence** is associated with higher satisfaction, though effects there are often more modest than the broader job-fit literature.

### Interpretation
A role that fits what you naturally like doing tends to produce better outcomes than a role that is objectively “good” but misaligned with your interests.

For someone in research, this matters a lot: a role can be prestigious or higher-paying and still be a poorer fit if the day-to-day work is less interesting.

---

## 4) Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, relatedness

Another large body of research says well-being at work depends heavily on whether work satisfies three basic psychological needs:

- **autonomy** — feeling you have choice/control
- **competence** — feeling effective and growing
- **relatedness** — feeling connected to others

### Key findings
Across many studies and reviews in **self-determination theory**:
- need satisfaction at work predicts **higher motivation**, **higher engagement**, and **greater job satisfaction**
- and predicts **lower burnout** and **lower turnover intentions**

### Why this matters here
“Interesting work” often scores highly on:
- autonomy,
- competence growth,
- and sometimes meaning.

A team-lead role might increase:
- autonomy,
- competence in a new domain,
- status.

But if it reduces the actual research work you enjoy, it may lower the “interesting work” component while increasing management load.

So from a research perspective, your choice is not simply:
- interesting vs salary

It is more like:
- **interestingness/meaning**
- **fit**
- **autonomy**
- **growth**
- **social environment**
- **pay**
- **stress/load**

---

## 5) Pay matters, but usually less than people expect for job satisfaction

There is solid evidence that pay has a **positive but modest** relationship with job satisfaction.

### Key finding
- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, meta-analysis: the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **small**.

A reasonable summary is:
- the correlation is **real**
- but much smaller than the correlations usually found for:
  - meaningful work,
  - autonomy,
  - job fit,
  - good management,
  - or engaging job design

### Important caveat
This does **not** mean money is unimportant.
Money matters more when:
- the raise meaningfully changes financial security,
- reduces stress,
- affects family options,
- changes location or lifestyle,
- or corrects a feeling of unfair pay.

But for a **“bit higher salary”**, the literature would generally predict a **smaller effect** on day-to-day job satisfaction than the effect of the job being substantially more or less interesting.

---

## 6) There is direct evidence that scientists accept lower pay for more “scientific” work

This is especially relevant to you.

### Key finding
- **Stern (2004), “Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?”** found evidence that scientists are willing to accept **lower wages** for jobs that provide the non-monetary rewards of science, especially the ability to do research and publish.

### Interpretation
For research-oriented people, the labor market itself reflects that **interesting/scientific work is a valued good**. People are often willing to trade compensation for it.

This does not prove they are always happier afterward, but it is strong evidence that these non-monetary features are genuinely important and not just post-hoc rationalization.

---

## 7) Promotions / leading a team: the evidence is mixed

You specifically mentioned that leading a small team is something you’ve always wanted.

That is important, because leadership is itself a source of:
- growth,
- autonomy,
- identity,
- status,
- and sometimes meaning.

But the literature on **promotion/leadership and well-being** is **much less clean** than the literature on meaningful work or job design.

### Broad research pattern
Promotions often bring:
- higher status,
- more influence,
- sometimes higher satisfaction in the short run,

but also:
- longer hours,
- more role conflict,
- more responsibility,
- more stress,
- less time on the technical work itself.

So the net effect depends heavily on:
- whether you actually enjoy managing,
- whether the org supports managers well,
- whether you still get some of the work you find intrinsically rewarding.

### Research-based translation
If leading a team is a deep personal aspiration, it should not be treated as merely “extrinsic.” It may be part of your **intrinsic fit** and **growth needs**.

---

## 8) What about regret?

This is where the evidence is weaker.

I’m not aware of a strong literature that directly answers:
> “Do people regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paid/promotion job?”

What we do have are proxies:
- **job satisfaction**
- **career satisfaction**
- **engagement**
- **burnout**
- **turnover intentions**
- **mental well-being**

And on those outcomes, the research generally favors:
- meaningful/interesting work,
- good fit,
- autonomy,
- competence growth,

more strongly than modest pay differences.

### So the best research-based answer is:
- **Yes**, people in more intrinsically rewarding and better-fitting jobs tend to report **higher satisfaction and better well-being** on average.
- **No**, there is not a comparably strong body of evidence specifically on long-run **regret** after choosing interesting work over higher pay/promotion.

---

## 9) If I compress the literature into one sentence

For **knowledge workers and researchers**, a move from **interesting research** to **less interesting work** in exchange for a **moderate pay increase** is, on average, more likely to reduce day-to-day job satisfaction than increase it — **unless** the new role strongly improves other non-monetary factors you care about, such as **leadership**, **autonomy**, **impact**, or **fit**.

---

## 10) The most relevant papers / literatures to look at

If you want a reading list, I’d start with:

1. **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976, 1980)**  
   Job Characteristics Model

2. **Fried, Y., & Ferris, G. R. (1987)**  
   Meta-analysis of the Job Characteristics Model

3. **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007)**  
   Meta-analysis of work design characteristics and outcomes

4. **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005)**  
   Meta-analysis of person-environment fit

5. **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L. (2019)**  
   Meta-analysis on outcomes of meaningful work

6. **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010)**  
   Meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction

7. **Stern, S. (2004)**  
   *Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?*

---

If you want, I can do one of two follow-ups:

1. give you a **more technical summary with effect sizes**, or  
2. build a **decision framework using only variables that have strong empirical support**.

answer:
Yes. The literature is fairly consistent on one big point:

**Once pay is already “good enough,” intrinsic job qualities — interesting work, autonomy, learning, meaning, and fit — tend to predict sustained job satisfaction more strongly than pay level alone.**

A few important nuances:

- **Direct “regret” studies** on choices like “more interesting job vs higher-paid less interesting job” are surprisingly limited.
- But the **indirect evidence** — job satisfaction, career satisfaction, well-being, burnout, and turnover — points in a pretty clear direction.
- **Leadership opportunities** complicate things, because management can increase meaning/status/autonomy for some people, but also stress, meetings, and loss of hands-on work.

## What research says

### 1) Interesting/intrinsically rewarding work matters a lot
A large work-design literature finds that jobs with:
- **skill variety**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- **opportunities to learn/use ability**

are associated with higher:
- job satisfaction
- intrinsic motivation
- engagement
- lower burnout / turnover intention

A key meta-analysis here is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design research

The broad pattern is that these job-content variables often show **moderate relationships** with satisfaction and motivation — typically much larger than the relationship between raw pay level and satisfaction.

So, in plain English:  
**Doing work that feels interesting and cognitively engaging is not a “soft” factor; it is one of the central predictors of liking your job.**

---

### 2) Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being
A related but slightly different construct is **meaningful work**: feeling that the work matters, fits your values, or contributes to something worthwhile.

Meta-analytic evidence finds meaningful work is associated with:
- higher job satisfaction
- higher life satisfaction
- more engagement
- less intent to quit

Relevant reference:
- **Allan et al. (2019)** — meta-analysis on outcomes of meaningful work

This matters because “interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but in practice they often travel together for research roles.

---

### 3) Pay matters — but less than many people expect for long-run satisfaction
There is good evidence that **pay level and job satisfaction are positively related**, but the relationship is usually **small to modest**, not huge.

Relevant reference:
- **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction

The rough pattern from this literature is:

- **absolute pay** helps, especially when it affects comfort, security, and perceived success
- but **non-pay job characteristics** often explain more of the variance in satisfaction
- **pay fairness** and **social comparison** often matter as much as, or more than, the absolute number

So the research does **not** say salary is irrelevant.  
It says that after a certain point, **job content tends to matter more for day-to-day happiness at work** than another increment in already-high compensation.

---

### 4) Fit matters a lot — including fit with wanting to lead
Another strong result in organizational psychology is that **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** predict satisfaction and commitment.

Relevant reference:
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis on person-environment fit

This is especially relevant to your case because your tradeoff is not just:
- interesting work vs money

It is also:
- **individual contributor research role** vs
- **leading a team**, which you say you’ve always wanted

Research would suggest that if leading people is a **genuine intrinsic preference** — not just status-seeking — then it can be a major positive because it increases fit.

But there’s a catch:

> Wanting to “be a leader” and wanting the actual day-to-day reality of management are not the same thing.

Management often means less direct technical work, more coordination, more performance management, more conflict handling, and more organizational friction.

So from a research perspective, the relevant variable is not “leadership prestige”; it is **whether the actual work of management fits you**.

---

### 5) Autonomy/control is one of the strongest predictors of well-being
There is a large literature on **job control** and mental health. Jobs with low control are consistently associated with worse well-being, especially when demands are high.

Relevant reference:
- **Stansfeld & Candy (2006)** — meta-analytic review of psychosocial work environment and mental health

This matters because a job can be:
- prestigious
- higher-paid
- higher-level

and still be worse subjectively if it brings:
- less autonomy
- more meetings/politics
- more role conflict
- less time doing the actual work you find absorbing

A high-demand job can be energizing **if** it also has high control.  
High demands + low control is where things often go badly.

---

### 6) Intrinsic vs extrinsic goals: intrinsic tends to win for well-being
A related body of work from self-determination theory finds that people who orient more strongly toward **intrinsic goals** (growth, relationships, contribution, mastery) tend to have better well-being than those organized more around **extrinsic goals** (money, status, image), even when extrinsic goals are attained.

Relevant references:
- **Kasser & Ryan** (1990s)
- broader **self-determination theory** work by **Deci & Ryan** and later reviews in work settings

This does **not** mean “money is bad.”  
It means that when people choose primarily on extrinsic dimensions, the well-being payoff is often weaker and less durable than expected.

---

## So: do people who stay in the more interesting job tend to be happier?

**On average, yes — if “more interesting” also means more intrinsic motivation, more learning, more autonomy, and better fit.**

That is the strongest research-backed answer.

More precisely:

- People in jobs with richer intrinsic characteristics tend to report **higher job satisfaction**
- They also tend to report **more engagement** and **less desire to quit**
- These effects are often **larger than the effect of raw pay level**
- But this is moderated by:
  - autonomy/control
  - manager and culture
  - work hours/stress
  - whether the role matches their desired identity

So “interesting work” is usually a good bet for happiness, but it is **not enough by itself** if the role is also chaotic, underpowered, or burns you out.

---

## Do they regret staying in the more interesting job?

This is where the evidence is weaker.

I don’t know of a strong literature directly measuring long-term regret in exactly this tradeoff. The field mostly measures:
- job satisfaction
- career satisfaction
- turnover intention
- burnout
- mental health
- commitment

From those proxies, the pattern suggests:

- **Choosing the intrinsically better-fitting job** is usually associated with better later evaluations
- **Choosing for money/status alone** often produces less durable gains because of adaptation and comparison effects

But there are two common sources of regret that the literature would take seriously:

### A) Regret from ignoring a real intrinsic desire to lead
If you genuinely want to build and lead a team, then passing on that may create dissatisfaction because of **poor fit** and blocked aspiration.

### B) Regret from overvaluing title/promotion and undervaluing daily work
If the leadership role means much less of the work you actually enjoy, then the daily experience may worsen even if the external package improves.

So the research-based answer is not “interesting always wins.”  
It is more like:

> **The best predictor is usually the combination of intrinsic interest + autonomy + fit.**

---

## What is probably *most* predictive in your situation

If I were translating the literature into variables that matter most here, they would be:

1. **How much more interesting is the Anthropic work, really?**  
   Small difference or huge difference?

2. **How much actual autonomy/control would you have in each role?**  
   Team lead with freedom is different from team lead inside heavy process.

3. **Do you want to lead people, or do you want advancement/status?**  
   Research strongly distinguishes these in effect, even if not always explicitly.

4. **Would the Meta role reduce your hands-on research time below the level that makes work rewarding?**

5. **Is the salary increase materially meaningful, or mostly symbolic?**  
   Research suggests this matters a lot. If it changes your real life meaningfully, that’s different from “a bit higher.”

6. **What are the manager/culture differences?**  
   These often dominate job-content effects in practice.

---

## Bottom line from the research

A fair summary would be:

- **Interesting, meaningful, autonomy-rich work is one of the strongest known predictors of job satisfaction.**
- **Pay increases improve satisfaction, but usually less than people expect once compensation is already high.**
- **Leadership opportunities can improve satisfaction when they reflect genuine fit, but can reduce it if they replace the work you actually enjoy.**
- **Direct evidence on regret is limited, but the broader evidence suggests people do better when they optimize for intrinsic fit rather than salary/status alone.**

So if you want the most research-grounded shorthand:

> **If both jobs are already well-paid, the literature would generally favor the role with better intrinsic interest, autonomy, and fit — unless the management opportunity represents an equally important intrinsic goal for you.**

## Papers / authors worth looking up
If you want to read the actual literature, these are good starting points:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — work design meta-analysis  
- **Judge et al. (2010)** — pay and job satisfaction meta-analysis  
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — person-environment fit meta-analysis  
- **Allan et al. (2019)** — meaningful work meta-analysis  
- **Stansfeld & Candy (2006)** — psychosocial work environment and mental health  
- **Deci & Ryan / self-determination theory** — autonomy, competence, relatedness at work  
- **Kasser & Ryan** — intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations and well-being

If you want, I can also turn this into a **one-page decision memo** with:
- the main findings,
- likely effect directions,
- and a research-based checklist tailored to your Anthropic vs Meta choice.

answer:
Yes. The broad answer from organizational psychology and labor economics is:

**On average, people are more satisfied in jobs that are intrinsically interesting/meaningful/autonomous than in jobs that are merely better paid.**  
**Pay matters, but usually less than people expect once pay is already high and perceived as fair.**  
**Management/promotion effects are mixed and depend a lot on fit.**

A few important caveats first:

- There is **not** a clean body of research on your exact choice: “highly interesting frontier-research IC role vs somewhat higher-paid, less interesting team lead role at another top lab/company.”
- The evidence mostly comes from adjacent literatures: **job design**, **intrinsic motivation**, **meaningful work**, **person-job fit**, **pay and job satisfaction**, and **promotion/managerial wellbeing**.
- Researchers usually measure **job satisfaction, engagement, burnout, turnover intentions, and life satisfaction**, not “regret” directly.

## 1) Interesting work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

A very robust result is that jobs high in:

- **autonomy**
- **task variety**
- **skill use / complexity**
- **task significance / meaning**
- **feedback / learning**

produce higher job satisfaction and motivation.

### Classic line of work
- **Hackman & Oldham**’s Job Characteristics Model argued this decades ago.
- Meta-analyses such as **Fried & Ferris (1987)** and especially **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** found that these job characteristics are consistently associated with:
  - higher job satisfaction
  - higher internal work motivation
  - stronger commitment
  - lower turnover intentions

Among these, **autonomy** is usually one of the strongest predictors.

### Why this matters for your question
What most people mean by “interesting work” overlaps heavily with these constructs:
- intellectually stimulating
- you learn
- you solve hard problems
- you feel agency
- the work matters

The literature is very consistent that these qualities are not nice-to-haves; they are central to whether people like their jobs.

---

## 2) Meaningful work has a strong association with wellbeing

There is also a sizable literature on **meaningful work**.

Meta-analytic work by **Allan and colleagues** (late 2010s / early 2020s) finds that people who experience their work as meaningful tend to report:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher work engagement
- higher life satisfaction
- better wellbeing
- lower intentions to quit

This is not exactly the same as “interesting,” but in knowledge work they often travel together.

### Important nuance
Meaningful work is not only “the topic is fascinating.” It can also come from:
- having impact
- mentoring others
- building a team
- ownership
- seeing the downstream effect of your decisions

So a team-lead role may gain meaningfulness even if it loses some topic-level intellectual interest.

---

## 3) Pay matters, but usually less than interesting work once pay is already good

One of the better-known findings here is that the correlation between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is positive but **not that large**.

### Meta-analysis
- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**: pay is related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is generally **small** compared with what many people intuitively expect.

Depending on exactly how it is modeled/corrected, the association is often described as roughly in the **small** range.

### What seems to matter more than raw pay
Research suggests people care a lot about:
- whether pay feels **fair**
- whether it is **competitive**
- whether it signals recognition/status

A famous field/natural-experiment paper:
- **Card, Mas, Moretti, & Saez (2012)** showed that when people learn they are paid less than relevant peers, job satisfaction drops and job search rises.

So the literature suggests:
- going from **underpaid to fair** matters a lot
- going from **already very well paid to slightly more well paid** tends to matter much less for day-to-day satisfaction

For someone already in a highly compensated technical role, a “bit higher salary” would generally be predicted to have **modest** effects on happiness unless the current pay feels unfair.

---

## 4) Intrinsic goals tend to predict better wellbeing than extrinsic goals

A related literature from **Self-Determination Theory** and values research is useful here.

### Core result
People tend to do better when their work supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

And people who orient more toward **intrinsic goals** (growth, interest, contribution, connection) tend to have better wellbeing than those primarily oriented toward **extrinsic goals** (money, status, image).

Relevant authors include:
- **Deci & Ryan**
- **Kasser & Ryan**
- **Sheldon & Elliot** (self-concordance work)

The punchline is not “money is bad.” It’s:

> If a choice aligns with what you actually care about intrinsically, it tends to produce better sustained wellbeing than a choice driven mainly by external rewards.

That is one of the more stable findings in the motivation literature.

---

## 5) Promotions and leadership roles do not reliably increase happiness as much as people expect

This part is especially relevant to your situation.

There is evidence that promotions/managerial roles bring:
- higher pay
- more status
- more influence
- often more autonomy

But also:
- more stress
- more responsibility
- more interruptions / meetings / people problems
- less time on the core technical work people originally liked

### What research generally finds
The average effect of promotion on overall wellbeing/job satisfaction is **mixed**:
- some aspects improve, especially **pay satisfaction** and status
- other aspects can worsen, especially **stress**, hours, and enjoyment of daily tasks

This is where **person-job fit** becomes central.

### Person-environment fit
A very influential meta-analysis:
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**

It found that when people fit their job/organization well, they report:
- higher job satisfaction
- stronger commitment
- lower turnover intentions

Applied to your case:

- If you have genuinely wanted to lead people for a long time, then the Meta offer is not just “more money.” It may satisfy a real intrinsic goal.
- But if what you mainly love is doing frontier research yourself, and the leadership role pulls you away from that, the fit could worsen even if the title improves.

The literature would predict that **fit with your motives** matters more than the prestige of promotion alone.

---

## 6) People often overestimate the lasting happiness effect of salary/status gains

A general wellbeing result, not limited to work, is **hedonic adaptation**:
- people often adapt to raises, status gains, and improved circumstances faster than they expect

There is also a broad **affective forecasting** literature showing that people are not great at predicting what will make them happy long-term.

Applied to jobs, this usually means:
- the novelty of a raise/title often fades
- the texture of your daily work matters persistently because you experience it every day

So if one option has more appealing daily work and the other mainly has more money/status, the literature suggests people often **underweight daily experience** and **overweight the symbolic upside**.

That does not mean promotions are bad; it means their benefits are often less durable than imagined unless the new role itself is satisfying.

---

## 7) Is there direct evidence on “regret”?

Less than you might hope.

Researchers more often measure:
- job satisfaction
- engagement
- burnout
- intention to quit
- life satisfaction

There is **much less direct evidence** on “years later, do people regret choosing the more interesting job over the better-paid one?”

The closest robust inference is:

- people tend to fare better when their work aligns with intrinsic interests and values
- people adapt to extrinsic gains
- poor fit predicts dissatisfaction and turnover

So while “regret” isn’t commonly the measured variable, the broader evidence leans toward:
- **sustained daily fit** matters a lot
- **money/status alone** are weaker long-run predictors than many people assume

---

## 8) If I compress the literature into one sentence

If both jobs already clear the “good pay / prestige / security” bar, then:

> **Long-run happiness is usually predicted more by the quality of the work itself (interest, autonomy, meaning, fit) than by a moderate increase in compensation.**

The biggest exception is:

> **If the leadership opportunity is itself intrinsically important to you**, then the Meta role may not be “less interesting” in the dimensions that matter most for wellbeing.

That is exactly where the literature on **fit** becomes decisive.

---

## 9) Practical research-based takeaway for your specific tradeoff

Without giving personal advice, the literature would suggest evaluating the offer across **intrinsic job qualities**, not just “salary vs interest.”

For example, compare the two roles on:

- **intellectual interest**
- **autonomy**
- **learning**
- **meaning/impact**
- **fit with your identity**
- **opportunity to lead/mentor**
- **time spent on the work you most enjoy**
- **fairness of compensation**
- **stress / role overload**
- **long-term optionality**

That is more faithful to the research than a simple “more money vs more interesting.”

---

## 10) Key references to look up

A short reading list:

- **Hackman, J.R., & Oldham, G.R.** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Fried, Y., & Ferris, G.R. (1987)** — meta-analysis on the Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, S.E., Nahrgang, J.D., & Morgeson, F.P. (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design characteristics
- **Judge, T.A., Piccolo, R.F., Podsakoff, N.P., Shaw, J.C., & Rich, B.L. (2010)** — pay and job satisfaction meta-analysis
- **Card, D., Mas, A., Moretti, E., & Saez, E. (2012)** — peer pay and job satisfaction
- **Deci, E.L., & Ryan, R.M.** — Self-Determination Theory
- **Kasser, T., & Ryan, R.M.** — intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations and wellbeing
- **Sheldon, K.M., & Elliot, A.J.** — self-concordance and wellbeing
- **Kristof-Brown, A.L., Zimmerman, R.D., & Johnson, E.C. (2005)** — person-environment fit meta-analysis
- **Allan, B.A., et al.** — meta-analytic work on meaningful work and wellbeing

If you want, I can turn this into a **more formal literature summary with 10–15 papers and one-sentence takeaways for each**, or a **decision table grounded in the literature rather than opinion**.
