answer:
Yes. The broad research picture is:

**Interesting / meaningful / intrinsically motivating work is usually a stronger predictor of job satisfaction than pay level is.**  
But **leadership/advancement can matter a lot too when it fits your values**, and the evidence on **regret** is much thinner and more indirect.

A good way to think about the literature is that “interesting work” is not one variable; researchers usually study it as some combination of:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **meaningfulness / purpose**
- **autonomy**
- **skill variety / challenge**
- **person-job fit / values fit**

Those variables are consistently linked to well-being at work.

## What the research says

### 1) Interesting/meaningful work is strongly associated with happiness at work
A few robust literatures point the same way:

#### Work design research
Classic job design theory says jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they have:
- **skill variety**
- **task identity**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
  Meta-analysis of the work design literature found that these kinds of job characteristics are **substantially related** to:
  - higher **job satisfaction**
  - higher **internal work motivation**
  - better **performance**
  - lower **turnover intentions**
  - less **burnout/strain**

In plain English: jobs that are more interesting, meaningful, and autonomous tend to make people like their work more.

#### Meaningful work
- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019/2021; meta-analysis)**  
  Meta-analytic work on **meaningful work** finds fairly strong associations with:
  - **job satisfaction**
  - **work engagement**
  - **life satisfaction**
  - lower **turnover intentions**
  - lower **distress/burnout**

The effect on **job satisfaction** is usually much stronger than the effect on overall life happiness, but there is some spillover to life satisfaction too.

### 2) Pay matters, but its link to job satisfaction is surprisingly modest
One of the clearest results in organizational psychology is that **pay level and job satisfaction are positively related, but not strongly**.

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, meta-analysis  
  The corrected correlation between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is only around **.15**.

That is: more pay does predict more satisfaction, but the relationship is **much weaker** than many people intuitively expect.

This doesn’t mean money is unimportant. It means:
- once basic needs / security are covered,
- and especially for already well-paid knowledge workers,
- **the daily nature of the work** often matters more for how satisfying the job feels.

### 3) Income does matter for well-being, but with diminishing returns
In the broader happiness literature:

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** found that higher income strongly improves **life evaluation**, while **emotional well-being** rose less and appeared to flatten above a threshold.
- **Killingsworth (2021)** challenged the flattening result and found experienced well-being continuing to rise with income, though roughly **log-linearly**—which still implies **diminishing marginal returns**.

So a salary bump does matter. But the gain from going from “comfortable” to “somewhat more comfortable” is generally smaller than people predict, especially compared with changing what you do every day.

### 4) Fit often matters as much as, or more than, either “interest” or “status”
A key result that’s very relevant to your situation:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, *Personnel Psychology*  
  Meta-analysis on **person-environment fit** found that when people’s jobs and organizations fit their values, preferences, and identity, they report:
  - higher **job satisfaction**
  - higher **commitment**
  - lower **turnover intention**

This matters because “leading a small team” is not just an extrinsic perk. If leadership is something you’ve genuinely wanted for a long time, then that can be part of **values fit** or **identity fit**, which has real predictive power for satisfaction.

So the research is **not** simply “interesting work beats everything.”  
It’s closer to:

> People do best when their work is intrinsically engaging **and** aligned with what they value and want to become.

### 5) Intrinsic motives tend to produce better well-being than extrinsic motives
Related literature from self-determination theory:

- Work that supports **autonomy**, **competence**, and **relatedness** tends to improve well-being and motivation.
- Pursuing more **intrinsic goals** tends to be associated with better well-being than pursuing mainly **extrinsic goals** like money or status.

This is a well-established theme in work motivation research and self-determination theory.

A relevant nuance for your case:
- **salary bump** is mostly extrinsic
- **team leadership** could be extrinsic if it’s mostly title/status, **or intrinsic** if it’s about growth, mentorship, agency, and impact
- **interesting research** is usually strongly intrinsic

So the Meta offer is not one-dimensional in research terms.

## What about regret?
This part of the evidence is weaker.

There is **not**, as far as I know, a clean body of research saying:
> “People who stay in the more interesting job regret it less than people who take the higher-paying leadership job.”

That exact comparison is not well studied.

What we do have:

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: over the long run, people tend to regret **inactions** more than actions.
- **Roese & Summerville (2005)**: education/career are among the most common domains of regret.

But that does **not** directly tell you which option will be regretted more. It depends on what feels like the foregone identity:
- “I didn’t pursue the chance to lead”
vs
- “I left unusually interesting work for a less interesting role”

Research on regret is more useful here as a warning that **identity-relevant missed opportunities** can loom large later.

## What seems most supported overall

If I compress the literature into a few evidence-based statements:

1. **Interesting / meaningful / autonomous work strongly predicts job satisfaction.**
2. **Pay increases predict satisfaction too, but much less strongly than most people think.**
3. **Values fit matters a lot.**  
   If leadership is a core aspiration, that can genuinely increase later satisfaction.
4. **Management roles are mixed.**  
   They often bring more autonomy/status/impact, but also more meetings, conflict, admin load, and less time on the work itself.
5. **Direct regret evidence is sparse.**  
   The strongest regret result is about not pursuing identity-defining opportunities, not about “interestingness” per se.

## If you want the most research-grounded way to think about your case
The literature suggests asking not “Which has the higher headline upside?” but:

- **How much of my week would be spent on activities I find intrinsically engaging?**
- **How important is leadership to my identity, not just my ego?**
- **Would managing people satisfy autonomy/competence/impact needs, or mainly crowd out the work I enjoy?**
- **Is the extra pay materially important for my life, or mostly symbolic?**
- **Which role is a better fit for who I want to become over the next 3–5 years?**

That framing is very consistent with the actual research.

If you want, I can give you:
1. a **short reading list** of the most relevant papers/meta-analyses, or  
2. a **strictly research-based decision framework** for comparing these two offers.

answer:
Yes—there is quite a lot of research relevant to this, though not many studies on your exact choice of “more interesting IC research role vs. better-paid small-team lead role.”

### Short version

The broad finding from organizational psychology is:

1. **Intrinsic job characteristics**—how interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and growth-promoting the work is—are **strong predictors of job satisfaction and well-being**.
2. **Pay matters**, but its relationship to job satisfaction is **much weaker** than many people assume, especially once pay is already high enough that both options are financially comfortable.
3. **Management/leadership roles are not uniformly better or worse**. They tend to help if they fit what the person genuinely wants, because **person-job fit** is a strong predictor of satisfaction.
4. On **regret specifically**, the evidence is thinner. But I’m not aware of strong evidence that people systematically regret choosing more interesting work over somewhat higher pay. If anything, broader regret research leans toward regretting not pursuing more meaningful/self-concordant paths.

## What the research says

### 1) Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work strongly predicts job satisfaction

A lot of the classic and modern evidence converges here.

#### Work design / job characteristics
The “job characteristics” literature finds that jobs with more:
- **autonomy**
- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- often also **learning and complexity**

tend to produce more:
- job satisfaction
- intrinsic motivation
- engagement
- lower burnout

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
  Meta-analysis of work design research.  
  Main result: motivational job characteristics like autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback had **moderate-to-strong associations** with job satisfaction and internal motivation.

The key practical point is that “interesting work” is not a fluffy factor in the literature—it maps to constructs that reliably predict satisfaction.

#### Meaningful work
Another strong line of evidence is on **meaningful work**.

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis  
  Found that meaningful work is associated with:
  - **higher job satisfaction**
  - **higher life satisfaction**
  - **more engagement**
  - **less psychological distress / burnout**

The association with **job satisfaction** is typically much larger than the association between **pay level** and job satisfaction.

## 2) Pay matters, but less than people often expect

There is real evidence here, and it’s pretty consistent.

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, *Journal of Vocational Behavior*  
  Meta-analysis on **pay and job satisfaction**.  
  The headline result is that the correlation is **positive but modest**—roughly in the **small** range.

That means:
- higher pay does predict higher job satisfaction **on average**
- but it is **not one of the strongest predictors**
- the effect is much smaller than many intrinsic job features

Also, two nuances show up repeatedly in this literature:

### a) Relative pay and fairness matter more than raw pay
People care a lot about:
- whether pay feels fair
- whether they feel underpaid relative to peers
- whether the pay change materially changes their life

A “bit higher salary” usually has less psychological impact than a large change in financial security.

### b) Marginal gains from income diminish
In well-being research more broadly, extra income usually helps more when it changes:
- security
- stress about expenses
- freedom from constraints

Once both jobs are already in a very high-income range, the incremental happiness gain from extra compensation is generally smaller than at lower income levels.

So if both roles already clear your “comfortable and secure” threshold, the literature would generally predict that **daily work quality** matters more than a moderate compensation delta.

## 3) Person-job fit is one of the biggest predictors

This may be especially relevant for your case, because “leading a small team” is not just a status change; it may be a genuine goal.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, *Personnel Psychology*  
  Meta-analysis on **fit at work**.  
  Found that **person-job fit** has a **strong relationship** with job satisfaction and related outcomes.

This is important because your choice is not simply:
- intrinsic work vs extrinsic reward

It may be:
- **more interesting technical content**
vs.
- **a role that fits a valued identity shift** (becoming a leader/manager)

If leading people is something you truly want—not just because it looks good or pays more—then that is **also an intrinsic factor**, not merely an extrinsic one.

In other words, research would not automatically code the Meta option as “less meaningful.” If management is a deeply held aspiration, the fit literature says that matters a lot.

## 4) Self-determination theory: intrinsic motivation tends to support better well-being

A huge body of research under **Self-Determination Theory (SDT)** says that well-being is supported when work satisfies needs for:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

And people do better when they pursue goals for **self-concordant** reasons rather than external ones.

Key review:
- **Deci, Olafsen, & Ryan (2017)**, *Annual Review of Organizational Psychology and Organizational Behavior*

Implication:
- If “interesting research” gives you autonomy and competence, that strongly supports well-being.
- If “leading a small team” fulfills a long-standing, authentic goal and increases autonomy/impact/relatedness, that can also strongly support well-being.
- If the management role is mostly attractive because of title/pay/prestige, SDT would predict a weaker well-being payoff.

So the research doesn’t say “always choose the more interesting content.” It says:
> People tend to do better when their work is intrinsically rewarding and aligned with their psychological needs and values.

## 5) Leadership/management roles: benefits are real, but so are the costs

The evidence here is more mixed.

Leadership roles often come with:
- more autonomy
- more status
- more impact
- more pay

But also:
- more interruptions
- more emotional labor
- more conflict management
- less hands-on technical work
- more administrative load

The most relevant general framework is the **job demands-control** literature:
- high demands are less harmful when accompanied by high control/autonomy
- high demands + low control are much worse

So management can be satisfying if:
- you actually want to do it
- you’ll have real discretion
- the organization supports managers well
- you won’t be trapped in low-agency coordination work

I don’t know of strong evidence that “becoming a manager” is uniformly good for happiness. The literature is more like:
> It helps some people a lot, especially when it matches their motives and gives them real control; it hurts others when it takes them away from the work they actually enjoy.

## 6) On regret: evidence is weaker, but broader regret research is suggestive

This is the least cleanly answered part of your question.

I don’t know of a strong body of studies showing:
- people who chose more interesting work later regretted it more than people who chose higher-paying work
or the reverse.

What we do have is broader regret research:

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: over the long run, people often regret **inactions** more than actions.
- **Roese & Summerville (2005)**: biggest regrets tend to cluster around domains like education, career, and relationships—often involving missed opportunities tied to the “ideal self.”

That doesn’t directly prove “choose interesting work,” but it does suggest that long-term regret often centers on **not pursuing what felt meaningful or identity-relevant**, rather than on narrowly maximizing external rewards.

So the most evidence-based thing I can say is:
- **there is strong evidence on satisfaction and well-being**
- **much less direct evidence on regret**
- what regret evidence exists is at least **compatible with** the idea that people later regret not pursuing work that felt more authentic or meaningful

## If I had to summarize the literature in one sentence

For sustained job satisfaction, **interesting/meaningful/autonomous work and person-role fit usually matter more than a modest pay increase**; but if leading a team is a deeply valued goal rather than a prestige move, that leadership opportunity may itself count as an intrinsic source of satisfaction.

## A research-based way to think about your specific tradeoff

You can map your options onto the variables the literature actually cares about:

### Likely stronger predictors of future satisfaction
- How **interesting** are the day-to-day tasks?
- How much **autonomy** will you have?
- How much **learning/mastery** is there?
- How much **meaning/impact** do you feel?
- How strong is the **fit** with the kind of person you want to become?
- Will the role support or erode **energy/burnout**?

### Usually weaker predictors than people think
- Moderate salary differences, especially at already high income levels
- Prestige/status alone

### Ambiguous and highly person-dependent
- Moving into management
- Scope/leadership
- Amount of coordination vs. hands-on work

## Bottom-line answer to your direct question

If by “interesting” you mean work that is:
- intrinsically engaging
- meaningful
- autonomous
- growth-promoting

then **yes, people in those jobs tend, on average, to be more satisfied and often happier at work** than people in less interesting jobs with somewhat better extrinsic features.

The best evidence says that **these intrinsic characteristics matter more for job satisfaction than pay level does**.

But your case has one twist:
- **leading a small team** may itself be intrinsically valuable to you.

So the research does **not** reduce your choice to “interesting work vs money.”
It may really be:
- **interesting research**
vs.
- **interesting leadership identity**

And the literature says that whichever option better matches your authentic motivations and daily experience is more likely to produce sustained satisfaction.

If you want, I can turn this into a **1-page evidence-based decision matrix** using the main research constructs: autonomy, meaning, competence, fit, pay, leadership aspiration, and burnout risk.

answer:
Yes—there is quite a lot of research on this, and the broad result is fairly consistent:

**Once pay is already “good enough,” the intrinsic qualities of the job—interestingness, autonomy, skill use, meaning, fit—usually matter more for long-run job satisfaction than a modest pay increase.**

That is not a philosophical claim; it’s pretty close to the center of the empirical literature on job satisfaction and well-being.

## What the research most reliably says

### 1) Interesting / meaningful / autonomous work is a strong predictor of job satisfaction
A large body of work in organizational psychology finds that jobs are more satisfying when they offer:

- **skill variety / challenge**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance / meaning**
- **feedback / learning**
- **good social environment**

This goes back to the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham) and was supported in later meta-analyses.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**: work design characteristics such as autonomy, task variety, significance, and feedback show **moderate to strong relationships** with job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and lower strain/turnover.

So if by “interesting” you mean “intellectually engaging, varied, challenging, meaningful,” the literature says that kind of work tends to be materially better for well-being at work.

---

### 2) Pay matters, but the link from pay level to job satisfaction is surprisingly small
The classic meta-analytic result here is:

- **Judge et al. (2010)**: the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but small**—roughly **r ≈ .15**.

That’s an important result. It does **not** mean money is irrelevant. It means that, statistically, salary level by itself explains relatively little of the difference in how satisfied people are with their jobs.

Two nuances:
- **Pay fairness** and **pay satisfaction** matter more than raw dollars.
- Money matters much more when it changes **security**, **stress**, or **life options** than when it is just a modest increment on top of an already high salary.

For someone already in a well-paid research role, a “bit higher salary” would generally be expected to have a smaller effect on happiness than daily job content.

---

### 3) Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being
There is also a distinct literature on **meaningful work**.

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** meta-analysis: meaningful work is associated with **higher job satisfaction**, **higher work engagement**, **greater well-being**, and **lower turnover intentions**.

The effect sizes in that literature are generally **much larger** than the pay-level effect above.

So if “Anthropic is much more interesting” also means “this work feels more meaningful / aligned / identity-relevant,” the evidence points pretty strongly toward that mattering a lot.

---

### 4) Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, relatedness are central
A related research tradition is **Self-Determination Theory**.

The recurring finding is that people do better when work supports:
- **autonomy**: “I have meaningful control”
- **competence**: “I’m using and growing my abilities”
- **relatedness**: “I feel connected/respected”

Reviews and meta-analytic work in this area consistently show that satisfaction of these needs predicts:
- greater well-being
- stronger motivation
- better performance
- lower burnout / turnover

For research jobs, **autonomy + competence** are especially salient. Highly interesting technical work often scores well on both.

---

## What about regret?

This part is less directly studied than job satisfaction, but there are relevant findings.

### 5) Long-term regret tends to track “ideal-self” missed opportunities
A useful line of regret research finds that people’s most enduring regrets are often about not becoming the person they hoped to become.

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the long run, people tend to regret **inactions** more than actions.
- **Davidai & Gilovich (2018)**: long-term regrets are often tied to failures to pursue the **ideal self** rather than failures of duty.

That does **not** mean “always take the new opportunity.” It means the regret question is often less about pay and more about identity:
- “Am I the kind of person who really wanted to lead?”
- “Am I the kind of person who really wanted to do frontier research?”
- “Which path feels more central to the person I’m trying to become?”

So the regret literature does **not** give a simple “interesting work wins” rule. It says regret often comes from passing up opportunities that are tightly connected to one’s core aspirations.

In your case, that means the leadership piece may matter more than the salary bump.

---

## What about becoming a manager / team lead?

Research here is more mixed.

The evidence does **not** show that promotions or managerial roles reliably make people happier in the long run. They often bring:
- more status
- more pay
- more autonomy in some respects

but also:
- more stress
- more administrative load
- more responsibility for others
- less time on the work that originally felt intrinsically rewarding

The general pattern in the literature is:
- management is good **if it fits the person**
- not everyone who wants advancement is happier as a manager
- promotion gains often partly **adapt away**

So the strongest research-based reason to prefer Meta would not be “higher salary,” but rather:
- if **leading a team** is a deep, identity-level goal
- and you think you would genuinely like the **day-to-day reality** of management, not just the title

---

## If you want a blunt summary of the evidence

For someone already in a highly paid, high-status role:

- **Marginal salary increases** usually buy **less happiness** than people expect.
- **Interesting, meaningful, autonomy-rich work** tends to predict **substantially more job satisfaction**.
- **Regret** is more likely to come from violating a core aspiration than from forgoing a modest pay bump.
- Therefore, in your exact tradeoff, the research would usually put the real comparison as:

**interesting work now**  
vs  
**identity-relevant leadership opportunity**

—not interesting work vs money.

---

## Best-known references to look up

If you want papers/reviews rather than summaries, these are good anchors:

1. **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
   *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*  
   Main result: pay level has a **small** relationship with job satisfaction.

2. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
   *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*  
   Main result: autonomy, task variety, significance, feedback, etc. matter a lot for satisfaction and motivation.

3. **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**  
   *Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.*  
   Main result: meaningful work is strongly linked to satisfaction, engagement, and lower turnover.

4. **Gagné & Deci (2005)** and later SDT-at-work reviews/meta-analyses  
   Main result: autonomy, competence, relatedness at work predict well-being and motivation.

5. **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**  
   Main result: in the long run, people regret **inactions** more than actions.

6. **Davidai & Gilovich (2018)**  
   *The ideal road not taken*  
   Main result: enduring regrets are often about not pursuing the “ideal self.”

---

## Bottom line
If you want the most research-backed answer in one sentence:

**For people who are already well compensated, the literature generally predicts that more interesting/meaningful/autonomous work leads to greater sustained job satisfaction than a modest increase in pay—but a genuine aspiration to lead can matter a lot for long-term regret, because regret is often about missed identity-defining opportunities rather than money.**

If you want, I can turn this into a **short annotated bibliography** with 8–10 papers and one-line takeaways for each.

answer:
Yes. The research literature is fairly consistent on one big point:

**the day-to-day quality of the work itself**—especially whether it is interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and a good fit for your strengths—tends to predict job satisfaction and well-being **more strongly than salary level alone**, once pay is already good.

A few key results:

## 1) “Interesting/meaningful work” is a strong predictor of satisfaction

In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually studied indirectly through constructs like:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **meaningful work**
- **job design**: autonomy, skill variety, task significance, feedback
- **person-job fit**

These variables show **moderate to strong** associations with:

- job satisfaction
- engagement
- lower burnout
- lower turnover intentions
- sometimes life satisfaction

### Representative findings
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, a large meta-analysis on work design, found that jobs with more **autonomy, task variety, significance, and feedback** are strongly associated with higher **job satisfaction** and internal motivation.
- **Allan et al. (2019)**, meta-analysis on **meaningful work**, found that meaningful work is positively related to **job satisfaction, work engagement, organizational commitment, and life satisfaction**, and negatively related to turnover intentions.
- **Kristof-Brown et al. (2005)**, meta-analysis on **person-environment fit**, found that **person-job fit** is a robust predictor of job satisfaction and intent to stay.

So if by “interesting” you mean: *I genuinely enjoy the work, it uses my abilities, I learn, I care about the problems, and the work feels significant*—then yes, research says that matters a lot.

---

## 2) Salary matters, but usually less than people expect for job satisfaction

The strongest result here is:

- **Judge et al. (2010)** meta-analysis: the correlation between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**—around **.15**.  
  In plain English: higher pay helps, but it is far from the main determinant of whether people like their jobs.

That fits a broader pattern from labor economics and well-being research:

- Money matters a lot when it changes **financial security** or reduces strain.
- But among already highly paid professionals, the **marginal effect of “a bit more salary”** on day-to-day well-being is usually smaller than the effect of job content, autonomy, fit, and relationships.

A related nuance:
- **pay satisfaction** and **perceived fairness** often matter more than raw pay level.

So a “bit higher salary” is not nothing, but research would not usually expect it to compensate fully for a substantial drop in interest.

---

## 3) Promotions/leadership tend to help some people—but the average effect is mixed and fit-dependent

There is some evidence that promotions improve job satisfaction:

- **Kosteas (2011)** found that receiving a promotion is associated with higher job satisfaction.

But this does **not** mean “promotion beats interesting work” in general.

Why not?

Because a promotion often changes multiple things at once:
- more status
- more pay
- more autonomy
- but also more stress, meetings, coordination load, responsibility, and less hands-on work

So the effect depends heavily on whether the new role fits what the person actually values.

For someone who truly wants to lead, management may be intrinsically rewarding.  
For someone who mostly likes doing the core technical/research work, moving into management can reduce satisfaction even if it looks like career progress.

This is where **person-job fit** research matters most: the best predictor is often not “individual contributor vs manager” in the abstract, but whether the role matches your motives and strengths.

---

## 4) Intrinsic goals tend to predict better well-being than extrinsic goals

A related literature distinguishes **intrinsic** vs **extrinsic** aspirations:

- **Intrinsic**: growth, learning, interest, mastery, contribution, relationships
- **Extrinsic**: money, status, image

Research from **Self-Determination Theory** and related work (e.g. **Kasser & Ryan**; **Gagné & Deci**) generally finds that prioritizing intrinsic motives is associated with better well-being than prioritizing extrinsic ones.

That does **not** mean money/status are bad. It means that when people organize major decisions mainly around extrinsic rewards, they tend to do worse on well-being than when the choice also supports autonomy, competence, and meaning.

This is especially relevant in your case because **“leading a small team” may itself be intrinsic**, not merely extrinsic. If leadership is something you’ve “always wanted,” then this is not just salary/title. It may satisfy:
- autonomy
- mastery
- impact
- meaning

So the research would treat that differently from “higher pay but worse work.”

---

## 5) On “regret”: there’s much less direct evidence

Here the literature is thinner.

I’m not aware of strong research showing, in a clean causal way, that people who choose the more interesting job later regret it more or less than those who choose the better-paid/promoted role.

What the evidence **does** show is:

- people in jobs with more meaning/autonomy/fit are generally more satisfied and less likely to want to leave;
- people who prioritize intrinsic over extrinsic goals tend to report better well-being;
- people often **overestimate** how much lasting happiness they will get from salary/status changes relative to daily lived experience of the work.

So the regret evidence is indirect rather than decisive.

---

# What the literature would imply for your specific tradeoff

If I translate the research into your case, the evidence suggests:

### If the Meta role is:
- somewhat more money,
- more managerial responsibility,
- but clearly **less interesting day-to-day**,

then the literature would generally predict that the loss in interest/meaning could easily outweigh the gain from pay alone.

### But if the Meta role gives you:
- a real chance to do something you deeply value (**leading a team**),
- more autonomy/influence,
- a stronger sense of growth or impact,

then it becomes a **fit question**, not simply “interesting work vs money.”  
And the research says fit can be extremely important.

So empirically, the right comparison is probably not:

- **interesting research** vs **more pay**

but rather:

- **interesting research / strong technical fit**
vs
- **leadership aspiration / greater influence / different kind of intrinsic reward**

That is a much harder and more individualized tradeoff.

---

# The cleanest research-based takeaway

If you want the shortest evidence-based summary:

1. **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.**
2. **Salary level has a positive but relatively small relationship with job satisfaction**, especially once pay is already high.
3. **Promotions and leadership roles help mainly when they fit the person.**
4. There is **less direct evidence on regret**, but the broader literature suggests people often undervalue the importance of the work itself relative to money/status.

So: **yes, people in more interesting jobs do tend, on average, to be happier with their work than people who simply earn a bit more.**  
But if **leading people is itself one of the most interesting/meaningful things for you**, then the evidence does not clearly favor staying.

---

# Good papers/reviews to look at

If you want a reading list, these are good starting points:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** — *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*
- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** — *Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.*
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.*
- **Kosteas (2011)** — *Job satisfaction and promotions.*
- **Gagné & Deci (2005)** — *Self-determination theory and work motivation.*
- **Kasser & Ryan** (1990s) — intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations and well-being

If you want, I can turn this into a **more rigorous literature summary with exact effect sizes and a simple decision matrix** tailored to your choice:
- technical-interest value
- leadership aspiration
- pay difference
- option value for future roles
- predicted effect on well-being based on the literature.

For instance, scientists often accept significant pay cuts for the freedom to publish and engage in groundbreaking research. I could offer to provide actual papers if needed, suggesting, "If you want, I can provide a more formal reading list with DOIs." It’s important to clarify that the studies are mostly observational or meta-analytic and not causal, although the evidence seems to converge. I want to keep my response concise yet informative.
answer:
Yes — there is a decent amount of research relevant to this, though not usually framed as “interesting job vs slightly higher pay + team leadership.” The literature breaks it into things like:

- intrinsic motivation / interesting work
- meaningful work
- autonomy
- person–job fit
- pay
- promotion / managerial responsibility

## Short answer

The strongest overall finding is:

**People tend to be more satisfied and psychologically better off in jobs that are intrinsically interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and a good fit for them.**  
**Pay matters too, but its relationship with job satisfaction is usually much smaller than those intrinsic/job-design factors.**

On **regret specifically**, the evidence is thinner. I don’t know of a clean body of research showing “people who chose the more interesting job later regretted it less” in exactly your kind of decision. But the indirect evidence points in that direction: **intrinsic qualities of work predict sustained satisfaction more reliably than modest pay differences do.**

---

## 1) “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

A big literature in organizational psychology goes back to the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses.

### Main result
Jobs tend to produce higher motivation and satisfaction when they have more:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- often also **learning/challenge**

A strong summary is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, a major meta-analysis of work design research.

### What they found
Work design characteristics — especially **autonomy**, **feedback**, and other “motivating” features — show **moderate to strong associations** with:

- job satisfaction
- internal motivation
- engagement
- lower strain/burnout

In practice, the effect sizes for these factors are typically **larger than the effect of pay on job satisfaction**.

### Interpretation for your question
If by “interesting” you mean “the work itself is intellectually engaging and absorbing,” the literature says that is **not a minor luxury**. It is one of the main drivers of whether people actually like their jobs.

---

## 2) Meaningful work is also strongly linked to well-being

Another important literature is on **meaningful work**.

A good synthesis is:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis on meaningful work.

### Main result
People who experience their work as meaningful tend to report:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher life satisfaction
- greater well-being
- stronger commitment
- lower turnover intentions
- less distress/burnout

These are not tiny effects.

### Important nuance
“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical. A job can be interesting but not meaningful, or meaningful but not very interesting. But in elite technical/research roles they often overlap: novelty, learning, impact, and identity can all contribute.

---

## 3) Autonomy matters a lot — often more than people expect

Self-determination theory is very relevant here.

The basic idea: people function better when work supports:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

At work, autonomy and competence are especially relevant to research/engineering jobs.

Meta-analytic reviews of this literature find that satisfaction of those needs is strongly linked to:

- job satisfaction
- engagement
- lower burnout
- general well-being

So if your current role lets you do work you find deeply absorbing, and with intellectual freedom, that combination has strong empirical support as a source of well-being.

---

## 4) Pay matters — but usually less than people think for job satisfaction

There is good evidence here too.

A key paper is:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction.

### Main result
The relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**.  
The correlation is generally remembered as being around **small-to-moderate, closer to small** than most people intuitively expect.

In plain English:

- Higher pay does predict somewhat greater satisfaction.
- But it is **far from the dominant factor**.
- People often overestimate how much marginal pay increases will improve how they feel about work.

This is especially true when you compare it to things like:

- whether the work is engaging
- whether you have autonomy
- whether it fits your strengths/identity
- whether your manager/team context is good

### Broader well-being research on income
Outside job-specific research, subjective well-being research generally finds:

- money helps, especially by reducing insecurity
- but it shows **diminishing marginal returns**
- and daily experience is heavily shaped by how you spend your time, not just what you earn

So for someone already well-paid, a **“bit higher salary”** is less likely to dominate than a big difference in how stimulating the work feels day to day.

---

## 5) People often accept lower pay for more interesting/meaningful work

Economists study this as **compensating differentials** and **nonpecuniary job amenities**.

A very relevant paper for someone in research is:

- **Stern (2004), “Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?”**

### Main result
Scientists appear willing to accept **lower pay** in exchange for work with more scientific/intellectual value — e.g. ability to do real research, publish, work on frontier problems.

This doesn’t prove they are happier afterward, but it does show that in revealed-preference terms, people often value intellectually meaningful work enough to trade away money for it.

That’s important because it suggests this is not just rhetoric: the labor market behavior is consistent with people placing substantial value on interesting work.

---

## 6) Promotions / leadership are mixed for well-being

This is the part most people discuss too loosely.

Becoming a manager or team lead can increase:

- status
- pay
- influence
- autonomy of one sort

But it often also increases:

- responsibility
- interruptions
- people problems
- administrative load
- stress
- time fragmentation
- less time doing the core work you previously enjoyed

### What research generally finds
Panel studies on promotion tend to show:

- **satisfaction with pay/status often goes up**
- but **stress and workload also tend to go up**
- the effect on overall well-being is **mixed**
- the positive effects are often smaller or less durable than people expect

So “I always wanted to lead a small team” could matter a lot **if leadership itself is intrinsically meaningful to you**.  
But if team leadership is mostly attractive as a milestone/status marker, the literature does **not** support assuming it will make you happier overall.

That distinction is important.

---

## 7) Person–job fit is one of the best predictors of long-run satisfaction

Another relevant meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** on person–job/person–organization fit

### Main result
Better fit predicts:

- higher job satisfaction
- stronger commitment
- lower turnover intention
- better well-being outcomes

This is very relevant because your question is not “what do people in general prefer?” but “what should *I* do?”

If a large part of your identity and energy comes from working on genuinely interesting frontier problems, then a job that is “objectively good” but **poorly matched to that preference** may reduce satisfaction substantially.

In other words, the literature says:
**preferences are not noise here — they are part of the causal story.**

---

## 8) What about regret?

This is the weakest-evidence part of your question.

I’m not aware of a strong, canonical literature that cleanly answers:

> “Do people who stay in more interesting jobs later regret it less than those who choose higher pay/leadership?”

What we do have are indirect findings:

### A. Intrinsic vs extrinsic goals
Work in self-determination theory and adjacent psychology, especially **Kasser & Ryan**, finds that prioritizing **intrinsic goals** (growth, connection, meaning) over **extrinsic goals** (money, status, image) is generally associated with better well-being.

That is not identical to a job-choice regret study, but it points in a consistent direction.

### B. Hedonic adaptation
People often adapt relatively quickly to salary/status improvements.  
They do **not** adapt as fully to the day-to-day texture of work, because that texture is constantly re-experienced.

That implies that choosing a job with worse daily experience for a modest external gain may be more regrettable than people predict.

### C. Counterfactuals in career regret
Career-regret research often finds that regret is linked less to “I didn’t maximize comp” and more to:
- lack of fit
- underuse of talents
- loss of meaning
- not pursuing valued growth

This literature is less clean than the others, so I would treat it as suggestive rather than decisive.

---

# Bottom-line research conclusion

If I compress the literature as faithfully as possible:

1. **Interesting, autonomous, meaningful work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and sustained work well-being.**
2. **Pay has a positive but comparatively modest effect on job satisfaction**, especially when comparing already-good jobs.
3. **Leadership/promotion has mixed effects**: more status/influence, but also more stress and less hands-on interesting work.
4. **Person–job fit matters a lot**: if intellectual interest is central for you, sacrificing it can be costly.
5. On **regret**, direct evidence is limited, but the broader evidence suggests that people often underweight the long-run importance of day-to-day work quality and overweight money/status gains.

So, purely from the research literature, the default prediction would be:

- If the new role is **substantially less interesting**, and the salary increase is only **modest**, then **staying in the more interesting role is more likely to maximize satisfaction/well-being**.
- The main exception is if **leading a small team is itself a major intrinsic source of meaning/growth for you**, not just a credential.

That last clause matters because “team leadership” can be either:
- an extrinsic/status move, or
- an intrinsically valued form of work.

Research predicts very different outcomes depending on which it is.

---

## If you want a compact reading list

These are good anchors:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — work design meta-analysis  
- **Judge et al. (2010)** — pay and job satisfaction meta-analysis  
- **Allan et al. (2019)** — meaningful work meta-analysis  
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — person-environment fit meta-analysis  
- **Stern (2004)** — willingness to trade pay for research-oriented work  
- **Kasser & Ryan (1993, 1996)** — intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations and well-being

If you want, I can turn this into either:

1. a **more academic summary with citations and findings**, or  
2. a **decision matrix grounded in the literature** for your exact choice.

answer:
Yes. The broad research answer is:

**For people who are already well-paid enough that money is not relieving major financial stress, the intrinsic quality of the work usually predicts sustained job satisfaction and well-being more strongly than pay does.**  
By “intrinsic quality,” the literature usually means things like **interest, autonomy, challenge, learning, task significance/meaning, and fit with your values and strengths**.

A few of the strongest findings:

## 1) “Interesting” work is strongly linked to job satisfaction

A very established line of research is the **job characteristics / work design** literature.

- **Hackman & Oldham** originally argued that jobs are better when they have:
  - **skill variety**
  - **task identity**
  - **task significance**
  - **autonomy**
  - **feedback**

- A major meta-analysis:
  - **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
    found that these work-design features had **substantial associations** with outcomes like:
    - job satisfaction
    - internal motivation
    - engagement
    - lower burnout

The practical takeaway from that whole literature is that **jobs that are more interesting/challenging/autonomous tend to make people more satisfied with their work**, and this effect is not small.

## 2) Person-job fit is a very strong predictor

Another strong result:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** meta-analysis on **person-environment fit**  
  found that **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** are strongly related to:
  - job satisfaction
  - organizational commitment
  - lower intention to quit

This matters for your case because “interesting” often really means:
- “this matches what I naturally care about”
- “this uses my strengths”
- “this kind of work energizes me”

That kind of fit is one of the better predictors we have of long-run satisfaction.

## 3) Meaningful work is also strongly associated with well-being

There is also a large literature on **meaningful work**.

Across studies, people who experience their work as meaningful tend to report:
- higher job satisfaction
- higher engagement
- better overall well-being
- less burnout / less psychological distress

Meta-analytic work by **Allan and colleagues** and related reviews generally finds **medium-to-strong relationships**, not tiny ones.

So if your current role is “much more interesting,” that likely overlaps with **meaning**, **curiosity**, and **intrinsic motivation**, all of which usually help.

## 4) Pay matters — but less than many people expect

The classic result here is:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** meta-analysis on **pay and job satisfaction**  
  found that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but fairly small**.

This is one of the most robust findings in the area.  
In plain English:

- More money does improve things.
- But **how much you like the work itself** usually matters more for job satisfaction than salary differences do.

That does **not** mean salary is unimportant. It matters a lot when:
- you’re under financial stress
- the compensation gap is large
- compensation changes materially affect your life options / security / family plans

But when comparing **two already-high-paying jobs**, the literature usually suggests that **day-to-day work quality dominates modest pay differences**.

## 5) Intrinsic motives usually beat extrinsic motives for well-being

This comes from **self-determination theory** and related work.

Research by **Deci & Ryan**, **Kasser & Ryan**, and many later studies suggests that:
- **intrinsic goals** (growth, mastery, relationships, contribution, meaning)
  are more reliably associated with well-being
than
- **extrinsic goals** (money, status, image)

At work, this usually shows up as:
- autonomy
- competence/mastery
- relatedness
- meaningful contribution

predicting better well-being than status or money alone.

So the general research pattern is:  
**if you choose more money/status at the cost of intrinsic engagement, that often does not maximize happiness.**

## 6) What about leading a team?

This is the important complication in your case.

You said leading a small team is “something I always wanted to do.”

From a research perspective, that means the Meta option is **not just an extrinsic reward**. It may satisfy intrinsic needs too:
- autonomy
- growth
- identity
- mastery of a new role
- status/recognition
- perhaps meaning through helping others develop

There is some evidence that **promotions** improve job satisfaction, at least partly beyond the salary increase.

- **Kosteas (2011)** and related panel-work suggest promotions tend to raise job satisfaction, not just pay satisfaction.

But the effect is mixed because management can also bring:
- more stress
- more meetings
- less maker time
- less direct contact with the technical work itself

So the research would not frame your choice as simply:
- interesting work vs money

It’s more like:
- **technical intrinsic interest**
versus
- **leadership/autonomy/growth interest**

That distinction matters.

## 7) Do people regret choosing the more interesting job less?

Here the evidence is weaker.

I don’t know of a famous clean study that directly asks:

> “People who choose the more interesting job over the better-paid, higher-status job—do they regret it less 10 years later?”

There is much less direct research on that exact question.

What we *do* have is indirect evidence:

- poor fit with one’s interests/values predicts:
  - lower satisfaction
  - more burnout
  - more turnover intentions
- intrinsic motivation and meaningful work predict:
  - higher engagement
  - persistence
  - better psychological outcomes

So while the “regret” evidence is not as direct, the broader literature points the same way:
**choosing against your intrinsic interests tends to be risky for long-run satisfaction.**

## If I compress the literature into one sentence

For already-successful professionals, **the strongest predictors of lasting job happiness are usually autonomy, interest, meaning, and fit — not salary — unless the money materially changes your life or the new role satisfies a major intrinsic goal of its own.**

## A research-based way to evaluate your specific choice

You could score each option on these dimensions, because these are the variables the literature cares about:

1. **Intrinsic interest**  
   How much do I want to do the actual day-to-day work?

2. **Autonomy**  
   How much control will I have?

3. **Learning / mastery**  
   Which role will help me grow in ways I value?

4. **Meaning / significance**  
   Which work feels more important to me?

5. **Person-role fit**  
   Am I fundamentally more of an individual contributor in this phase, or am I ready to derive satisfaction from management?

6. **Basic psychological needs**  
   Which role better satisfies:
   - autonomy
   - competence
   - relatedness

7. **Financial/security impact**  
   Is the salary gap actually life-changing, or just somewhat better?

8. **Leadership as an intrinsic value**  
   Do I want management because it is genuinely energizing, or because it feels like “the next step”?

That last question is especially important.  
If leading a team is deeply meaningful to you, then the Meta role may still be “intrinsically motivating,” just in a different dimension than technical interest.

## Bottom line from the research

- **Yes**: more interesting / intrinsically rewarding work is generally associated with greater happiness at work.
- **Yes**: these effects are often **stronger than pay effects**.
- **No strong evidence** says people systematically regret choosing interesting work over somewhat higher pay.
- **But** the leadership component changes the equation, because leadership can itself be intrinsically rewarding and can increase satisfaction if it fits you.

If you want, I can give you a **short reading list of the best papers/meta-analyses** on this question, with 1–2 sentence summaries of each.

**Examining job promotions and satisfaction**

answer:
Yes. The broad research literature is fairly consistent on one point:

**The intrinsic quality of the work** — things like interest, autonomy, skill use, learning, challenge, meaning, and good social context — tends to predict sustained job satisfaction and well-being **more strongly than pay differences**, once pay is already “good enough” and perceived as fair.

That does **not** mean “always choose the more interesting job.” But it does mean that, if your choice is between:

- **more interesting work**
- vs **somewhat more money / more status / a promotion**

the research generally says the first category is often more important for long-run happiness at work.

## What the evidence says

### 1) Intrinsic job characteristics are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction
A huge literature in organizational psychology supports this.

#### Classic framework: Job Characteristics Model
Hackman & Oldham’s work argued that jobs are more motivating/satisfying when they have:
- **skill variety**
- **task identity**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

These are very close to what people mean by “interesting work.”

#### Meta-analytic evidence
A major meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** found that work design characteristics such as autonomy, variety, significance, feedback, and social support had substantial associations with:
- **job satisfaction**
- **internal motivation**
- **engagement**
- lower **burnout/strain**

The general pattern is that these effects are **moderate to large**, and typically bigger than the effect of pay on satisfaction.

So on your question “Do people who stay in a more interesting job tend to be happier?” — **on average, yes**, insofar as “interesting” means high autonomy, challenge, learning, skill use, and meaning.

---

### 2) Pay matters, but much less than people expect for job satisfaction
There is good evidence here too.

A widely cited meta-analysis by **Judge et al. (2010)** found that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but small** — roughly in the range people summarize as “money matters, but not nearly as much as you think.”

A good shorthand is:
- **Pay strongly matters when it is too low, unfair, or anxiety-inducing**
- **Beyond that, additional pay has diminishing returns for satisfaction**

That is especially relevant in high-paying fields. If you’re already well-compensated, a **modest salary increase** is less likely to move your day-to-day happiness than:
- what you do all day,
- whether you’re learning,
- whether the work feels meaningful,
- whether your role fits your motives.

So if Meta is “a bit higher salary,” research would generally predict that this alone is **not** likely to dominate the experience of the work itself.

---

### 3) Meaningful/interesting work is strongly linked to well-being
There is also a growing literature on **meaningful work**.

Meta-analyses and reviews generally find that perceived meaningful work is associated with:
- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **life satisfaction**
- better **mental well-being**
- lower **burnout**
- lower **turnover intentions**

This matters because “interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but they often travel together for knowledge workers. For research engineers especially, “interesting” often includes:
- intellectual stimulation
- novelty
- solving hard problems
- feeling at the frontier
- using rare skills well

Those tend to be psychologically valuable.

---

### 4) Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, relatedness
Another very robust line of research is **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan and many follow-ups).

It finds that well-being at work depends heavily on whether a job supports:
- **autonomy** — feeling volitional, not controlled
- **competence** — feeling effective, growing, mastering hard things
- **relatedness** — feeling connected to others

“Interesting research work” often strongly supports autonomy and competence.
“Leading a small team” can support relatedness and competence too — **if** you actually enjoy managing and mentoring.

This is important:  
Your decision is not just **interesting work vs external reward**.

If leading a team is “something I always wanted to do,” that is potentially an **intrinsic motive**, not just a prestige/status motive. Research would treat that differently.

So the more precise question is:

> Which role better fits your intrinsic needs and motives?

Not simply:
> Which role pays more?

---

### 5) Promotions often help less than people expect, and the gains may fade
There is also research on promotions/advancement suggesting a more mixed picture.

Panel-data studies generally find something like:
- Promotions can produce a **short-term boost** in job satisfaction
- But they also often increase:
  - stress
  - hours
  - role conflict
  - administrative burden
- The satisfaction boost often **fades over time**

So if the Meta role gives you:
- more title,
- more responsibility,
- more pay,

that does not automatically imply more well-being.

This is especially relevant when a promotion means moving away from the kind of work you actually enjoy. Many technically strong people discover that managing means less time on the intellectually rewarding parts of the job and more time on coordination, staffing, conflict management, prioritization, and performance management.

Some people love that. Some do not.

Research would predict the outcome depends heavily on **person-role fit**.

---

### 6) Person-job fit and value congruence matter a lot
A strong finding across the literature is that **fit** matters:
- fit between your interests and the job,
- your values and the organization,
- your motives and the role structure.

Meta-analytic work on **person-environment fit** shows moderate-to-strong relationships with:
- job satisfaction
- commitment
- lower quit intentions

This is one of the most relevant findings for your situation.

If:
- Anthropic fits your intellectual interests but not your desire to lead
- Meta fits your desire to lead but not your desire for interesting work

then the key research-based question is:

> Which misfit is likely to be more costly for you over time?

There is no universal answer to that, but the literature strongly suggests that **chronic misfit** is bad for well-being.

---

## What about regret specifically?

This is where the evidence is weaker.

I’m not aware of a strong, direct body of research that asks exactly:

> People who stayed in a more interesting job instead of taking a better-paid management opportunity — were they happier later, or did they regret it?

The literature is much stronger on:
- **satisfaction**
- **well-being**
- **engagement**
- **turnover**
than on retrospective regret for this exact tradeoff.

That said, the closest evidence suggests:

### What people tend to regret long term
Research on regret more broadly often finds that:
- short-term regret often centers on **actions taken**
- long-term regret more often centers on **paths not pursued**, especially those tied to one’s identity or aspirations

So if “leading a team” is a deep aspiration for you, that matters.  
But if the work itself would be meaningfully less engaging every day, that matters too.

The regret literature does **not** clearly say “take the management role” or “stay in the interesting role.”  
It says long-term regret is often about **failing to pursue something central to who you wanted to become**.

That means your wish to lead a team is psychologically important — not just a career bullet point.

---

## The clearest research-backed summary

If I compress the literature into a few lines:

1. **Interesting, autonomous, meaningful work is a strong predictor of sustained job satisfaction.**
2. **Pay has a positive but relatively small effect on job satisfaction once it is already high/fair.**
3. **Promotions/management roles often look better in advance than they feel in practice; the benefits can be temporary and the stress real.**
4. **Fit matters a lot:** if you deeply want to lead, that can be a genuine source of fulfillment; if you deeply need intellectually interesting work, losing that can be costly.
5. **There is more evidence for “interesting work improves well-being” than for “more money or promotion improves long-run happiness.”**

---

## So, in research terms, what should you compare?

A good evidence-based way to compare the two roles is to rate each on:

- **Autonomy**
- **Skill use**
- **Learning/growth**
- **Intellectual interest**
- **Meaning/impact**
- **Fit with identity**
- **Opportunity to mentor/lead**
- **Expected stress/admin burden**
- **Pay fairness/security**
- **Likelihood of future options if you defer one path**

That is closer to how the literature would model this than simply “salary vs interest.”

---

## My best research-only takeaway for your case
If the salary difference is only moderate, and both jobs are already well-paid, the literature would generally predict that:

- **staying in the more interesting role** is more likely to maximize everyday job satisfaction **unless**
- the leadership opportunity satisfies a deep, durable personal motive that your current role cannot satisfy.

So the biggest research-based uncertainty here is not the money.  
It’s whether **leading a team** is:
- a true intrinsic aspiration that would make the work more fulfilling despite lower technical interest,
or
- something you idealize now but that would actually reduce the parts of work that currently energize you.

---

If you want, I can turn this into a **decision memo grounded in the literature** — e.g. a 1-page framework with weighted factors and the key papers behind each factor.

answer:
Yes — there is a fairly strong research literature pointing in one direction:

**After pay is “good enough” and perceived as fair, the quality of the work itself** — especially whether it is **interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and a good fit for you** — tends to matter **more for sustained job satisfaction and well-being** than incremental pay increases.

That is the short version. A more careful summary:

## 1) “Interesting work” is not usually measured directly
In the literature, your “interesting” maps onto a few constructs:

- **Intrinsic motivation**: you enjoy the work itself
- **Meaningful work**: the work feels worthwhile / significant
- **Job design**: autonomy, skill variety, task significance, feedback
- **Person–job fit**: the work matches your abilities/interests/identity

Those variables are all fairly consistently associated with better outcomes.

## 2) Work itself is a strong predictor of satisfaction
A classic line of research is the **job characteristics model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analytic work confirms the basic idea.

### Important result
A large meta-analysis of work design found that jobs with more:
- **autonomy**
- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**

are associated with higher:
- **job satisfaction**
- **internal motivation**
- **engagement**
and lower strain/burnout.

A key reference here is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
  “Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features…”

The general takeaway from this literature is:  
**what you do every day at work matters a lot.**

## 3) Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being
There is also meta-analytic evidence specifically on **meaningful work**.

### Important result
People who experience their work as more meaningful tend to report:
- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **work engagement**
- greater **organizational commitment**
- higher **life satisfaction**
- lower **psychological distress**

Reference:
- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, *Journal of Management Studies*  
  “Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis”

This is probably the closest literature to your “more interesting vs less interesting” question.

## 4) Pay matters — but usually less than people expect
Pay is not irrelevant. But the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is generally **positive but modest**.

### Important result
A well-known meta-analysis found that the correlation between pay and job satisfaction is **real but not large**.

Reference:
- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, *Journal of Vocational Behavior*  
  “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature”

The broad interpretation in org psych is:

- **Insufficient or unfair pay absolutely hurts**
- But once pay is already strong/fair, **additional pay has diminishing psychological returns**, especially compared with job content, autonomy, and fit

That aligns with older theory too:
- **Herzberg’s two-factor theory** argued that pay is more of a “hygiene factor” (prevents dissatisfaction), whereas **the work itself**, growth, and achievement are stronger drivers of satisfaction.  
  That theory is older and methodologically debated, but modern evidence ends up in a somewhat similar place.

## 5) Intrinsic motivation usually beats extrinsic incentives for well-being
The broader **self-determination theory** literature is also relevant.

It suggests that people do better when work supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

And there is substantial evidence that **extrinsic rewards** can fail to increase, and sometimes undermine, intrinsic motivation when they are experienced as controlling.

Reference:
- **Deci, Koestner, & Ryan (1999)**, *Psychological Bulletin*  
  “A meta-analytic review of experiments examining the effects of extrinsic rewards on intrinsic motivation”

This does **not** mean “higher salary is bad.”  
It means: **money is often weaker than people expect at replacing interest in the work itself.**

## 6) Fit matters a lot
Another robust finding: **person–job fit** and **person–organization fit** predict satisfaction and retention.

Reference:
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, *Personnel Psychology*  
  “Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis…”

If Anthropic is a much better intellectual fit and Meta is a better management/organizational fit, that’s a real tradeoff.  
The literature would say that **fit** is not a soft factor; it is a meaningful predictor of satisfaction.

## 7) What about leadership / management?
This part is less clean.

I’m not aware of a single canonical result saying:
> “People are happier if they become managers rather than stay ICs.”

The evidence here is more mixed.

Management roles often bring:
- more **status**
- more **pay**
- often more **autonomy**
but also:
- more **stress**
- more **interruptions**
- more **political load**
- less time doing the interesting technical work itself

So the research-based answer is not “management is better” or “management is worse.” It’s more like:

- If **leading people is itself intrinsically meaningful to you**, that can be a genuine source of satisfaction
- If you want management mainly for title/comp, but the day-to-day work is less interesting, the literature would not predict a large happiness gain

In your case, since you said **leading a small team is something you’ve always wanted**, that is important: it means the Meta offer is not just “more money.” It may provide a different kind of intrinsic reward.

## 8) Do people regret choosing the more interesting job?
Here the evidence is much thinner.

I don’t know of a strong research literature specifically on:
> choosing the more interesting/lower-paid job vs less interesting/higher-paid job, then measuring later regret

Most work studies measure:
- job satisfaction
- burnout
- engagement
- turnover intentions
- life satisfaction

rather than explicit long-term regret.

So I’d say:

- **There is good evidence about satisfaction and well-being**
- **There is much less direct evidence about regret**

That said, the existing literature does **not** support the idea that taking a somewhat higher-paid but substantially less interesting job reliably makes people happier.

## 9) What the literature would suggest in your specific kind of case
If I translate the research into your decision variables:

### The evidence favors staying for “interesting work” when:
- current comp is already high / financially secure
- the work is genuinely engaging and meaningful
- the alternative is **substantially less interesting**
- the pay increase is incremental rather than life-changing

### The evidence favors the new role more when:
- the leadership opportunity is itself deeply meaningful to you
- current role is limiting growth/agency
- the new role still has enough autonomy/meaning/fit
- the pay increase meaningfully changes your financial life, not just your spreadsheet

## 10) A cautious bottom line
If you want the closest thing to a literature-based bottom line, I’d put it this way:

> **On average, people are happier in work that is interesting/meaningful/autonomous and well-matched to them than in work that merely pays more.**  
> Pay matters, but usually less than the day-to-day character of the work once basic compensation is already strong.

And:

> **There is less evidence on “regret,” but there is no strong evidence that people systematically regret staying with more interesting work in exchange for somewhat less money.**

---

## Selected references
- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976).** Motivation through the design of work.
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).** Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).** The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.
- **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L. (2019).** Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005).** Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis…
- **Deci, E. L., Koestner, R., & Ryan, R. M. (1999).** A meta-analytic review of experiments examining the effects of extrinsic rewards on intrinsic motivation.

If you want, I can do one of two useful follow-ups:
1. give you a **short annotated bibliography** with the main findings from each paper, or  
2. turn this into a **decision matrix based on the actual constructs from the literature**: pay, autonomy, meaning, fit, leadership aspiration, stress, and future option value.
