answer:
Yes. The research literature leans pretty clearly in one direction:

**On average, people are more satisfied and often happier in jobs that are intrinsically interesting, autonomous, and meaningful than in jobs that are simply higher paid.**  
The pay effect is real, but in most studies it is **smaller** than the effect of job content/fit.

A few important nuances:

- “Interesting work” in the research usually shows up as **autonomy, skill variety, challenge, learning, task significance, and meaning**.
- “Leading a small team” is **not just an extrinsic perk**. If you genuinely want to lead, that can itself be an intrinsic source of satisfaction.
- There is **much less direct research on regret** than on job satisfaction, engagement, burnout, and turnover.

## What the evidence says

### 1) Job content predicts satisfaction strongly
A big literature in organizational psychology shows that the design of the work itself matters a lot.

Classic framework:
- **Hackman & Oldham (1976)**: jobs are more motivating/satisfying when they have:
  - skill variety
  - task identity
  - task significance
  - autonomy
  - feedback

Meta-analytic update:
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** reviewed a large work-design literature and found that these characteristics have **moderate to strong relationships** with:
  - job satisfaction
  - internal work motivation
  - engagement
  - lower turnover intentions

In plain English:  
If one job is materially more interesting, more autonomous, and more meaningful, research would generally predict **higher day-to-day satisfaction** in that job.

---

### 2) Meaningful / intrinsically motivating work is strongly linked to well-being
Related literatures on **meaningful work** and **self-determination theory** converge on the same result.

- **Gagné & Deci (2005)**: people do better psychologically when work supports:
  - **autonomy**
  - **competence**
  - **relatedness**
- Reviews of the **meaningful work** literature consistently find that meaningful work is associated with:
  - higher job satisfaction
  - higher engagement
  - lower burnout / turnover intention
  - better overall well-being

This is especially relevant if by “interesting” you mean:
- intellectually engaging
- aligned with your values
- something you would care about even if no one paid you extra for it

That kind of work tends to predict better outcomes than work chosen mainly for higher compensation or status.

---

### 3) Salary matters, but less than many people expect
There is solid evidence that actual pay and job satisfaction are related — but not overwhelmingly.

- **Judge et al. (2010)** meta-analysis: the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**.
  - The rough takeaway from that literature is: **money helps, but it explains only a limited share of how satisfied people are with their jobs.**

Two further findings matter a lot:

#### a) Relative pay / fairness matters more than raw pay
- **Card et al. (2012)**: people’s job satisfaction drops when they learn they are paid less than peers.
- So compensation matters a lot when it affects:
  - fairness
  - status
  - perceived appreciation
  - financial security

#### b) Once pay is already “good enough,” job content tends to matter more
If both options already clear the threshold of:
- comfort
- security
- no major family/financial strain

then the incremental happiness from “a bit higher salary” is usually **smaller** than the effect of daily work quality.

So if Meta is only **somewhat** higher-paid, and Anthropic is much more interesting, the literature would usually predict that **interestingness has the larger effect on day-to-day well-being**.

---

### 4) For creative / research work, intrinsic interest matters even more
Your case is not generic office work; it’s research engineering. That matters.

- **Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford (2014)** meta-analysis: **intrinsic motivation** is especially important for performance, particularly for **quality** of performance.
- In complex, creative, cognitively demanding work, intrinsic interest tends to matter more than external incentives.

That means for research roles, “interestingness” is not just about happiness. It is also often tied to:
- persistence
- creativity
- deep work
- quality of output

So the evidence may be even more relevant in your case than in routine jobs.

---

### 5) Person-job fit is a major predictor
A very robust result in organizational psychology is that **fit** matters.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** meta-analysis:
  - better **person-job fit**
  - better **person-organization fit**
  are associated with higher job satisfaction, stronger commitment, and lower turnover intentions.

This is important because your decision is **not just**:

- interesting work vs money

It is also:

- technical/research fit vs managerial/leadership fit

If “leading a small team” is something you’ve genuinely wanted for a long time, that is a meaningful signal. Research would say:
- if leadership fits your motives and identity, it can raise satisfaction
- if you mainly want the title/status but not the day-to-day people management, it often disappoints

So the real comparison is likely:

- **Anthropic**: stronger intellectual/content fit
- **Meta**: potentially stronger leadership-growth fit

Both are intrinsically relevant, not just one.

---

### 6) Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction
This is not just about work.

- **Tait, Padgett, & Baldwin (1989)** and later work show a **moderate relationship** between job satisfaction and life satisfaction.
- Some longitudinal work suggests the influence runs both ways, but job satisfaction clearly contributes to broader well-being.

So if one role makes your daily work meaningfully better, that often affects life outside work too.

---

## What about regret specifically?
Here the evidence is weaker.

There is **far less direct research** on:
- “Do people regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paying one?”

than on:
- satisfaction
- burnout
- turnover
- well-being
- engagement

What the indirect evidence suggests:

### People tend to do better when choices align with intrinsic motives
Research from **self-determination theory** and related work on **intrinsic vs extrinsic goals** generally finds that people focused on intrinsic goals (growth, interest, relationships, contribution) report better well-being than those focused mainly on extrinsic goals (money, image, status).

That does **not** mean “money is bad” or “prestige is bad.”  
It means that choices driven primarily by external rewards tend to be less satisfying over time than choices that fit deeper motives.

### Regret is more likely when people ignore stable preferences
There is also broad decision research suggesting people often mispredict what will matter later, especially by overweighting:
- salary
- prestige
- title
and underweighting:
- daily experience
- task enjoyment
- fit with identity

So while direct “career regret” evidence is sparse, the broader literature would predict **more durable satisfaction** from the role whose day-to-day activities you actually want to do.

---

## If I had to summarize the research in one sentence
For someone already in a high-paying, high-skill career, **the literature usually predicts that interesting, autonomous, meaningful work contributes more to lasting job satisfaction than a modest salary increase**.

The strongest exception is when the alternative role satisfies another **intrinsic need** that matters deeply to you — in your case, possibly **leading a team**.

So the empirical framing of your decision is probably not:

- “interesting work vs a bit more money”

but rather:

- “deep research interest” vs “leadership growth/fit”

And research says both of those are important; the likely winner is the one that better matches your enduring motivations.

---

## Most relevant papers to look at
If you want a reading list, these are good starting points:

1. **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976).**  
   *Motivation through the design of work: Test of a theory.*  
   Journal of Organizational Behavior / Organizational Behavior and Human Performance.

2. **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).**  
   *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*  
   Journal of Applied Psychology.

3. **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).**  
   *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*  
   Journal of Vocational Behavior.

4. **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005).**  
   *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.*  
   Personnel Psychology.

5. **Gagné, M., & Deci, E. L. (2005).**  
   *Self-determination theory and work motivation.*  
   Journal of Organizational Behavior.

6. **Cerasoli, C. P., Nicklin, J. M., & Ford, M. T. (2014).**  
   *Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives jointly predict performance: A 40-year meta-analysis.*  
   Psychological Bulletin.

7. **Card, D., Mas, A., Moretti, E., & Saez, E. (2012).**  
   *Inequality at work: The effect of peer salaries on job satisfaction.*  
   American Economic Review.

8. **Tait, M., Padgett, M. Y., & Baldwin, T. T. (1989).**  
   *Job and life satisfaction: A reevaluation of the strength of the relationship and gender effects.*  
   Journal of Applied Psychology.

If you want, I can do a second pass and turn this into a **more decision-focused summary**, e.g.:
- which variables seem most predictive in cases like yours,
- how to interpret “wanting to lead” in research terms,
- or a compact **evidence-based scorecard** for comparing Anthropic vs Meta.
prompt:
I'm working as a Research Engineer in Anthropic doing very interesting stuff. I got a proposal from Meta - a bit higher salary, leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do, but much less interesting. I wonder what to do. 

There must be some research on how important is doing interesting work comparing to other factors. Do people who stay in a more interesting job tend to be happier? Or do they regret that? Can you recall any results on that? I'm mostly interested in research results, not personal opinions.

reasoning:
**Evaluating promotion impacts**

I’m considering evidence from panel data that shows promotions can boost job satisfaction in the short term, especially regarding pay and security. However, it seems that this effect on overall job satisfaction may diminish over time. I might want to look into longitudinal panel studies about promotions. Let’s check memory for sources like "The effects of promotions on job satisfaction" or even the German Socio-Economic Panel, maybe something by Kornrich? Not entirely sure.
**Considering job satisfaction factors**

I might want to avoid making speculative statements about regret since I have limited direct evidence. What I can note is that "interesting work" frequently tops international job surveys as an important attribute. When preferences are surveyed, meaningful work often ranks highly, sometimes even above salary after reaching a pay threshold. However, it seems that intrinsic job qualities—like interest, meaning, and autonomy—strongly predict job satisfaction and well-being more than pay does, especially for those already well-compensated.
**Gathering decision-relevant insights**

I could suggest a decision-relevant checklist based on research, but since the user wants mostly research rather than opinions, I’ll focus on key constructs and their likely implications. It makes sense to gather specific results, including rough effect sizes and citations. For instance, in Judge et al. (2010), they found a correlation of about 0.15 between pay level and job satisfaction. Even after correcting for errors and employing lagged designs, the correlation remains modest.
**Analyzing job satisfaction factors**

I'm considering several factors related to job satisfaction. First, discussing promotions may not be straightforward. There's research by Green or Clark that shows promotions can raise pay satisfaction, but effects on overall job satisfaction are mixed, especially when increased hours or stress come into play. 

I might also touch on job crafting and the concept of "flow," where skill variety improves engagement. There’s this idea of diminishing returns on high incomes, indicating that additional pay impacts life satisfaction less as salaries rise. 

The literature supports that while higher incomes can improve well-being, marginal returns diminish with increased earnings.
**Evaluating work factors impact**

I’m considering that a salary bump might not be as significant as how interesting the work is. It’s important to compare interesting work against other factors. Since both companies probably offer high salaries, the extra pay's impact could be minimal.

I should also think about work hours. More intense jobs may negatively affect well-being. A meta-analysis on the demands-resources model suggests that while leadership can ramp up job demands, it can also provide resources like autonomy and learning opportunities that help buffer those demands.
**Evaluating job satisfaction factors**

There's strong evidence that the intrinsic qualities of work influence job satisfaction more than pay, while research on later regret is limited. People tend to be happier in jobs they find interesting and meaningful, especially when their pay is already adequate. 

Key findings suggest that while pay has a small to moderate correlation with satisfaction, elements like autonomy, variety, and significance in job design have much stronger effects. Leadership roles show mixed results; they can improve satisfaction through increased status and pay but might also lead to stress.
**Analyzing aspirations and job satisfaction**

There’s limited evidence on regret, but research indicates that intrinsic aspirations lead to better well-being than extrinsic ones, resulting in less burnout and turnover. While most studies are correlational, longitudinal studies often show that moving to more meaningful jobs tends to boost satisfaction, whereas promotions and pay increases yield diminishing returns.

From a research standpoint, the essential question isn't simply "higher salary vs. interesting work," but rather "which combination offers more autonomy, meaning, and fit?" At higher income levels, non-monetary job attributes explain a greater variance in satisfaction.
**Exploring wage differentials**

I could reference the literature on compensating wage differentials, which suggests that workers often accept lower pay in exchange for better non-monetary job attributes. Economists conclude that these non-pecuniary aspects are highly valued by employees. 

There’s significant research in labor economics that examines job amenities like interesting work, flexibility, autonomy, and purpose. Many studies highlight people's willingness to accept lower wages for schedule flexibility, remote work, and meaningfulness, pointing to large valuations for non-monetary job features that can sometimes equal meaningful pay differences.
**Analyzing job satisfaction and promotions**

I'm looking into Kosteas' 2011 paper, which discusses how promotions can lead to increased job satisfaction, though it's often short-lived. Workers experience temporary happiness from promotions, but it doesn’t fully boost their long-term satisfaction, especially after adjusting for their expectations. I have to consider Clark's 1999 paper, which suggests that promotions might not change satisfaction significantly due to added stress and longer hours. There's a lot to unpack here!
**Considering income and job quality**

I need to address that among already highly compensated knowledge workers, income differences of around 10-20% usually predict less change in well-being compared to significant variations in day-to-day job quality. This is based on effect sizes but should be noted as "on average." I might mention sparse evidence regarding "regret" and highlight that it can be higher when people feel they've compromised their core values. I should be cautious and say, "I don’t know strong direct studies on this."
answer:
Yes — there is a fairly solid research base here, though usually not using the exact phrase **“interesting work”**. In the literature, the closest constructs are:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **meaningful work**
- **autonomy**
- **skill variety / learning / challenge**
- **person–job fit**

And the broad pattern is:

> **People tend to be more satisfied and psychologically healthier in jobs that are intrinsically engaging, meaningful, and autonomy-supporting than in jobs that mainly score higher on extrinsic rewards like pay.**

That does **not** mean pay or advancement don’t matter. It means that, on average, the non-pay quality of the work often predicts day-to-day work well-being **more strongly** than pay does, especially once compensation is already high enough.

## What the research seems to say

### 1) Pay matters, but its link to job satisfaction is surprisingly small
A well-known meta-analysis by **Judge et al. (2010)** found that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but small** — roughly in the range people summarize as “money matters, but much less than most expect.”

That is one of the most robust findings in this area:  
- higher pay is associated with somewhat higher job satisfaction,
- but the effect is much weaker than many people intuitively assume.

This is especially relevant when the choice is between **two already-elite, high-paying jobs**. In that range, the marginal well-being benefit of extra salary is usually smaller than the benefit of better day-to-day work experience.

Related income-and-happiness research in economics/psychology also finds **diminishing marginal returns** to income: more money still helps on average, but the difference between “very high” and “slightly higher” tends to matter much less than the difference between “financially strained” and “comfortable.”

### 2) Job design / interestingness-type factors have stronger associations with satisfaction
A major meta-analysis on work design by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** found that characteristics like:

- **autonomy**
- **task variety**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- **job complexity**

have **moderate to strong** associations with:

- **job satisfaction**
- **internal work motivation**
- **engagement**
- sometimes performance

This is one of the strongest lines of evidence relevant to your question, because “interesting work” in practice often means some combination of:
- intellectually challenging,
- varied,
- high-skill,
- meaningful,
- autonomous.

Those features consistently predict better work experience.

### 3) Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being
There’s also a large literature on **meaningful work**. Meta-analytic reviews (for example, **Allan et al.**) generally find that meaningful work is positively associated with:

- **job satisfaction**
- **work engagement**
- **life satisfaction**
- **lower distress / lower burnout**

Again, a caveat: most of this evidence is correlational, though not all of it. But the pattern is quite consistent.

If your current role feels deeply interesting because it connects to identity, purpose, or intellectual curiosity, research suggests that is not a trivial preference — it is one of the core ingredients of work well-being.

### 4) Intrinsic motivation tends to beat extrinsic motivation for well-being
From **Self-Determination Theory** and related work: environments that support
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

tend to produce better motivation and well-being than environments organized mainly around external rewards.

Meta-analytic work (e.g. **Van den Broeck et al.**) supports this: satisfaction of those psychological needs at work is strongly related to:
- higher engagement,
- higher satisfaction,
- lower burnout,
- better functioning overall.

That does **not** imply “follow your passion” in a naive way. It does imply that if a job is interesting because it lets you exercise competence, curiosity, and agency, that usually has real psychological value.

### 5) Fit matters a lot
Another strong finding in organizational psychology is **person–job fit** and **person–organization fit**. Meta-analyses such as **Kristof-Brown et al. (2005)** show that fit is strongly related to:

- job satisfaction,
- commitment,
- lower turnover intentions,
- better well-being.

This matters because “interestingness” is partly objective and partly personal.

If *you* strongly value:
- intellectually novel work,
- frontier research,
- technical depth,
- being close to hard problems,

then the cost of moving to less interesting work may be larger **for you** than for someone who mainly values management scope, status, or organizational scale.

So the most research-consistent framing is not:
> “Is interesting work always better than more pay and leadership?”

It is more:
> “Which option better matches the values and psychological needs that most drive my well-being?”

### 6) Leadership/promotion is not a guaranteed happiness gain
Research on promotions/managerial moves is more mixed.

There is evidence that promotions and leadership roles often improve:
- satisfaction with **pay**,
- **status**,
- sometimes **autonomy**.

But they also often increase:
- **stress**,
- **hours**,
- **role conflict**,
- responsibility for others.

So the net effect on overall well-being is not uniformly positive.

In other words:
- if leading a team is something you intrinsically want, that can be a real positive;
- if it is mostly attractive as a marker of advancement, it may not compensate for losing engaging work.

That distinction — intrinsic desire to lead vs. external prestige of leading — is very consistent with the literature.

## Do people regret choosing the more interesting job?

This is the weakest part of the evidence base.

I’m **not aware of strong direct longitudinal research** that cleanly answers:

> “Do people later regret staying in the more interesting job rather than taking the higher-paying, less interesting one?”

What we do have are adjacent findings:

1. **Intrinsic work qualities** predict satisfaction and lower burnout.
2. **Extrinsic rewards alone** have weaker associations with sustained satisfaction.
3. **Value-congruent choices / fit** predict better outcomes.
4. Promotions and pay increases often produce smaller or less durable gains than people expect.

So the best evidence-based inference is:

> People do not, on average, seem to get sustained well-being gains from optimizing only for pay/status when that means sacrificing strongly valued intrinsic aspects of work.

But I would phrase that as an inference, not as a definitive “regret” result.

## If I had to summarize the literature in one sentence

For people who are already well compensated, **the day-to-day quality of the work — especially interest, autonomy, meaning, learning, and fit — usually matters more for job satisfaction than incremental pay does.**

## Practical implications from the research, not personal advice

If you want to reason about this in a research-grounded way, the variables most worth estimating are:

1. **How much less interesting is the new work, really?**  
   Not just “less cool” — specifically: less autonomy? less learning? less creativity? less meaning?

2. **How much do you intrinsically want to lead?**  
   If team leadership is itself deeply motivating, that is not merely an extrinsic perk; it’s another intrinsic job quality.

3. **What is the pay increase as a fraction of already-high compensation?**  
   Research would predict smaller well-being gains from an increment at the top end than from changes in work quality.

4. **Which role better fits your identity and values?**  
   Fit is one of the strongest predictors of satisfaction.

## A short reading list
If you want papers/reviews to look up:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction  
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis on work design/job characteristics  
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis on person-environment fit  
- **Van den Broeck et al. (2016)** — meta-analytic review on basic psychological needs at work  
- **Allan et al.** — meta-analytic work on meaningful work and well-being  
- classic background: **Hackman & Oldham** on the Job Characteristics Model

If you want, I can also do one of two more specific things:

1. give you a **more technical literature summary with effect sizes and caveats**, or  
2. help translate this into a **decision framework based only on empirically supported predictors**.

answer:
Yes. The broad research answer is:

**For people who are already well-paid, the day-to-day quality of the work itself — especially interest, autonomy, meaning, learning, and fit — usually predicts job satisfaction more strongly than incremental pay does.**  
The evidence on **future regret** is thinner and less direct, but there are some relevant findings.

## 1) “Interesting work” is a strong predictor of job satisfaction

In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually studied indirectly through things like:

- **skill variety**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance / meaning**
- **feedback**
- **complexity / challenge**
- **opportunities to learn**
- **intrinsic motivation**

Two very robust literatures are relevant:

### A. Job design / work characteristics
The classic **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham) and later meta-analyses found that jobs with more autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback are associated with:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **internal motivation**
- lower **turnover intentions**
- often better **well-being**

A major meta-analysis:
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** reviewed a large work-design literature and found that characteristics like autonomy, task variety, task significance, and feedback had **moderate positive associations** with job satisfaction and engagement.

### B. Self-determination theory
This literature says people do better when work supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

When work satisfies those needs, people tend to report more:
- well-being
- engagement
- persistence
- satisfaction

This is one reason “interesting work” matters so much: it often bundles together autonomy, mastery, challenge, and meaning.

**Bottom line:** research strongly supports the idea that people are happier in work they find engaging and intrinsically motivating.

---

## 2) Salary matters, but usually less than people think once basic needs are comfortably met

There is also direct research on **pay vs job satisfaction**.

A well-known meta-analysis:
- **Judge et al. (2010)** found that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but relatively small**.

The rough takeaway from that literature is:

- **being underpaid** or feeling **unfairly paid** clearly hurts satisfaction
- but once pay is already high enough that financial stress is low, **extra pay has diminishing returns**
- the **content of the work** and the **fit** often matter more for satisfaction than a marginal salary increase

This lines up with broader happiness research:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** and later work by **Killingsworth** and others show that income does improve well-being, but the effect on **daily emotional experience** is much smaller than many people expect, especially at higher income levels.
- At high professional incomes, more money often affects **life evaluation** (“I’m successful”) more than everyday experience (“my workday feels good”).

So if the Meta offer is only **“a bit higher salary”**, the research would usually predict that salary alone is **unlikely** to outweigh a large drop in work interest.

---

## 3) Meaningful work and person-job fit matter a lot

Another strong line of research is **person-environment fit** / **person-job fit**.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**: fit between the person and the job/organization is strongly related to job satisfaction, commitment, and lower turnover intentions.

This matters because your choice is not just:
- interesting research vs more money

It’s also:
- current role that fits your research interests
vs
- leadership role that may fit your aspiration to lead

Research would predict that **fit with your values and identity** matters a lot.  
If leading a team is something you’ve wanted for a long time, that is not trivial — it is a genuine source of potential satisfaction.

So the real comparison is not “interesting vs money”; it is more like:

- **intellectual interest / intrinsic motivation**
vs
- **leadership aspiration / growth / identity fit**

And both of those are supported by the literature as meaningful drivers of satisfaction.

---

## 4) Leadership roles are mixed: they can increase satisfaction, but only if you actually like the day-to-day reality of management

The evidence here is less clean than for job design, but the general picture is:

Leadership roles can increase:
- autonomy
- influence
- status
- sense of impact
- growth

But they also often increase:
- stress
- administrative load
- interruptions
- conflict management
- responsibility for others
- time away from the work you originally loved

So the research-based question is not “Do people like leading?” in the abstract.  
It’s closer to:

**Do people who value leadership and have strong fit for it become happier when their daily work shifts from craft/technical work toward people-management?**

The answer seems to be: **sometimes, but very much not always**.

In practice, many technical professionals are happiest when they can keep some combination of:
- intellectually interesting work
- autonomy
- mentoring/influence
without becoming full-time managers too early

That is not a moral claim; it’s a fit claim.

---

## 5) On regret: the evidence is weaker, but there are two useful findings

There isn’t much direct research asking:

> “Do people regret choosing the more interesting lower-paid job over the less interesting higher-paid leadership job?”

I wouldn’t pretend there is a neat definitive study on that exact tradeoff.

But two adjacent findings matter:

### A. Long-term regret often centers on values and identity
People tend to regret choices that feel **inauthentic** or inconsistent with what they cared about most.

So if you later felt:
- “I sold out the work I loved for a small pay bump”
that could produce regret.

But if you later felt:
- “I always wanted to lead, and I kept avoiding the chance”
that could also produce regret.

### B. Inaction regrets often grow over time
A classic finding from regret research:
- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)** argued that over the long term, people often regret **inactions** more than actions.

Applied carefully, that suggests:
- if leading a team is a deep, identity-level aspiration,
- **not** taking a serious leadership opportunity could become a regret source later.

But that is about **regret**, not necessarily **daily happiness**.

And that distinction matters a lot.

You can have:
- higher day-to-day happiness in the interesting job,
but
- more long-term regret about never trying leadership

or the reverse.

---

## 6) What the literature would predict in a case like yours

Very roughly, for someone already in a high-status, high-pay role:

### If you stay in the more interesting Anthropic role
Research would predict higher odds of:
- stronger daily engagement
- higher intrinsic motivation
- better job satisfaction
- less “why am I doing this?” feeling

especially if the work is genuinely more intellectually compelling.

### If you take the Meta role
Research would predict better outcomes **if**:
- leading is itself intrinsically meaningful to you
- the role gives genuine autonomy and influence
- you won’t spend most of your time on bureaucratic coordination
- the work is “less interesting” but still sufficiently engaging
- you value growth into leadership as part of your identity

In other words:

**The literature is more favorable to “interesting work” than to “slightly more money.”**  
But it is **not** automatically more favorable to “interesting individual-contributor work” than to “less interesting work plus a deeply valued leadership step.”

That second tradeoff is much more balanced.

---

## 7) If you want the most research-grounded summary possible

I’d summarize the literature like this:

1. **Interesting / meaningful / autonomy-supportive work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.**
2. **Pay has a real but smaller effect, especially once pay is already high.**
3. **Perceived fit with your values and goals matters a lot.**
4. **Leadership can be satisfying when it matches your motives, but it often disappoints people who love the craft more than the managerial reality.**
5. **Regret evidence is less direct, but missed identity-relevant opportunities can loom large later.**

So if your question is:

> “What is more likely to make someone happier: a slightly higher salary or more interesting work?”

Research says: **more interesting work, on average.**

If your real question is:

> “What is more likely to make someone happier: more interesting work, or a meaningful first chance to lead?”

Research says: **it depends much more on your personal fit**, because both are important non-monetary drivers of satisfaction.

---

## 8) Useful papers / literatures to look at

A short reading list:

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R.** (1976/1980) — Job Characteristics Model  
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P.** (2007) — meta-analysis of work design
- **Judge, T. A., et al.** (2010) — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C.** (2005) — person-job / person-organization fit meta-analysis
- **Gagné, M., & Deci, E. L.** (2005) — self-determination theory at work
- **Kahneman, D., & Deaton, A.** (2010) — income and emotional well-being
- **Killingsworth, M. A.** (2021) and later reconciliation work — updated income/well-being evidence
- **Gilovich, T., & Medvec, V. H.** (1995) — action vs inaction regret

If you want, I can next do one of two things:

1. give you a **more technical literature summary with effect sizes**, or  
2. help you build a **research-based decision matrix** for your specific Anthropic vs Meta choice.

answer:
Yes — there is a fairly solid research base here, and the broad result is:

**Work that is intrinsically good** — interesting, meaningful, autonomous, skill-using, growth-promoting, and well-matched to your interests — is usually a **stronger predictor of sustained job satisfaction and well-being** than pay level alone.  
A **higher salary helps**, especially if pay is low or feels unfair, but among already well-paid professionals its association with job satisfaction is typically **much smaller** than the association of intrinsic job qualities.

The main caveat is that **“leading a team” can itself be an intrinsic good** if it satisfies motives like autonomy, impact, mentoring, and growth. So the research does **not** say “stay technical” or “take the money”; it says to take seriously the **day-to-day psychological quality** of the work.

## What the research tends to find

### 1) Interesting/meaningful work strongly predicts job satisfaction
A large literature in organizational psychology finds that jobs are better, psychologically, when they have things like:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- **opportunities for learning/mastery**

This comes from the classic **Job Characteristics Model** and later meta-analyses.

**Key result:** these “motivational job characteristics” are typically **strongly associated** with:
- job satisfaction
- intrinsic motivation
- engagement
- lower turnover intention

A major meta-analysis:
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** found that motivational job characteristics had **substantial relationships** with job satisfaction and related outcomes.

Relatedly, research on **meaningful work** finds that people who experience their work as meaningful tend to report:
- higher job satisfaction
- greater engagement
- better overall well-being
- lower withdrawal/turnover intentions

Meta-analytic evidence:
- **Allan et al. (2019)**: meaningful work is **robustly positively associated** with job satisfaction, work engagement, and well-being.

### 2) Pay matters, but less than many people expect
There is a famous tendency for people to overestimate how much salary differences will affect long-run happiness, especially once compensation is already high enough to remove financial stress.

For **job satisfaction specifically**, the relationship between **pay level** and satisfaction is real but **modest**.

A widely cited meta-analysis:
- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** found the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction was only about **0.15**.

That is not zero — but it is **small** by organizational-psychology standards.

Interpretation:
- If one job pays much too little, pay is very important.
- If both jobs pay well, a somewhat higher salary usually does **less** for satisfaction than differences in:
  - interestingness
  - autonomy
  - fit
  - meaning
  - growth

### 3) Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction
This matters because your question is really about being happier overall, not merely liking your work.

Meta-analytic work suggests:
- **job satisfaction and life satisfaction are moderately correlated**

A classic meta-analysis:
- **Heller, Judge, & Watson (2002)** found a substantial positive relation between the two.

So if a job is much more satisfying day to day, that often does **not** stay contained within the office; it tends to affect general well-being.

### 4) Fit with your interests matters a lot
Research on **person-job fit** and **vocational interest congruence** finds that people do better when the work matches their interests, values, and preferred activities.

Meta-analytic evidence:
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**: person-job fit is positively related to job satisfaction and commitment.
- **Nye, Su, Rounds, & Drasgow (2012)**: better match between vocational interests and occupation is associated with better satisfaction and performance.

This is very relevant to your situation because there are really **two different “interestingness” questions**:
1. Is the **domain/problem space** interesting?
2. Is the **mode of work** interesting? (IC research vs people leadership)

If you deeply want to lead a team, that may improve fit even if the topic is less compelling.

### 5) Intrinsic vs extrinsic goals: intrinsic tends to win for well-being
A separate line of work in motivation psychology finds that prioritizing **intrinsic goals** (growth, relationships, contribution, mastery) tends to be associated with better well-being than prioritizing **extrinsic goals** (money, status, image) as the main drivers.

This is strongly associated with **Self-Determination Theory**:
- work is better psychologically when it supports **autonomy**, **competence**, and **relatedness**

There is a large body of evidence that satisfaction of these needs at work predicts:
- well-being
- motivation
- persistence
- less burnout

Classic authors:
- **Deci & Ryan**
- reviews/meta-analytic work on need satisfaction at work, e.g. **Van den Broeck et al.**

This doesn’t mean money/status are bad. It means that when they are the **primary basis** for a choice, they often produce less durable well-being than choices aligned with intrinsic motives.

## What about regret specifically?
This is the part where the evidence is **thinner**.

There is **much more research on satisfaction and well-being** than on “Which job choice do people later regret?”

What can be said with some confidence:

### 1) People often regret not pursuing their “ideal-self” paths
Regret research suggests that enduring regrets often involve failures to pursue the life one felt one **should have lived**, rather than simply missing out on a higher payoff.

A classic paper:
- **Roese & Summerville (2005)** found that common life regrets center on domains like education, career, romance, and self-development.

In regret theory more broadly, long-run regrets often skew toward:
- **inaction**
- not pursuing one’s values
- not becoming the person one wanted to become

That is not identical to “people regret choosing salary over interest,” but it is directionally relevant.

### 2) There is more evidence for “happier now” than for “regret later”
So the strongest evidence-based statement is:

- People in more intrinsically rewarding work are generally **more satisfied and often better off psychologically**.
- Evidence specifically phrased as “they later regret not taking the higher-paying role” is **much less developed**.

## What about promotions / leading a team?
This is where your case gets more nuanced.

Research does **not** show that promotion or management is uniformly better or worse for well-being. It often brings:

**Potential gains**
- more autonomy
- more influence
- more meaning via impact on others
- development and identity growth

**Potential costs**
- more interruptions
- more role conflict
- more stress
- less time doing the core work you currently enjoy
- people-management burdens

So the key variable is often **fit**, not “manager > IC” or vice versa.

A leadership role can be highly satisfying if what you’ve “always wanted” is:
- coaching
- setting direction
- hiring/building
- multiplying others’ impact

But it can be unsatisfying if what you actually wanted was:
- recognition/status
- a title bump
- a vague idea of advancement

because the day-to-day reality of management is often very different from the fantasy of it.

That distinction is not personal advice; it follows from the fit/autonomy/meaning literature.

## The strongest evidence-based takeaways
If I compress the literature into a few propositions:

### Fairly strong conclusions
1. **Interesting/meaningful/autonomous work predicts job satisfaction strongly.**
2. **Pay level predicts job satisfaction, but much more weakly.**
3. **Job satisfaction relates meaningfully to overall life satisfaction.**
4. **Fit with your interests and motives matters a lot.**
5. **Intrinsic aspects of work usually have more durable well-being effects than extrinsic rewards alone.**

### Weaker but plausible conclusions
6. People probably **overweight salary/status** relative to daily work experience when forecasting happiness.
7. Long-run regret may more often come from **not pursuing valued, identity-relevant paths** than from not maximizing pay, but this is less directly studied.

## If you want a research-grounded way to think about your specific choice
Not personal advice, just translating the literature into variables that matter most.

Try evaluating each role on:

- **Autonomy**: How much control over what/how you work?
- **Competence/growth**: Which role will make you feel more mastery and learning?
- **Meaning/significance**: Which work will feel more worth doing?
- **Interest/curiosity**: Which role gives more day-to-day fascination?
- **Fit**: Which role better matches your actual motivations?
- **Leadership motive**: Do you want to lead because mentoring/building is intrinsically appealing, or mainly because it seems like the next rung?
- **Pay sufficiency/fairness**: Is Anthropic already comfortably above your threshold where extra money mostly changes little day to day?

That is basically what the research says matters most.

## Short answer
If by “more interesting” you mean **more meaningful, more engaging, more aligned with your interests, and more intrinsically motivating**, then yes:

**People in those jobs generally do tend to be happier with their work, and often happier overall, than people who choose mainly on salary/status.**  
The evidence for **regret** is less direct, but what exists is more consistent with regretting failures to pursue intrinsically valued paths than regretting modestly lower compensation.

If you want, I can also give you a **short reading list of the most relevant papers/meta-analyses** on this question, or summarize the evidence in a **decision table** tailored to your exact tradeoff:  
**interesting IC research vs slightly higher pay + first-line management.**

answer:
Yes. There is a fairly robust literature suggesting that, for happiness and job satisfaction, **interesting/meaningful work and autonomy usually matter more than incremental pay differences**, especially once pay is already high enough that financial stress is low.

A concise summary of the evidence:

## What the research tends to find

### 1. Interesting, meaningful, well-designed work strongly predicts job satisfaction
This is one of the most consistent findings in organizational psychology.

- **Job characteristics theory**: work is more satisfying when it has
  - skill variety,
  - task significance,
  - autonomy,
  - feedback,
  - and a sense of whole-task ownership.

- A large meta-analysis found that these job-design features are **moderately to strongly related** to job satisfaction, internal motivation, and engagement.

**Key source**
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, meta-analysis of work design research.  
  Main finding: **autonomy, task variety, task significance, and feedback** are among the strongest predictors of positive work attitudes.

This is probably the closest research analogue to “interesting work.”

---

### 2. Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being
There is also a substantial literature on “meaningful work” specifically.

- People who experience their work as meaningful tend to report:
  - higher job satisfaction,
  - higher life satisfaction,
  - more engagement,
  - stronger commitment,
  - lower turnover intention,
  - and less burnout.

**Key source**
- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis on meaningful work.  
  Main finding: meaningful work has **moderate-to-strong positive associations** with job satisfaction and well-being.

This doesn’t prove causality perfectly, but the pattern is strong and very consistent.

---

### 3. Pay is positively related to satisfaction, but usually only weakly
Pay matters, but its relationship to job satisfaction is much smaller than many people expect.

**Key source**
- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction.  
  Main finding: **pay level has only a weak positive correlation with job satisfaction**.

So, in plain English: higher salary helps, but usually not nearly as much as people think, unless the difference meaningfully changes your financial security or reduces a real stressor.

For someone already in a high-paying role, a “bit higher salary” is, according to this literature, unlikely to dominate the effect of losing interest in the work.

---

### 4. Person-job fit matters a lot
A related literature asks whether the job matches your interests, values, and strengths.

**Key source**
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, meta-analysis on person-environment fit.  
  Main finding: **person-job fit and person-organization fit are strongly related to job satisfaction and lower intention to quit**.

This is relevant because your choice is not just “money vs no money”; it is also **fit**:
- Anthropic: highly interesting work, likely stronger intellectual fit
- Meta: leadership opportunity, but less interesting content

Both could be “fit” in different dimensions.

---

### 5. Leadership/promotion helps some people, but the effect is mixed
There is research showing that promotions can increase job satisfaction, but the effect is not huge and often isn’t permanent.

**Key source**
- **Kosteas (2011)** on promotions and job satisfaction.  
  Main finding: promotions are associated with higher job satisfaction, but much of the effect seems tied to **future prospects/status** rather than purely the new day-to-day work; the effect is not necessarily large or lasting.

This is important because “leading a team” can improve well-being if it gives you:
- more autonomy,
- more influence,
- more growth,
- attainment of a valued goal.

But it can also reduce well-being if it adds:
- people-management stress,
- less time on the work you actually enjoy,
- more politics,
- more responsibility without corresponding meaning.

So the literature does **not** say “management is better.” It says the outcome depends a lot on whether leadership itself is intrinsically valuable to you.

---

### 6. Intrinsic motivation usually predicts better experience than extrinsic incentives
Another relevant line of work distinguishes intrinsic motivation (“this work is interesting/satisfying in itself”) from extrinsic motivation (“I do it for pay/status”).

Broadly:
- intrinsic motivation is strongly linked to persistence, engagement, creativity, and satisfaction;
- extrinsic rewards can help, but they do not replace intrinsic interest.

**Useful sources**
- **Deci & Ryan** / Self-Determination Theory
- **Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford (2014)**, meta-analysis on intrinsic motivation and incentives

For research work specifically, intrinsic motivation tends to matter a lot because the work is cognitively demanding and open-ended.

---

## What about happiness, specifically?
The happiness literature generally distinguishes:

1. **Evaluative well-being**  
   “How satisfied am I with my life/career overall?”
2. **Experienced well-being**  
   “How do I feel day to day?”

Income tends to have a stronger relationship with evaluative measures than with moment-to-moment emotional experience. Day-to-day experience is often more affected by:
- what you actually do each day,
- whether you find it engaging,
- whether you have autonomy,
- whether the work feels meaningful.

So if one job is much less interesting, the literature would usually predict that this matters a lot for your **experienced well-being**.

---

## Do people regret choosing the more interesting job?
Here the evidence is weaker.

I’m not aware of a strong, clean literature specifically asking:

> “Do people later regret choosing a more interesting job over a better-paid/leadership job?”

Most studies use proxies like:
- job satisfaction,
- life satisfaction,
- burnout,
- turnover,
- engagement,
- commitment.

From those proxies, the literature leans toward:
- people generally do better when work remains meaningful/interesting and aligned with their values;
- salary alone is a relatively weak predictor once compensation is already high;
- promotions/leadership help mainly when they satisfy a genuine personal goal or improve autonomy/fit.

So on “regret,” the best evidence-based answer is:
- **direct regret evidence is limited**,
- but the variables most associated with later satisfaction are meaning, fit, autonomy, and intrinsic motivation.

---

## The most relevant nuance in your case
Your decision is not just:

- interesting work vs slightly more money

It is also:

- interesting work vs a longstanding desire to lead a team

Research would treat that second part very differently from pure compensation.

If leadership is a genuine, self-concordant goal — something you actually want, not just something that looks good — then it may contribute to well-being in the same way meaning/autonomy do.

A useful lens from motivation research is:
- **extrinsic gain**: slightly more salary
- **intrinsic / self-concordant gain**: getting to lead, develop others, shape a direction

If leading is truly important to you, the literature would not automatically favor staying in the more interesting technical role.

---

## My evidence-based bottom line
If I strip this down to the literature:

- **Interesting/meaningful work**: strong predictor of satisfaction and well-being
- **Autonomy and fit**: strong predictor
- **Pay level**: real but usually weak predictor, especially at already-high income
- **Promotion/leadership**: mixed; helpful when it aligns with your values and desired identity, less helpful when it mainly adds stress/status

So the research does **not** support “take the higher salary.”
It more strongly supports:

> Choose the role with the better combination of  
> **interest + meaning + autonomy + fit + attainment of goals you genuinely care about.**

If that is Anthropic, the literature tends to favor staying.
If the leadership opportunity at Meta satisfies a deep personal goal and gives real autonomy/meaning despite less interesting subject matter, the evidence becomes more mixed.

---

## Papers/authors worth looking up
If you want a reading list:

1. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
   *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature*

2. **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**  
   *Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis*

3. **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
   *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature*

4. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
   *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit*

5. **Kosteas (2011)**  
   *Job satisfaction and promotions*

6. **Deci & Ryan**  
   Self-Determination Theory work on autonomy, competence, relatedness

7. **Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford (2014)**  
   *Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives jointly predict performance: A 40-year meta-analysis*

If you want, I can also turn this into a **decision memo** with a small table like “what the literature predicts for Anthropic vs Meta on satisfaction, burnout risk, growth, and regret risk.”

answer:
Yes — there is a fair amount of research adjacent to your question, though not much that studies *exactly* “interesting IC research job vs slightly higher-paid management role at another company.”

The strongest evidence-based summary is:

1. **Interesting / meaningful / autonomy-rich work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement.**
2. **Pay matters, but usually less than people expect once compensation is already high enough and perceived as fair.**
3. **People oriented toward intrinsic work values** (interest, growth, meaning, autonomy) **tend to report better long-run well-being than people oriented mainly toward extrinsic rewards** (money, status, image).
4. **Leadership opportunity is not “just extrinsic.”** If leading a team is something you deeply want, research would treat that as part of person-job fit and intrinsic motivation too.
5. **Direct evidence on “regret” is much thinner** than evidence on satisfaction/well-being. But the literature does *not* suggest that choosing the more interesting work systematically leads to more regret.

## What the literature says

### 1) Job characteristics that make work “interesting” are strongly tied to satisfaction

A classic line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analytic work such as **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**.

The recurring result is that features like:

- **autonomy**
- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- **opportunities for learning**

are consistently associated with:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **internal motivation**
- higher **engagement**
- lower **turnover intentions**
- often lower **burnout**

So if by “interesting” you mean “intellectually engaging, non-routine, challenging, meaningful, and with freedom,” then yes: that cluster of job features is one of the most reliable predictors of liking your work.

### 2) Meaningful work has a strong relationship with well-being

There is also a more recent “meaningful work” literature. Meta-analytic work such as **Allan et al. (2019)** finds that meaningful work is positively associated with:

- **job satisfaction**
- **work engagement**
- **life satisfaction**
- **sense of meaning in life**

and negatively associated with:

- **psychological distress**

The effect sizes here are generally **moderate to strong**, and in practice stronger than what is usually found for raw pay level.

That doesn’t mean “meaning beats everything.” It means that if a role feels substantially more meaningful or interesting to you, research says that is likely to matter a lot for well-being.

### 3) Pay matters, but the link between pay and job satisfaction is not that strong

A key paper here is **Judge et al. (2010)**, a meta-analysis on **pay and job satisfaction**. The short version is:

- the relationship is **positive**
- but it is **fairly modest**, not huge

This fits a broader literature on well-being and income:

- More money helps, especially when it reduces constraints or insecurity
- But people **adapt**
- And satisfaction depends a lot on **relative comparisons** and **fairness**, not only absolute dollars

So if the choice is between:
- one already very well-paid job, and
- another slightly-higher-paid job,

then the incremental happiness gain from salary alone is usually **smaller** than people intuitively expect.

In other words: research does **not** support “take the slightly higher salary; you’ll probably be happier.” Usually the evidence points the other way unless the money meaningfully changes your life conditions.

### 4) Intrinsic vs extrinsic values: intrinsic tends to win on well-being

A very relevant literature comes from **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci, Ryan; applied to work by **Gagné & Deci, 2005**, among others).

Core finding: well-being is better when work satisfies needs for:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

And work motivation is healthier when it is more **intrinsic** or **identified** (“this matters to me”) rather than purely **external** (“money, status, prestige”).

Related research on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations — for example **Kasser & Ryan** and later organizational work — generally finds that people who place relatively more emphasis on **interest, growth, contribution, mastery** tend to report better well-being than those focused more on **image, status, money**, even when the latter succeed.

This doesn’t mean money is bad. It means **money is a weaker and less durable source of satisfaction than interest/meaning/fit**.

### 5) Person-job fit matters a lot

Another very relevant meta-analysis is **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** on **person-environment fit**.

The robust result: when the role fits your interests, values, and preferred way of working, people show:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **commitment**
- lower **intentions to quit**

This matters because your choice is not really just:

- “interesting work” vs “more money”

It is also:

- “deep technical/research fit” vs “leadership/managerial fit”

If *leading a small team is something you always wanted*, then that is a **real fit variable**, not just a resume signal. Research would say that kind of fit can matter substantially.

So the evidence-based framing is probably:

> Which role gives the better overall fit across interest, autonomy, growth, meaning, and desired identity?

not merely:

> Which one is more interesting today?

### 6) What about regret?

This is the weakest part of the evidence base.

I’m not aware of strong direct studies showing, in general, that people who choose more interesting work over better-paid work later regret it more. The better-supported literature is on **well-being**, **satisfaction**, **fit**, and **turnover**, not explicit regret.

That said, two things are relevant:

#### a) Long-run satisfaction evidence favors intrinsic fit over extra pay
The literature above suggests people generally do better, over time, when work is more interesting/meaningful and aligned with intrinsic motivations.

#### b) General regret research often finds more long-run regret for inaction than action
Classic work by **Gilovich & Medvec** suggests that over the long run, people more often regret **not pursuing important opportunities** than making imperfect choices.

I would use that cautiously here, because it is not specific to jobs. But if “I always wanted to lead a small team” is a deep aspiration, then *not testing that* could plausibly become a regret source even if the work itself is less interesting.

So on regret, the research does **not** cleanly say “stay with the interesting work.” It says something more like:

- sacrificing meaningful/interesting work for extra pay alone is often not a recipe for greater happiness
- but ignoring a deeply held aspiration for leadership may also create regret if that aspiration is central to your identity

## The most research-backed practical takeaway

If you wanted the most evidence-based summary in one sentence, I’d put it this way:

> **For people who are already financially comfortable, day-to-day job quality — especially interesting work, autonomy, meaning, growth, and fit — tends to matter more for sustained well-being than incremental pay.**

But in your case, the leadership opportunity means the tradeoff is not just “interestingness vs salary.” It may be:

- **current intellectual interest**
vs
- **leadership fit / growth / autonomy / identity**

And research would say both sides of *that* tradeoff matter.

## If you want a few concrete papers to look up

A good short reading list would be:

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976, 1980)** — Job Characteristics Model  
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design  
- **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction  
- **Gagné & Deci (2005)** — Self-Determination Theory and work motivation  
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — person-environment fit meta-analysis  
- **Allan et al. (2019)** — meta-analysis on meaningful work outcomes  
- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)** — long-term regret of inaction vs action

If you want, I can also turn this into a **one-page evidence-based decision matrix** using the variables that the literature says matter most: interest, autonomy, meaning, fit, leadership aspiration, stress, and compensation.

Yes — there is a fairly strong research literature on this, and the short version is:

**On average, “interesting/meaningful/autonomous” work is a much stronger predictor of long-run job satisfaction than pay differences, especially once compensation is already high enough that basic financial stress is not the main issue.**  
But your case has a twist: **leading a team is also an intrinsic factor**, not just an extrinsic one. So the research doesn’t reduce your choice to “interesting work vs money”; it’s more like **research-interest vs leadership-interest**, with pay as a secondary factor.

## What the literature tends to find

### 1) Interesting / meaningful / autonomous work strongly predicts job satisfaction
A few literatures all point the same way:

- **Job Characteristics Model**: jobs with more **skill variety, autonomy, task significance, feedback**, etc. produce more internal motivation and higher satisfaction.
  - Classic source: **Hackman & Oldham**
  - Large meta-analytic update: **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
    Their results show that job design features like autonomy and task characteristics have **substantial** associations with satisfaction and motivation.

- **Self-Determination Theory at work**: people do better when work satisfies needs for **autonomy, competence, and relatedness**.
  - Key reviews: **Gagné & Deci**; **Van den Broeck et al.**
  - Main result: when people feel they are doing work they genuinely endorse and are growing in, they report more well-being, engagement, persistence, and less burnout.

- **Meaningful work** literature:
  - Recent meta-analyses (e.g. **Allan and colleagues**) find that meaningful work is linked to:
    - higher **job satisfaction**
    - higher **engagement**
    - higher **life satisfaction**
    - lower **turnover intentions** and **burnout**

So if by “interesting” you mean intellectually engaging, important, growth-producing, and self-concordant, research is very favorable to that.

## 2) Pay matters, but usually less than people expect for job satisfaction
The cleanest result here is:

- **Judge et al. (2010)** meta-analysis on **pay level and job satisfaction** found the relationship is **positive but modest** — commonly summarized as roughly **small** in size.

In plain English:
- Higher pay does make people somewhat more satisfied.
- But the effect is usually **much smaller** than the effect of job quality variables like autonomy, fit, meaning, and interesting work.
- Pay matters more when:
  - current pay feels **unfair**
  - someone is under real **financial pressure**
  - the pay gap changes important life options

For highly paid workers already above the “stress-reduction” zone, marginal dollars tend to buy **less additional well-being** than people predict.

That is consistent with the broader subjective well-being literature:
- more income generally improves well-being,
- but there are **diminishing returns**,
- and people adapt pretty quickly to higher pay.

## 3) Person-job fit is a very big deal
Another strong result:

- **Kristof-Brown et al. (2005)** meta-analysis on **person-environment fit** found that **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** are strongly related to:
  - job satisfaction
  - organizational commitment
  - lower intent to quit

This matters for your decision because the question is not only “Which role is objectively better?” but “Which role better fits what I most value?”

If you care deeply about:
- hard technical/research problems,
- learning,
- intellectual excitement,

then leaving an interesting research role for a less interesting management role often predicts lower satisfaction.

If you care deeply about:
- building and leading,
- having leverage through others,
- shaping a team,
- being responsible for outcomes,

then a leadership role can be intrinsically rewarding even if the subject matter is less fascinating.

## 4) Leadership itself can increase satisfaction — but the evidence is more mixed
The research here is less clean than for “meaningful work” or “pay.”

In general, leadership roles often come with:
- more **autonomy/control**
- more **status**
- more **impact**

which can increase satisfaction.

But they also often come with:
- more meetings and coordination
- more conflict management
- less direct craft/technical work
- more role overload

So the best-supported conclusion is not “management makes people happier” or “management makes people less happy.” It’s:

> **Management helps if it fits what you actually want to spend your days doing.**

That’s very consistent with person-job fit and self-determination research.

## 5) On regret specifically: the evidence is thinner, but there are some relevant findings
There is **not** a strong direct literature saying “people who chose the more interesting job later regretted it less than people who chose the higher-paying job.”

What we do have is adjacent regret research:

- **Gilovich & Medvec** and later regret work suggest that, over the long run, people often regret **inactions** more than actions — especially failures to pursue the **ideal self** or valued life paths.
- Self-concordance research suggests people feel better when their goals are aligned with their real interests/values rather than external rewards/status.

Translated carefully:
- If **leading a team** is a genuine long-held aspiration, declining it may be more regret-inducing than the salary question.
- If **doing frontier, intellectually absorbing work** is central to your identity, leaving that behind may be the bigger regret.

So the regret literature does **not** say “take the interesting job” or “take the leadership role.” It says regret tends to come more from failing to pursue what feels like a core self-defining path than from leaving some money on the table.

## Relative importance: what seems strongest in the evidence?
If I had to compress the literature into a ranking for long-run job satisfaction:

1. **Meaning / interest / autonomy / fit**
2. **Relationships / manager / culture**
3. **Fairness and adequacy of pay**
4. **Incremental pay above an already high level**

That is an oversimplification, but directionally it’s close to the evidence.

## What this implies for your specific choice
The research-based read is roughly:

- If the Meta offer is mainly:
  - a bit more money
  - more status
  - but substantially less interesting work

then **average predicted job satisfaction probably favors staying** in the more interesting role.

- But if the Meta offer is:
  - a real chance to do something you’ve deeply wanted for a long time — lead a team, build people, shape direction —
  - and that aspiration is not just prestige but genuinely motivating,

then the trade-off is no longer “interesting vs money”; it’s **one form of intrinsic reward vs another**. In that case, the literature would tell you to focus on **which role better matches your central motives**, not on salary.

## The most relevant empirical takeaway
Probably the single most decision-useful finding is:

> **Intrinsic job quality variables (interesting work, autonomy, meaning, fit) usually have stronger and more durable links to happiness at work than pay level does.**

The main exception is when the pay difference materially changes your life or when the new role satisfies a different intrinsic motive you care about just as much — in your case, leadership.

## If you want papers/results to actually read
A good starter list would be:

- **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction  
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis on work design/job characteristics  
- **Kristof-Brown et al. (2005)** — meta-analysis on person-environment fit  
- **Gagné & Deci (Self-Determination Theory at work)**  
- **Van den Broeck et al.** — need satisfaction at work / SDT reviews  
- **Allan et al.** — meta-analytic work on meaningful work outcomes  
- **Gilovich & Medvec** — long-run regret / action vs inaction

If you want, I can give you a **more formal mini-literature review** with:
1. paper names,  
2. 1–2 sentence findings,  
3. which ones are strongest methodologically, and  
4. a “what this means for your exact dilemma” section.

There must be some research on how important is doing interesting work comparing to other factors. Do people who stay in a more interesting job tend to be happier? Or do they regret that? Can you recall any results on that? I'm mostly interested in research results, not personal opinions.
