answer:
Yes—there is quite a lot of research relevant to this, though not usually framed exactly as “interesting job vs higher-paying job.” In the literature, the closest constructs are:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **meaningful work**
- **job characteristics** such as autonomy, skill variety, task significance, feedback
- **person-job fit**
- **intrinsic vs extrinsic work values**

### Short version

The best-supported general finding is:

> **People tend to be more satisfied and psychologically healthier in work that is intrinsically rewarding, meaningful, and well-matched to their values, and this usually matters more than salary differences once compensation is already “good enough.”**

That does **not** mean money is irrelevant. It means that:
- salary has a **real but modest** relationship with job satisfaction,
- while **interesting/meaningful/autonomous work** has a **stronger and more consistent** relationship.

Direct research on **later regret** from choosing interesting work over higher pay is much thinner. But the adjacent literature suggests that **mismatch in day-to-day work content** is a major source of dissatisfaction, and people often **overweight salary/title** and **underweight everyday experience** when forecasting future happiness.

---

## 1) Work design matters a lot

A classic line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses.

The central result is that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they involve:
- **skill variety**
- **task identity**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

These are close to what many people mean by “interesting work.”

### What the evidence says
Meta-analytic work, especially:

- **Fried & Ferris (1987)** — meta-analysis of the Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — very influential meta-analysis of work design

finds that these job features are **reliably associated** with:
- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **internal work motivation**
- better **engagement**
- lower **turnover intentions**
- better **well-being**

Among these, **autonomy** is especially robust.

### Implication
If the Anthropic job is substantially more interesting in the sense of:
- deeper intellectual engagement,
- more autonomy,
- more sense of meaningful contribution,

the literature would predict **higher day-to-day satisfaction** there, all else equal.

---

## 2) Meaningful work and intrinsic motivation are strongly linked to well-being

A separate but related literature looks at **meaningful work**.

The general result is:

> Feeling that your work is meaningful is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, commitment, and overall well-being.

Relevant reviews/meta-analyses include work by:
- **Rosso, Dekas, & Wrzesniewski (2010)** — major review of meaningful work
- more recent meta-analytic work in vocational/organizational psychology on meaningful work and well-being

These studies generally find that meaningful work is positively associated with:
- **job satisfaction**
- **work engagement**
- **life satisfaction**
- lower **burnout**
- lower **turnover intention**

### Implication
If “interesting” for you also means “this work matters and I care about it,” that is not just a preference—it is one of the strongest known predictors of sustainable work satisfaction.

---

## 3) Intrinsic values tend to predict better well-being than extrinsic values

There is also a large literature from **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan and many others).

The basic finding is:

> Work and life choices that satisfy needs for **autonomy**, **competence**, and **relatedness** are associated with higher well-being than choices driven mainly by **money, status, or image**.

This does **not** mean high pay is bad. It means that when people orient mainly around extrinsic rewards, they tend to do worse psychologically than when they pursue intrinsically valued goals.

Relevant strands:
- **Deci & Ryan** on self-determination theory
- **Kasser & Ryan** on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations
- work-context studies showing that need satisfaction at work predicts engagement and well-being

### Implication
If “leading a team” is something you want because it expresses:
- growth,
- impact,
- mentoring,
- responsibility,

then it may count as **intrinsic**, not extrinsic.

If instead it is mostly attractive because of:
- title,
- status,
- prestige,

the literature is less favorable.

That distinction matters a lot.

---

## 4) Pay matters, but less than most people think

There is a robust literature on pay and satisfaction.

A useful summary is the meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** on **pay and job satisfaction**

The broad result is:

> The relationship between pay level and job satisfaction is positive, but modest.

Not zero. Not huge.

This is one of the most replicated findings in this area: **more money helps, but it explains much less of job satisfaction than people often assume.**

Two extra nuances:

### a) Diminishing marginal returns
For subjective well-being, the effect of income tends to be **diminishing**, especially once basic comfort/security needs are well covered.

There is debate about exact shape/plateau:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** suggested a plateau for emotional well-being
- later work like **Killingsworth (2021)** argued against a sharp plateau

But even in the more pro-income findings, the marginal value of going from “very well paid” to “even more well paid” is usually smaller than people intuit.

### b) People value non-monetary job attributes a lot
Labor economists study **compensating differentials**: workers often accept lower pay for better non-monetary conditions.

Research like:
- **Mas & Pallais** on valuing work arrangements

shows workers will give up meaningful amounts of compensation for desirable job features like flexibility, control, and other amenities. “Interesting work” is harder to price, but the general point is strong: **non-pay job attributes have large value to people.**

### Implication
If the Meta offer is only “a bit higher salary,” the literature would generally predict that this alone is **unlikely** to offset a large drop in work interest/meaning—especially at already high compensation levels.

---

## 5) Person-job fit may be the single most important moderator

This is important for your case because you specifically mention **wanting to lead a small team**.

Research on **person-environment fit** and **person-job fit** finds that:

> Satisfaction is highest when the job supplies what the person personally values.

A core reference:
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis on fit at work

They find better fit is associated with:
- higher job satisfaction
- higher organizational commitment
- better performance
- lower intent to quit

### Why this matters for your decision
Your tradeoff is not just:

- interesting work vs money

It is also:

- **individual-contributor research work** vs **team leadership**

If leading people is genuinely important to you, then the Meta role may provide a different kind of intrinsic reward:
- mentoring
- social impact
- building
- influence
- broader responsibility

If, however, what you really love is the direct research itself, and leading a team mostly sounds like something you “should” do at some point, then the fit evidence would caution against sacrificing too much daily-interest content.

### Key research-based question
Not “Which option is objectively better?”
But:

> **Which role gives you more of the work characteristics you personally value most on ordinary Tuesdays?**

That is the kind of variable the literature says matters most.

---

## 6) What about regret?

This is the weakest part of the evidence base.

I do **not** know of a strong, famous literature showing directly that “people who chose the more interesting job later regret it less” in a clean causal way.

What we do have are nearby findings:

### a) People mispredict future happiness
Research on **affective forecasting** shows people are often bad at predicting what will make them happy.

Key names:
- **Wilson & Gilbert**
- **Kahneman**
- **Schkade & Kahneman** on the **focusing illusion**

The relevant result is:

> People tend to overweight salient, easy-to-compare features (salary, title, prestige) and underweight the texture of everyday experience.

That is highly relevant here.

### b) Daily experience matters a lot
Work satisfaction is strongly shaped by:
- what you actually do all day,
- whether it feels absorbing,
- whether you feel competent,
- whether you have autonomy,
- whether the work feels meaningful.

People often forecast based on the symbolic story of the job rather than the lived reality.

### c) Regret tends to follow poor fit more than lower compensation per se
The person-job fit literature suggests that regret/dissatisfaction is more likely when the role does not match:
- your values,
- your strengths,
- the type of work you enjoy doing.

So while I can’t point to a crisp “regret” meta-analysis for your exact question, the best inference from adjacent research is:

> People are more likely to regret choices that worsen their day-to-day fit, even if those choices improve salary or status.

---

## 7) If I translate the literature into your specific tradeoff

Not as advice, but as a research-based forecast:

### The literature would lean toward staying if:
- the work at Anthropic is **substantially more interesting**
- the salary gap is **modest**
- you already have **financial security**
- your current role has high **autonomy**, **competence**, and **meaning**
- the Meta role would move you away from what you most enjoy doing every day

### The literature would lean toward taking Meta if:
- leading a team is a **deeply held intrinsic goal**, not just a status marker
- the team lead role would still give you high **autonomy**, **challenge**, and **meaning**
- the new role fits your long-term identity better
- the salary increase materially changes something real for you, not just abstract “more”

---

## 8) The strongest empirical bottom line

If you want the simplest evidence-based summary:

1. **Interesting/meaningful/autonomous work predicts job satisfaction very strongly.**
2. **Pay predicts job satisfaction, but usually only modestly.**
3. **Fit matters enormously**—especially whether leadership is something you truly value in itself.
4. **Direct “regret” evidence is sparse**, but forecasting research suggests people often overvalue salary/title and undervalue daily task enjoyment.

So the literature does **not** say “always choose interesting work.”
It says something closer to:

> **At your likely compensation level, the enduring determinants of satisfaction are much more likely to be the daily character of the work and your fit with the role than the marginal increase in salary.**

---

## Representative references

A non-exhaustive list of good starting points:

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976/1980)** — Job Characteristics Model  
- **Fried, Y., & Ferris, G. R. (1987)** — Meta-analysis of the Job Characteristics Model  
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007)** — Meta-analysis of work design and outcomes  
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005)** — Meta-analysis on person-environment fit  
- **Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M.** — Self-Determination Theory  
- **Kasser, T., & Ryan, R. M.** — Intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations and well-being  
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010)** — Pay and job satisfaction meta-analysis  
- **Rosso, B. D., Dekas, K. H., & Wrzesniewski, A. (2010)** — Review of meaningful work  
- **Schkade, D. A., & Kahneman, D. (1998)** — Focusing illusion  
- **Wilson, T. D., & Gilbert, D. T.** — Affective forecasting  
- **Mas, A., & Pallais, A.** — Valuation of non-monetary job amenities

If you want, I can also do one of two follow-ups:

1. **give you a more formal literature review** with a few actual effect sizes and stronger caveats, or  
2. **turn this into a decision framework strictly grounded in the literature** (e.g. autonomy/meaning/fit/pay/leadership aspiration) without adding personal opinion.
